This carries huge implications for modern fiber arts and everyday mishaps, as certain types of dense embroidery, cross-stitching, or even tighter forms of basketry and wire-weaving share the exact same structural mechanics as loom-work and must be paused on Shabbat. The deep spiritual essence of Oreg teaches us that while the creation of cloth is one of humanity's most fundamental achievements in mastering nature for clothing and shelter, Shabbat is a day where we completely step away from fabrication. We wear our beautiful clothes and admire the woven textures around us, but we entirely relinquish the power to interlace even a single thread, honoring a day where the fabric of the universe is already considered complete. Learn more!

### cloud132

*ID: `cloud132`*

Learn about the Melacha of Potze'a—the deliberate act of separating, unraveling, and undoing woven threads—and the fascinating reasons why picking at a loose strand can be a violation of Shabbat rest! While most people understand that creating a piece of cloth is an act of labor, the Torah teaches that strategically undoing that same fabric is equally creative. This category of work traces directly back to the construction of the Mishkan, where weavers would occasionally make mistakes in the complex, dual-faced tapestry patterns of the sanctuary curtains, or find damaged sections that required them to carefully slice, unravel, and separate the interlaced warp and weft threads so the material could be salvaged and re-woven properly. The core definition of Potze'a is this specific, constructive undoing of a woven or braided structure, where the goal is to return the fabric to its individual, usable strands or to clear space for a new pattern. On Shabbat, this means that systematically pulling apart a knitted blanket, unraveling the edge of a frayed piece of cloth to harvest the yarn, or separating the woven elements of a basket is strictly forbidden. What makes Potze'a so surprising in daily life is how it applies to everyday clothing mishaps, like that sudden urge to tug on a loose thread hanging from the hem of a sweater or jacket. If pulling that thread unravels a row of stitches or separates the interlaced fibers of the garment, it directly mirrors the ancient dismantling process used by the Mishkan's craftsmen. The rule teaches us that on Shabbat, we refrain from both building up and breaking down the material world, inviting us to leave our garments exactly as they are and to appreciate a day where nothing needs to be corrected, unraveled, or reformed. Learn more!

### cloud133

*ID: `cloud133`*

Learn about the melacha of Koshair—the precise art of tying permanent knots—and the fascinating halakhic boundaries that separate a casual double-knot from a forbidden act of creation! This category of labor traces directly back to the construction of the Mishkan, where camel drivers and craftsmen tied thick, permanent knots to secure the massive fabric tapestries to the wooden beams, and fishermen tied and repaired the nets used to catch the snails that provided the rare blue chilazon dye for the techelet color. The core definition of Koshair is the creation of a tight, durable, and professional-grade knot that is intended to remain intact long-term. On Shabbat, the law divides knots into three main categories based on two major factors: the professional skill required to tie it, and the duration the knot is meant to last. A knot that is both professionally crafted and meant to be permanent—like a specialized sailor's knot or an artisan's knot on a leather strap—is strictly forbidden by the Torah itself. On the flip side, a completely temporary, amateur knot that is meant to be undone within twenty-four hours—like tying a plastic garbage bag or securing a bag of bread with a simple single-knot—is totally permitted because it lacks the permanence required to be considered a true act of construction. The gray area lies in semi-permanent knots meant to last for a week or a few days, which Rabbinic law prohibits to ensure people do not accidentally cross into tying permanent ones. This creates unique scenarios for everyday life, especially when it comes to tying shoes. While a standard bow on a sneaker is perfectly fine because you untie it at the end of the day, tightening it with a tight, permanent double-knot that stays tied for weeks would be a serious issue. It teaches us that on Shabbat, we refrain from binding the physical world together in a lasting way. Learn more!

### cloud134

*ID: `cloud134`*

Learn about the Melacha of Matir—the deliberate act of untying a knot—and the fascinating halakhic symmetry that determines when opening a bundle becomes a forbidden labor on Shabbat! As the direct counterpart to the Melacha of Koshair, untying is rooted in the construction of the Mishkan, where craftsmen had to untie the permanent knots securing the heavy tapestries and ropes whenever the camp packed up to travel through the desert. On Shabbat, Matir prohibits the dismantling of any knot that falls under the category of a permanent or long-term bond. The rule creates a mirror image of the tying laws: any knot that is a Torah-level violation to tie is a Torah-level violation to untie, and any knot that is Rabbinically forbidden to tie is likewise forbidden to untie. This means that if you encounter a tight, professional knot that was meant to stay tied indefinitely—such as a heavily bound cargo cord or a specialized knot on a leather strap—you cannot untie it on Shabbat, even if it was originally tied years ago during the week. This law has direct practical effects on everyday activities, from opening a stuck knot on a package of food to handling tangled shoelaces. If something tightens accidentally into a knot (on Shabbat or even before), we are generally lenient to untie it, because it was never intended to be a knot. Tying something not meant to last more than 24 hours like a simple single (not double) shoe lace is also permitted. Learn more!

### cloud135

*ID: `cloud135`*

Learn about the Melacha of Tofair—the precise art of sewing—and how a single stitch can become a profound lesson in Shabbat rest! This creative labor traces back to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled artisans sewed together the vast sheets of woven goat hair and sheep wool to form the massive protective coverings for the desert sanctuary, and stitched the intricate fabrics of the priestly garments. The core definition of Tofair is the joining together of two separate pieces of flexible material—like cloth, leather, or paper—into a unified, single entity using a third connecting element like thread, string, or glue. On Shabbat, this means that passing a needle and thread through a garment to mend a tear, or even pulling a broken seam back together, is strictly forbidden. What makes Tofair so fascinating in modern life is that the prohibition is not limited to traditional needle and thread; it applies to any action that creates a lasting, continuous bond between separate objects. This includes using liquid glue to paste pages together, applying heavy duct tape to bind two surfaces, or even using a stapler to lock papers into a permanent file. When a sudden fashion emergency strikes on Saturday—like a popped button, a ripped hem, or a split seam on your Shabbat clothes—the solution requires a careful understanding of these boundaries. Because the law targets permanent, creative connections, you cannot use tape or glue, but you can use a temporary safety pin to hold the fabric together, provided it is pinned in a casual, temporary way that does not mimic actual tailoring. It transforms a minor weekend mishap into a moment of mindful awareness, showing that on Shabbat, we let our wardrobe and our world remain exactly as they are, trusting that a temporary patch and a sense of humor are all we need to honor the day. Learn more!

### cloud136

*ID: `cloud136`*

Learn about the Melacha of Koreah—the deliberate act of tearing materials—and why breaking something down can be a profound violation of Shabbat rest! While tearing seems purely destructive, the Torah teaches that when a tear serves a constructive, productive purpose, it is an act of master craftsmanship. This labor traces directly back to the construction of the Mishkan, where workers would carefully rip apart sections of the heavy curtains if a mistake was made in the complex weaving patterns, or tear fabrics to reshape them into the exact proportions needed for the sanctuary walls. The core definition of Koraya is the tearing or ripping apart of any unified object—whether cloth, leather, paper, or plastic—with the ultimate intent of improving it, preparing it for a future creative act, or achieving a specific functional benefit. On Shabbat, this means that tearing a piece of fabric to create a rag, ripping open a sealed envelope along its glued flap to read a letter, or cutting aluminum foil to a specific size are all problematic. This law leads into one of the most famous halakhic discussions in modern Jewish life: the restriction on tearing toilet paper from a roll on Shabbat. Because ripping the paper anywhere at all splits a continuous roll to create a separate, usable sheet for hygiene, the act of tearing itself is viewed by authorities as a highly constructive and functional creation, which is why observant homes use pre-cut tissues or prepare their paper before the holy day begins. When opening packaged food on Saturday, you are allowed to tear a bag open destructively to get to the food inside, because ruined packaging is not considered a constructive creation. But most rabbis say a bag shouldn't be opened in the regular manner at the place where it is glued together, if glue was used. Some are lenient about this though. The deep essence of Koreah reminds us that Shabbat is not a day to break things down so we can reform them to our liking; instead, it invites us to step away from the cycle of dismantling the world to fulfill our needs.

### cloud137

*ID: `cloud137`*

Learn about the Melacha of Tzad—the deliberate act of trapping living creatures—and the fascinating halakhic boundaries that extend a total Shabbat truce to even the smallest insects in your home! This category of creative labor traces directly back to the construction of the Mishkan, where hunters and workers had to capture wild animals to obtain their hides for the sanctuary coverings, as well as trap the rare chilazon snails used to harvest the sacred blue dye for the tapestries and priestly garments. The core definition of Tzad is the act of restricting the freedom of any wild, un-domesticated living creature—whether a mammal, a bird, a fish, or a bug—by placing it into an enclosed space from which it can no longer easily escape. On Shabbat, this means that casting a net over a fish, locking an animal in a cage, or chasing down a wild creature to confine it is strictly prohibited. What makes Tzad so surprising in everyday life is how it applies to tiny household pests like flies, mosquitoes, or bees. If a buzzing fly is bothering you and you clap a plastic cup over it or slam a box shut on it, trapping it against a table, you have directly performed the Melacha of Tzad by stripping that insect of its freedom of movement. The rules are so precise that even closing a window or a door can become an issue if a wild bird or a stray animal accidentally wandered into your room, as shutting the exit means you have now effectively trapped it inside. However, an essential exception to this rule exists in cases of real health risks or medical emergencies: if a venomous creature or a stinging insect like a bee poses a direct threat to someone, particularly if an individual has a life-threatening allergy, like to bee stings, the foundational Torah principle of Pikuach Nefesh—preserving human life—overrides the laws of Shabbat entirely, meaning you are fully permitted to trap or even kill the insect to eliminate the danger. Barring those urgent situations, observant homes approach bugs on Shabbat with a strategy of gentle avoidance rather than entrapment, often leaving windows open carefully or waiting for an insect to leave on its own. While you are fully permitted to close the door on a thoroughly domesticated pet like a dog or cat that naturally comes when called and stays inside your home, wilder creatures are left entirely untethered, reminding us that on Shabbat, we step back from trying to dominate, capture, or control the animal kingdom. Learn more!

### cloud138

*ID: `cloud138`*

Learn about the Melacha of Shochait—the act of slaughtering or taking the life of a living creature—and how this serious prohibition connects directly to everyday actions like flossing your teeth on Shabbat! This creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where animals were slaughtered for the sanctuary sacrifices and to process their skins for the heavy coverings that shielded the Tabernacle structure. The core definition of Shochait is the termination of life, and on Shabbat, it applies strictly across the entire animal kingdom, meaning that hunting, slaughtering, or killing any mammal, bird, fish, or insect is completely forbidden (unless it's danger to human life). Because the concept of Shochait centers on the extraction of life-sustaining fluid from a body, the Talmud expands this definition to include Chavurah—the act of causing a wound that draws blood from a living being, which is viewed as a partial form of taking life.  If a person has sensitive gums and knows with absolute certainty that flossing will cause their teeth to bleed, sliding that floss between their teeth becomes a direct violation of the prohibition against causing a wound on Shabbat. Because of this, authorities rule that if flossing is guaranteed to draw blood, it must be avoided on the day of rest. It shows that the spirit of Shochait is not just about avoiding major hunts or slaughterhouses; it is about cultivating a deep reverence for life and vitality in all its forms. By leaving the animal kingdom unbothered and ensuring our own bodies remain unwounded, we honor a day dedicated entirely to peace, vitality, and preservation, relying completely on the food and preparations we completed during the week.

### cloud139

*ID: `cloud139`*

Learn about the Melacha of Mafshit—the deliberate act of skinning and flaying—and how the painstaking separation of a hide from flesh reveals the Torah's total freeze on industrial processing on Shabbat! This advanced category of creative labor is part of the long, sequential chain of leather production that was vital to the construction of the Mishkan, where workers had to carefully strip the skins off slaughtered rams, tahash animals, and goats to gather the raw hides required to manufacture the weather-proof protective curtains for the desert sanctuary. The core definition of Mafshit is the physical separation of the skin or hide of any animal from its underlying meat or carcass in a productive, useful manner. On Shabbat, this means that any act of flaying, peeling away a hide to harvest leather, or stripping the skin off a carcass for processing is strictly forbidden. While a person is highly unlikely to find themselves skinning a deer on a Saturday, the intricate details of Mafshit carry fascinating applications when it comes to preparing and serving food for Shabbat meals. For instance, authorities explicitly distinguish between stripping the hide off an animal for industrial use and simply peeling food to eat it. While removing the skin from a cooked piece of chicken or a slice of meat during a meal is entirely permitted because it is a direct act of eating rather than leather manufacturing, a boundary shows up in a highly practical way when handling our everyday wardrobe on Saturday. Because animal hide naturally consists of multiple bonded layers, ancient parchment makers would carefully split and peel these layers apart to separate the quality outer layer from the inner layer. Halacha rules that this mechanical act of splitting leather applies even to processed, modern goods. If you have a heavy-duty leather belt, a reinforced strap, or the laminated sole of a leather shoe that begins to fray or split down the middle into two distinct layers, deliberately pulling or peeling those bonded layers further apart on Shabbat is off-limits due to this not so well known melacha! Learn more!

### cloud140

*ID: `cloud140`*

Learn about the Melacha of Me'abeid—the ancient craft of transforming raw hide into durable leather—and discover how this melacha reshapes even the way we salt our food on Shabbat! This sophisticated category of labor traces its roots directly to the construction of the Mishkan, where workers treated raw animal skins with specialized oils, salts, and chemical baths to soften, dye, and preserve them into the durable, weather-resistant leather needed for the Tabernacle outer coverings. The core definition of Me'abeid is the physical or chemical processing of any raw, organic material to preserve it, toughen it, or prevent it from decaying. On Shabbat, this means that any act of curing hides, treating leather with conditioning creams, or working a stiff material to make it supple is strictly forbidden. What makes Me'abeid so relevant to modern life is its direct derivative, a sub-category called Molei'ach (Salting). Because salting is the very first step tanners use to preserve a raw hide, the Talmud explains that salting a material to chemically alter its texture falls under this exact same category of labor. This boundary applies directly to how we handle food in the kitchen, specifically when it comes to salting raw vegetables like cucumbers, radishes, or onions. Halacha rules that heavily salting foods that naturally soften or lose water when exposed to salt closely mirrors the ancient chemical process of curing a hide. To avoid this issue on Saturday, observant individuals refrain from pouring a large amount of salt over a bowl of raw vegetables to let them marinate or pickle, opting instead to add salt right before serving, or mixing the vegetables with oil or vinegar first to coat them and dilute the curing effect of the salt. The spiritual essence of Me'abeid teaches us that while humanity has the brilliant capability to chemically alter and master the raw materials of nature to create long-lasting goods like a leather wallet or preserved food, Shabbat is a day where we completely halt this transformation. Learn more!

### cloud141

*ID: `cloud141`*

Learn about the Melacha of Memachek—the delicate art of smoothing and scraping surfaces—and discover how this finishing-touch boundary directly alters the way we use soap, toothpaste, and lotions on Shabbat! This meticulous category of creative labor traces its origins directly to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled craftsmen scraped raw animal hides with precision tools to remove hairs, eliminate rough bumps, and sand down imperfections until the leather was perfectly uniform and supple for the Tabernacle coverings. The core definition of Memachek is the mechanical smoothing, sanding, or scraping of any stiff, solid material—such as wood, stone, metal, or leather—to level out its surface and make it completely even. On Shabbat, this means that sanding down a piece of furniture, scraping a rough leather strap to smooth it out, or polishing a scratch out of a wooden surface to improve its texture is strictly forbidden. While a person is unlikely to be sanding wood or processing raw hides on a Saturday, Memachek has a massive, highly practical counterpart in daily life called Memarei'ach, which is the act of smoothing down malleable, pasty substances. This sub-category directly shapes personal hygiene and medical care on the day of rest, specifically when it comes to using thick creams, ointments, bar soaps, or even toothpaste. Halacha rules that spreading a thick layer of ointment over a wound, rubbing a bar of solid soap until it lathers smoothly, or squeezing a thick ribbon of toothpaste across a brush closely mirrors the ancient act of leveling a surface to make it flush. To avoid this boundary on Shabbat, observant individuals alter their routine by using liquid soap instead of bar soap, applying liquid oils or thin lotions that instantly absorb into the skin rather than thick creams, and gently dabbing a necessary medical ointment onto an injury without spreading or smoothing it flat. Learn more!

### cloud142

*ID: `cloud142`*

Learn about the Melacha of Mesarteit—the precise act of scoring guidelines on a surface—and discover how this preparatory boundary pauses the physical setup for writing or cutting on Shabbat! This specialized category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where meticulous scribes and craftsmen used sharp styling tools to score faint, perfectly straight lines onto animal hides and parchment sheets before ink ever touched the surface, ensuring that the sacred texts were written with flawless symmetry and that heavy fabrics were cut to exact specifications. The core definition of Mesarteit is the intentional marking, etching, or scratching of lines on a flexible or solid material to serve as a visual guide for a future productive action, such as writing text or cutting a precise boundary. On Shabbat, this means that using a stylus to press a faint line into a blank page, scratching a guide mark onto a piece of leather, or using a ruler to score a crease into heavy paper to help you fold it neatly is strictly forbidden. While a person is highly unlikely to be preparing raw parchment on a Saturday, the laws of Mesarteit carry fascinating and highly practical applications when it comes to dealing with modern materials and food presentation. Halacha rules that drawing lines or scoring a material to dictate a specific boundary is restricted even when no ink is involved. For instance, using a sharp knife to score a deep, precise line across the top of a cake or a batch of brownies to guide where the future cuts will go, or pressing a decorative border into a piece of dough to mark out a cutting pattern, directly mirrors the ancient act of ruling parchment. To navigate this boundary smoothly on Shabbat: if you are cutting the cake directly and simply slice right through it, any line your knife makes while moving through the cake is permitted. The issue is only drawing a preliminary guide line before making the actual cut. This rule even influences the way the Challah is marked before the Hamotzi blessing. While it is customary for many to make a slight mark with the knife so the bread can be sliced quickly after the blessing, halacha requires making only a single, tiny indentation at the starting point rather than etching a long, continuous guideline across the loaf, specifically to avoid the issue of ruling a cutting guide. Fascinating! Learn more!

### cloud143

*ID: `cloud143`*

Learn about the Melacha of Mechateich—the deliberate act of cutting a material to a specific size or shape—and discover how this precise manufacturing boundary directly shapes how we open packages, prepare food, and handle household items on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled artisans carefully measured and cut heavy woven tapestries, leather skins, and wooden beams to exact lengths and dimensions to ensure every single piece fit perfectly into the Tabernacle structure. The core definition of Mechateich is the intentional cutting, tearing, or trimming of any functional object to a specific, measured size, geometric shape, or precise dimension for a productive purpose. On Shabbat, this means that trimming a piece of plastic wrap to a specific length, cutting a loose thread to even out a fabric border, or sawing a wooden board to size for a DIY shelf is strictly forbidden. While a person is unlikely to be cutting building materials on Saturday, Mechateich introduces highly practical guidelines when it comes to routine household chores. A classic example is tearing toilet paper along its perforated lines; because you are deliberately tearing the paper at a measured, pre-cut boundary to get a specific size sheet, it is a direct violation of Mechateich (even NOT cutting on the lines is a problem of the melacha of Koreah). Similarly, snapping apart a multi-pack of yogurt cartons that are attached to each other at a perforated plastic seam falls under this restriction, as it cleanly divides the material into precisely sized individual units. Learn more!

### cloud144

*ID: `cloud144`*

Learn about the Melacha of Koteiv—the foundational prohibition against writing—and discover how this creative boundary shapes how we act on Shabbat! This central category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled builders wrote distinct letters or symbols on the wooden wall beams to mark their matching pairs, ensuring the massive structure could be disassembled and reassembled with perfect accuracy. The core definition of Koteiv is the formation of any meaningful symbol, character, or letter that conveys information upon a surface. To violate the Torah prohibition, a person must write at least two distinct letters or symbols using a permanent medium on a lasting surface. However, the Sages extended this boundary to protect the day of rest, rabbinically forbidding temporary writing and shaping any letters or images that don't pre-exist. Similarly, placing a torn page of a book back together so the letters line up can violate Koteiv. Even books with words or stamped designs printed directly onto the outer edges of the pages present a unique question, as opening and closing the book repeatedly splits and reunites the text, leading many authorities to prefer using standard books without edge-printing on the holy day. This boundary also guides how we play games like Scrabble; while simply placing loose, flat letter tiles side-by-side on a table is generally permitted, playing with travel-style games where the letters physically click, snap, or lock into a rigid frame or grid is questioned by authorities, as firmly securing letters into a backing is similar to writing. As for keeping score, rabbis who are concerned players may be accustomed to writing down their scores, suggest using a (not holy) book to track scores by flipping the pages to match their score!

### cloud145

*ID: `cloud145`*

Learn about the Melacha of Mocheik—the deliberate act of erasing or wiping away meaningful marks— on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled scribes would immediately erase or scrape off any mistaken lettering on the wooden wall beams or parchment skins to perfectly prepare the surface to be written upon correctly. The core definition of Mocheik is the intentional wiping, scraping, dissolving, or obliterating of any meaningful symbol, letter, or graphic. Under Torah law, this applies when erasing is done for a constructive purpose—specifically to clean a surface so it can be written on again, like wiping a whiteboard or slate clean. However, the Sages extended this boundary to rabbinically forbid destructive erasing where no rewriting occurs. This boundary directly guides how we handle written or printed materials on the day of rest, meaning that scratching out text, erasing pencil marks, or deliberately destroying a permanent record is prohibited. For Ashkenazi communities following the Rema, this restriction leads to a preference to avoid tearing through printed letters or logos when opening food packages from the outset, though if done accidentally or after the fact (b'dieved), it's permitted. For Sephardic communities following Maran Rav Ovadia Yosef, tearing through packaging text to access food or medication is permitted from the outset, as the destructive act is done with zero intent to erase or rewrite text. Learn more!

### cloud146

*ID: `cloud146`*

Learn about the Melacha of Boneh—the deliberate act of building or assembling a structure—and discover how this manufacturing boundary directly shapes how we handle household items, furniture, and temporary shelters on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled artisans erected the massive wooden wall panels and fit the heavy foundation sockets together to create a unified, sturdy Tabernacle. The core definition of Boneh is the creation, improvement, or assembly of any structure, vessel, or functional space by combining parts together into a stable unit. On Shabbat, this means that heavy construction, installing permanent shelving, or repairing a loose door handle that fell out of its socket is strictly forbidden. While a person is unlikely to be pouring concrete on Saturday, Boneh introduces highly practical guidelines for routine household adjustments. A classic example is setting up a pop-up canopy or a large, multi-panel tent for shade; because you are assembling a covered shelter that shields a significant area from the sun or rain, it can constitute the creation of an Ohel (tent/canopy), which falls under the restrictions of building. Similarly, snapping together modular plastic furniture or attaching/screwing parts that form a rigid, tight unit are all problems. Learn more!

### cloud147

*ID: `cloud147`*

Learn about the Melacha of Soter—the deliberate act of dismantling or demolishing a structure—and discover how this creative boundary guides how we handle household items, temporary setups, and container openings on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where the Levites carefully disassembled the massive wooden panels and foundation sockets of the Tabernacle whenever the Cloud lifted, preparing the components to be transported and perfectly rebuilt at the next wilderness camp. The core definition of Soter is the intentional dismantling, breaking down, or undoing of any stable structure, vessel, or functional unit. Under Torah law, this applies when the deconstruction is done for a constructive purpose—specifically to rebuild, improve, or clear space for a new structure. However, the Sages extended this boundary to rabbinically restrict destructive dismantling as well, introducing highly practical guidelines for everyday household objects. For instance, halacha directly guides how we handle temporary shelters; dismantling a large outdoor shade canopy or taking down a multi-panel tent on Shabbat can violate Soter, as undoing a covered shelter reverses the act of building. Similarly, unscrewing a loose door handle completely or forcefully taking apart modular plastic furniture units that were tightly locked together falls under this restriction. Learn more!

### cloud148

*ID: `cloud148`*

Learn about the Melacha of Mechabeh—the deliberate act of extinguishing a fire—and discover how this creative boundary completely pauses our control over flames and heating elements on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled metalworkers and artisans would extinguish burning wood embers to produce pure charcoal for smelting metals or to control the heat of ovens used for baking offerings. The core definition of Mechabeh is the intentional putting out, dimming, or dampening of any glowing flame, spark, or fire. Under Torah law, this applies when extinguishing is done for a constructive purpose—specifically to create charcoal or save a wick to burn again later. However, the Sages extended this boundary to rabbinically forbid any destructive extinguishing as well, introducing highly practical rules for the modern home. On Shabbat, this means that blowing out a candle, pouring water on a burning wick, or turning off a gas stove flame is strictly forbidden. Even turning down a live burner or lowering a flame to simmer food is restricted, as dimming a fire is a direct form of partial extinguishing. While a person is unlikely to be managing open forge fires on Saturday, Mechabeh shapes how we handle lighting and cooking appliances. To navigate this smoothly on Shabbat, observant individuals light all candles before the day of rest begins and leave them to burn out naturally, set automated timers to control electric lights, and leave stove burners at a steady, preset temperature under a protective cover (blech) until the week begins again. Learn more!

### cloud149

*ID: `cloud149`*

Learn about the Melacha of Mav'ir—the deliberate act of kindling or fueling a fire—and discover how this creative boundary completely pauses our control over heat and combustion on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor is so central that the Torah singles it out explicitly, tracing its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled artisans kindled fires to melt metals for the Tabernacle's vessels and to bake offerings. The core definition of Mav'ir is the intentional creation, expansion, or fueling of any flame, spark, or intense heat source. Under Torah law, this applies to striking a match, adding wood to a bonfire, or stepping on the gas pedal of an internal combustion engine car. While a person is unlikely to be forging metal on Saturday, Mav'ir introduces highly practical rules for modern technology. Halacha rules that completing an electrical circuit to turn on an appliance or lightswitch, adjusting a dial to raise a gas burner, or plugging in an electronic device is restricted, as creating a live electrical current or heating an element mimics or violates the strict boundaries of kindling a fire. This boundary directly shapes how we navigate modern hotels, manage kitchen appliances, and use electricity. To navigate this smoothly on Shabbat, observant individuals set automated timers to control home lights before the day begins, set slow-cookers to a steady, preset temperature before Friday evening, and rely on pre-existing warmth and light to honor the day of rest. Learn more!

### cloud150

*ID: `cloud150`*

Learn about the Melacha of Makeh B'patish—the final hammer blow that completes a craft—and discover how this creative boundary pauses the finishing touches on all our physical objects on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled craftsmen would strike a final, decisive blow with a hammer on newly forged metal vessels to smooth out any rough imperfections and render the item fully functional. The core definition of Makeh B'patish is the intentional execution of any final action that takes an incomplete, flawed, or unusable object and transforms it into a finished, ready-to-use product. Under Torah law, this applies to any craft—whether it is snipping a loose, dangling thread off a freshly tailored shirt, inserting a screw to stabilize a table, bending a bent spoon back into position, or forcefully snapping a detached zipper pull back onto its track to make it work again. Similarly, fixing an item by removing a defect—such as removing a hazardous, protruding nail from a wall to make the surface safe and smooth—is viewed as a finishing act of repair. To navigate these boundaries smoothly on Shabbat, observant individuals leave all minor repairs, assembly projects, and finishing touches until after Havdalah, fully enjoying our physical environment exactly as it was prepared before the holy day! Learn more.

### cloud151

*ID: `cloud151`*

Learn about the Melacha of Hotza'ah—the deliberate act of transferring or carrying objects between different domains—and discover how this creative boundary completely transforms our public movement and community spaces on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor is uniquely highlighted throughout the Torah and traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan. When Moshe gathered the nation to build the sanctuary, he opened with a strict warning to keep the Sabbath, teaching them that constructing Hashem's home does not override the day of rest. Thus, when the people halted the public transport of their gold, silver, and fabrics from their personal tents into the communal camp, that exact pause established the legal blueprint for ceasing public carrying on the holy day. The core definition of Hotza'ah is the moving of an object from a private domain (reshut hayachid) to a public domain (reshut harabim), vice versa, or carrying an object a distance of four cubits (about six feet) within a public domain. Under Torah law, a private domain is an enclosed space measuring at least four by four handbreadths with walls at least ten handbreadths high, while a true public domain is an unroofed, wide-open thoroughfare used by the masses. However, because most modern streets do not meet the strict population requirements of a biblical public domain, they are rabbinically classified as a karmelit (a semi-public area), where carrying is still restricted to protect the biblical boundaries. This creates highly practical implications for stepping outside the front door, as carrying everyday necessities like house keys, tissues, water bottles, or pushing a stroller becomes prohibited. To navigate this smoothly without an eiruv—a symbolic halakhic enclosure that unifies a neighborhood into a single shared 'private' domain—observant individuals utilize highly creative solutions. For instance, people fashion their house keys into wearable jewelry like functional tie clips, or belt buckles, since wearing a permitted ornament or garment does not constitute forbidden carrying. To ensure seamless movement on the day of rest, communities invest in establishing and maintaining an eiruv, while individuals without an eiruv ensure all unwearable items are left at home or placed at their destination before sunset on Friday. Learn more!

### cloud152

*ID: `cloud152`*

Learn about Kiddush Yadayim V'Raglayim — the mandatory sanctification of a Kohen's hands and feet before performing any sacred service in the Beit HaMikdash! 🌊 This crucial ritual was performed by placing the right hand over the right foot and the left hand over the left foot, allowing fresh water to pour over them simultaneously from a spigot on the copper Laver (Kiyor) or from any other sanctified vessel, poured either by himself or a colleague, as long as the flow was powered by direct human force (Koach Gava). Performing this act was an absolute requirement, and if a Kohen performed any service without first sanctifying his hands and feet, his service was completely invalid and disqualified. The sanctification would also be disqualified by any physical barrier like dirt or bandages on the skin (Chatzitzah), or instantly voided if he was up all night performing the service and then dawn broke the following morning. He would also need to wash again if the Kohen stepped outside the Temple courtyard, contracted ritual impurity, or even used the bathroom!. This elaborate process reminds us that divine service sometimes requires an uncompromised level of holiness. Learn more!

### cloud153

*ID: `cloud153`*

Learn about the rabbinic boundary of Muktzeh—items that are set aside and restricted from being moved—and discover how this rule completely changes how we interact with everyday objects on Shabbat! Unlike the 39 Melachot, which trace back to the physical labor of building the Mishkan, Muktzeh is a protective fence established by the Sages during the era of the Second Temple. The core definition of Muktzeh is any object that a person purposefully sets aside or removes from their mind before sunset on Friday, making it completely forbidden to move, handle, or carry throughout the holy day. The Sages enacted this boundary for several critical reasons: to prevent people from accidentally performing creative labor, to ensure the day of rest feels physically distinct from the workweek, and to protect the sanctity of the home from mundane clutter. Because Muktzeh focuses on an item's purpose, it introduces highly practical categories for things found in any modern household. The most common category is Kli SheMelakhto L'Issur (a tool whose primary use is forbidden on Shabbat), such as a hammer, a pen, or a smartphone; you are generally allowed to move these items only if you desperately need the physical space they are occupying, or if you need to use the object itself for a permitted Shabbat activity (like using a hammer to crack open a tough coconut). Another major category is Muktzeh Machmat Gufo (items that are inherently useless on Shabbat), which includes raw stones, dirt, or money; because these objects serve absolutely no functional purpose on the day of rest, they cannot be touched or moved under any circumstances. Even a broken item or a scrap of material that lost its utility right before Friday night becomes restricted, as it was not designated for a Shabbat purpose before the day began. To navigate these boundaries smoothly on Shabbat, observant families store their wallets, work tools, and electronics out of sight before Friday evening, creating a serene, work-free environment where every handled object is dedicated entirely to the peace and joy of the day. Learn more!

### cloud154

*ID: `cloud154`*

Learn about the mitzvah of Hadlakat Nerot—lighting Shabbat candles—and a fascinating halakhic puzzle! While lighting candles creates a beautiful atmosphere, the Sages established them for shalom bayit (peace in the home) so family members wouldn't stumble in the dark. This introduces a major rule: we must actively benefit from the light. But what happens if you welcome Shabbat early, while the sun is still blasting outside? This exact question sparks a brilliant debate among the classic legal authorities over a famous Talmudic proverb: 'A lamp at noon—what use is it?' (Chullin 60b). The Magen Avraham (OC 263:11) rules that if you light early and leave, your blessing is wasted. His fix? You must sit and force a brief, subjective benefit from the flames right after lighting. However, the Shulchan Aruch HaRav (OC 263:11) and the Aruch HaShulchan (OC 263:13) completely disagree with that workaround. They argue that daytime candlelight is entirely useless because the sun is doing all the work. To them, the only way to fulfill the mitzvah is to use extra long-lasting candles so that the flames are still burning strong when you return home after dark, which is when physical illumination is actually needed, and you can benefit from them then. To satisfy all sides, modern authorities like Shemirat Shabbat Kehilchatah (43:17) introduce a brilliant workaround using electric timers- and no, you don't need to run through the house flipping every switch in your bathrooms and closets! You only need to turn off and on the electric lights in the main dining area where you are actually lighting. When you strike the match and make your blessing, simply have explicit intention (kavanah) that your words act as an umbrella covering both the wax/oil candles in front of you and all the electric lights throughout the house. As the Mishnah Berurah (263:41) rules, that single blessing in your main room exempts every other space, ensuring that when the timers click on later in the dark, you get immediate, indisputable benefit from an illumination directly bound to your original blessing! Learn this well, and you'll never look at the lights in your home on Shabbat the same way again!

### cloud155

*ID: `cloud155`*

Learn about the fascinating laws of Nedarim—the binding power of vows—and discover how a person's spoken words can physically alter legal reality, creating a mind-bending halakhic puzzle that requires a piece of genuine legal time-travel to solve! 📜 Under Torah law, human speech behaves like a reality-warping tool; if a person declares an item forbidden to themselves, they legally transform that ordinary object into a prohibited item, making its use a direct biblical violation. Because people often make rash promises in moments of anger, the Torah provided a legal escape hatch through Hatarat Nedarim (the nullification of vows) by a Torah sage who can dissolve the vow entirely. However, this setup leads to a hilarious legal deadlock codified by the Rambam in Mishneh Torah, Hilchot Nedarim 4:13. 🤯 Consider a man who gets into a heated dispute with a local community and storms off after swearing: 'I forbid myself from deriving any benefit from the people of this city!' When his temper cools and he desperately regrets his words, he finds himself caught in a massive spiritual trap: the only local sage capable of releasing his vow is a resident of that forbidden city. If he asks the local rabbi for help, the act of receiving rabbinic guidance means he is actively deriving a benefit from a resident—violating the exact vow he is trying to escape! 🏛️ The Rambam elegantly resolves this logical paradox by dividing the timeline into two distinct realities. As the Rambam states in Chapter 4, Halachah 13, initially, it is forbidden for the person to ask such a sage to release his vow, for in this way, he is benefiting from the people of the city. After the fact (Bedi'avad), however, the vow is released, because retroactively, it is as if the vow were never taken in the first place! ⏳ This mechanism works because rabbinic nullification reaches back in time to undo the premise of the promise, completely erasing the violation from history. Fascinating, isn't it? Learn more!


# Part 9 — Standalone Halachic Disclaimer (docs/HALACHIC_DISCLAIMER.md)

# Halachic disclaimer

Be a Tzaddik is an **educational checklist tool**. It is not a substitute for a rabbi, posek, or personal halachic guidance.

- Wait times for meat and dairy, prayer obligations, and holiday practices vary by community and personal situation.
- Always follow local customs and an Orthodox rabbi you trust before relying on this app for halachic decisions.
- Location-based times are approximate; use your community's published zmanim when precision matters.
- On **Shabbat and Yom Tov**, the app shows a rest screen and asks you not to use electronics, based on your saved location and timezone.

Havdalah inside Kiddush (Yaknehaz):
• When a biblical holiday begins Saturday night (including Rosh Hashana), that night's Kiddush incorporates havdalah for Shabbat. The order is: (1) wine blessing, (2) holiday Kiddush, (3) candle blessing, (4) havdalah blessing — with the Yom Tov text concluding bein kodesh l'kodesh (not bein kodesh l'chol), (5) Shehecheyanu for the festival.
• This is the YaKNeHaZ order (Yayin, Kiddush, Ner, Havdalah, Zeman) codified in Shulchan Arukh for Motzei Shabbat into Yom Tov.
• Spices are not used.
• Use the Machzor or siddur nusach for Rosh Hashana — do not rely on memory for the long havdalah text.

Before or at Maariv:
• You may say Baruch hamavdil bein kodesh l'kodesh to begin Yom Tov-permitted activities before Kiddush, or rely on Vatodi'enu in the Amidah of Maariv — follow your community.

$prepLead:
• Have round challah, honey, apples, and symbolic foods ready for the Yom Tov meals after Shabbat (minhag).
• Confirm shofar and Musaf times for the first day(s) of Rosh Hashana after Shabbat — shofar is not blown on Shabbat itself.
• Tashlich when the first day is Shabbat: Ashkenazim postpone to Sunday — a universal Rabbinic gezeirah against carrying in public, even where there is an eruv. Many Sephardic communities (following the Arizal and Yalkut Yosef) recite Tashlich on the first day when Shabbat is Rosh Hashana, where carrying a machzor is permitted. Tashlich is prayers at the water — not feeding fish (throwing breadcrumbs is forbidden on Shabbat and Yom Tov).

Candles: after Shabbat ends, light Yom Tov candles from a flame lit before Shabbat began (pre-existing flame).
- $opener

Havdalah when Shabbat leads into Yom Tov:
• Havdalah is recited when entering a day of lesser holiness. Shabbat is holier than Yom Tov, so when Shabbat leads into a festival, havdalah is included in that night's Kiddush — not as a full separate havdalah with spices before Kiddush.
• Order (mnemonic YaKNeHaZ per many Ashkenaz poskim): Yayin (borei pri hagafen) → Kiddush for Yom Tov → Ner (borei me'orei ha'eish — recite over the Yom Tov candles on the table; you may move a burning candle on Yom Tov if needed for the blessing or the meal, unlike Shabbat muktzeh rules) → Havdalah (holiday text ending bein kodesh l'kodesh, not bein kodesh l'chol) → Zeman (Shehecheyanu on the first festival night when applicable).
• Spices (besamim) are omitted for this transition.
• Before Kiddush, melacha permitted on Yom Tov but not on Shabbat: many say Baruch hamavdil bein kodesh l'kodesh, or rely on the Vatodi'enu insert in Maariv — follow your Machzor.

$prepWhen: recite Baruch hamavdil bein kodesh l'kodesh after nightfall, then light Yom Tov candles from a pre-existing flame; 48-hour candle or pilot light per your rav.
- $pct%
- $pesachBegins

$chametzNote

Seder (first night):
• $sederWhen
• Matzah, maror, four cups of wine, reading the Haggadah, afikoman.
• Seder plate setup: zeroa (shankbone), beitzah (egg), maror, chazeret (per custom), charoset, karpas, salt water.
• Reclining (hasebha): Recline to the left when drinking the four cups and eating matzah, korech, and afikoman — do not recline while eating maror or chazeret (they symbolize slavery).
• Kiddush, festive meal, Hallel, Nirtzah.
• Follow your Haggadah step by step for the exact order and details.

Tomorrow by day:
$pesachChametzKitniyotLine
• Only eat food prepared for Pesach in kosher-for-Passover utensils.

- **Super-Special Mitzvah**: Light an oil candle (such as a floating wick in a small cup of oil) for the elevation of the souls of the "Neshamot Hagalmudot" (הנשמות הגלמודות) -the forlorn departed souls in Heaven who need just a small merit to get into the Garden of Eden (Gan Eden). Rabbis say these poor souls are just missing a small merit to be let into the eternal pleasure of Gan Eden,  and if you light a candle in their merit, you could help them get in! Then these souls are extremely grateful, and once they're in they can pray for you for all your wishes!

- 100 Blessings every day
- 100 Daily Blessings
- 14 Adar (15 in walled cities); Megillah, gifts to friends/poor, and feast
- 17 Tammuz
- 17 Tammuz — fast marking breaching of Jerusalem's walls
- 33rd day of the Omer; mourning eased in many communities; Chabad keeps haircut restrictions until Shavuot except for a 3 year old boy's first haircut
- A Chumash (from chamesh, "five") is a printed book of the five books of the Torah — Bereishit, Shemot, Vayikra, Bamidbar, and Devarim. Most Chumashim include commentaries such as Rashi. You use a Chumash for Shnayim Mikra, following along in shul, or learning at home.
- A Sefer Torah is a Torah scroll — the entire five books of the Torah handwritten by a sofer on kosher parchment, rolled on two wooden rollers (atzei chayim). It is read in synagogue with special honor; you usually do not study daily from the scroll the way you use a Chumash. Missing or extra letters can invalidate a scroll, so scribes train for years.
- A bentcher (birkon) is a small booklet with Birkat Hamazon and sometimes Shabbat songs and benching additions. Keeping one at the table makes bentching after bread meals easy. Many are bilingual; some include zimun instructions and festival inserts.
- A blech is a metal cover placed on the stove to cover flames and heat food on Shabbat.
- A bracha is a structured blessing, usually opening with "Baruch Atah Hashem…" It is how we acknowledge G-d as the Source of all blessing before eating, before mitzvot, and at holy moments. Different foods and mitzvot have specific brachot; saying the wrong one or none when required is a halachic issue. Brachot also train gratitude — pausing to recognize that what we enjoy comes from Above.
- A companion for your day
- A gemach (gemilut chasadim) is a free-loan fund or kindness society — lending items or money without interest, or channeling charity. On Purim, giving through a trustworthy gemach can fulfill matanot la'evyonim if the poor receive funds Purim day (and it's the same day you're celebrating Purim). Gemachim also run baby gear, wedding dress, and medical equipment loans in many communities.
- A halachic hour (sha'ah zmanit) is one twelfth of the daylight period from dawn to dusk — so it is longer in summer and shorter in winter, unlike a 60-minute clock hour. Deadlines such as latest morning Shema, chametz sale times, and plag hamincha use these hours. Apps and Jewish calendars convert them for your location.
- A keli is a vessel, utensil, or tool. It generally refers to any manufactured object or receptacle that holds, processes, or contains something. A keli is used for netilat yadayim — a cup, bottle, or washing cup (usually) with two handles.
- A kezayit is the olive-sized portion in Jewish law used to measure how much food you must consume for mitzvot — eating matzah at the Seder, maror, bentching after bread, and after-blessings. Because ancient volumes are difficult to calculate precisely, rabbis have established modern equivalents. Halachically, a kezayit is tied to the size of an egg — usually calculated as either half or one-third of an egg's volume depending on the authority. Many major organizations (such as Star-K) equate a kezayit to about 1.2 to 1.3 fluid ounces (approx. 35–40 ml), which visually translates to the size of a golf ball or a roll of quarters. By weight, this generally ranges between 25 and 33 grams — the stringent opinion of the Chazon Ish is approx. 33 g; the more lenient opinion of Rabbi Chayim Na'eh is approx. 27 g. (Note: For porous foods like matzah, the required weight is typically lower — around 15–20 grams depending on your rav.) The exact size depends on which authority you follow; ask your rav or use a community chart for the specific food or mitzvah you are measuring. Eating less than a kezayit may mean the mitzvah was not fulfilled.
- A machzor is a prayer book for the Jewish festivals and High Holidays — Rosh Hashana, Yom Kippur, Pesach, Sukkot, and Shavuot. It includes texts and prayers not found in the daily siddur. For Rosh Hashana and Yom Kippur, a machzor is required to perform the proper prayers.
- A mechitza divides men and women's seating in shul during prayer so focus stays on tefillah per traditional practice. Heights and designs vary by community.
- A mezuzah is the klaf — handwritten parchment with Shema and V'ahavta passages — affixed to doorposts of rooms used for dwelling. The case is protection for the scroll, not the mitzvah itself. Check scrolls by a sofer every few years. Touching the case and kissing the mezuzah when entering reminds you that G-d watches over the home.
- A mikveh is a kosher ritual pool of natural water meeting halachic size and flow rules. It is used for taharat hamishpacha, conversion, tevilat keilim, and some utensil and status changes. Immersion is a powerful transition — the person or vessel emerges spiritually renewed. Community mikvaot are discreet and staffed by trained attendants.
- A minhag is a binding community or family custom rooted in Jewish life — not merely "what people happen to do." Minhag can shape prayer text (nusach), mourning practices, Pesach stringencies, and holiday joy. When a minhag is established with rabbinic approval in a community, it often carries nearly the weight of law for that community. If you are new or between communities, ask your rav which minhagim to follow.
- A minyan is ten Jewish men above bar mitzvah age (13) forming a quorum for public prayer. Certain prayers — including Kaddish, Barchu, and repetition of the Amidah — require a minyan. The Talmud stresses that communal prayer is especially useful for getting prayers accepted by Heaven; joining a minyan is considered the ideal way of prayer.
- A parsha (parashah) is the weekly Torah portion read in synagogue on Shabbat morning. The Torah is divided into 54 portions so the whole five books are completed each year (with combined portions sometimes depending on the calendar). Shnayim Mikra means studying the same parsha during the week before it is read publicly. The name appears as "Parshat Bereishit," "Parshat Noach," and so on.
- A posek is a rabbi qualified to decide difficult halachic questions and write authoritative rulings. Your rav may consult poskim on novel cases. The term differs from a maggid or teacher who inspires but does not pasken. Major poskim include figures behind the Mishnah Berurah, Igrot Moshe, and contemporary halachic works your community follows.
- A revi'it (רביעית) is a standard unit of liquid volume in halacha, defined as one-quarter of a log — commonly translated as the volume of an egg and a half. It is the minimum volume required for ritual mitzvot involving liquids, such as Kiddush. Because physical volumes have shifted and been debated, several contemporary measurements exist depending on the posek followed: Rav Chaim Na'eh: ~86 ml (approx. 2.92 fl oz) — widely used as a lenient baseline, particularly in Sephardic tradition and for rabbinic obligations like netilat yadayim. Rabbi Moshe Feinstein: ~130–150 ml (approx. 4.4–5.1 fl oz) — many recommend this for Torah-level obligations like Shabbat Kiddush. The Chazon Ish: ~150 ml (approx. 5.1 fl oz) — stringent standard in many Ashkenazi and Hasidic communities for Torah-level mitzvot. Besides Kiddush, a revi'it is also required for Havdalah (the cup must hold a revi'it; you drink maleh lugmov), netilat yadayim (minimum water poured — may rely on the smaller measure), and the four cups at the Pesach Seder. After Kiddush, you drink maleh lugmov (a cheekful) or ideally the majority of the cup; ask your rav which measure to use.
- A series of short blessings said at the beginning of the morning prayer service, thanking G-d for things we tend to take completely for granted.

What they are:
Birchot HaShachar (בִּרְכוֹת הַשַּׁחַר — Blessings of the Dawn) is a sequence of ~15 blessings at the start of Shacharit. They originated as blessings said at each stage of waking up and preparing for the day — as you open your eyes, stand up, put on clothes, and step outside.

What you thank G-d for:
• Giving intelligence to the rooster to distinguish day from night
• Not making you a non-Jew, a slave, or a woman (men's version; women say "for making me according to His will")
• Opening the eyes of the blind
• Clothing the naked
• Straightening those who are bent
• Spreading the earth over the waters
• Giving strength to the weary
• And more...

What comes before Birchot HaShachar:
In the full morning service, Korbanot (passages about Temple offerings) typically come before or after these blessings. While these may be skipped if time is short, saying them is an important part of the complete service.

Timing:
• The earliest time to say Birchot HaShachar is from chatzos (halachic midnight / chatzos halayla).
• L'chatchila (ideally), say the first blessing — HaNoten LaSechvi Vinah (who gives the rooster understanding to distinguish day from night) — after alot hashachar (dawn). If you said it after chatzos, you have still fulfilled your obligation.
• The other blessings in the sequence may be said from chatzos onward if you wake up very early.
- A shaliach tzibur is the prayer leader who represents the congregation before G-d — reciting the repetition of the Amidah and guiding pacing. He must be someone the community accepts and who knows the laws of prayer. Women and men have different roles per community in who may lead which parts.
- A siddur (from seder, "order") is the Jewish prayer book with the fixed texts for daily and Shabbat services — blessings, Shema, Amidah, Birkat Hamazon, and more. Editions follow nusach (Ashkenaz, Sefard, Chabad, etc.), so words and order differ slightly. Your siddur is your map for davening.
- A sofer (scribe) is trained to write STaM — Torah scrolls, tefillin, mezuzot, and megillot — by hand with special ink on parchment. Letters must be formed exactly; mistakes can invalidate the scroll. Soferim also check existing klafim. Never buy tefillin or mezuzot without reliable certification.
- A sukkah is a temporary booth with schach roof where we eat (and some sleep) on Sukkot — recalling desert clouds of glory. Walls must stand; schach must shade more than sun. Decorate for joy; invite guests and ushpizin. Rain may exempt you from eating in the sukkah — follow halacha for your situation.

- Acharon shel Pesach
- Actively oppose idolatry! 🔨 The Rambam explains in Chapter 7 that we have a positive commandment to destroy items of idol worship and everything used for it. Here's something powerful: We don't just avoid idol worship - we actively work to eliminate it! Did you know? Even the wood and stones from an idol must be destroyed. Today's mission: Take an active stand against false beliefs in your life.

- Add some sweetness to Shabbat! 🍪 Bake or prepare some special treats for the holy day. Here's a fun mitzvah hack: Taste-test your creation before Shabbat to make sure it's perfect - this 'quality control' is actually an additional mitzvah of Shabbat preparation!

- Adjust wait times →
- Afikoman
- Afikoman — The Afikoman is matzah eaten at the end of the Seder so no other food follows — remembering the Pesach sacrifice. Children often "steal" it for a prize, adding joy. You need a kezayit-sized piece eaten before chatzos halachic (midpoint of the night) per many poskim. It is the last taste of matzah the Seder requires.
- After Mezonot, wine/grape juice, and/or shivat ha-minim tree fruits — select what you had at this sitting.
- After Yom Kippur ends at nightfall${'$'}tzeitLine, it is a mitzvah to eat a proper meal — not only a quick bite.

What to do:
• Complete Maariv and Havdalah (Havdalah includes a candle that was lit throughout Yom Kippur, wine, and no spices).
• Eat a festive break-fast — many prepare food in advance because cooking restrictions on Yom Kippur end at nightfall.
• Chabad tradition (Baal HaTanya) teaches that one's livelihood (parnassa) for the year is especially connected to this meal — eat with joy and spiritual intention.

This item becomes available at nightfall after the fast ends.
- After dairy → before meat
- After dairy: wait {wait} before meat
- After eating meat, you wait before eating dairy, and vice versa. The wait time is a matter of your family tradition — ask your rabbi if unsure.
- After eating or drinking, we thank G-d with an after-blessing (bracha acharona). Which blessing you say depends on what you ate or drank — and whether you ate or drank enough, within the required time.

The after-blessings:

Birkat Hamazon (בִּרְכַּת הַמָּזוֹן — Grace After Meals):
• After eating bread (Hamotzi)
• A full multi-paragraph prayer of thanks; a Torah-level commandment (Deuteronomy 8:10)
• When three or more adult Jewish men eat bread together, Zimmun (זִמּוּן — a formal invitation) is recited together before Birkat Hamazon
• A bentcher (בֶּנְטְשֶׁר) is a small booklet containing the text — commonly found at Shabbat tables
• After a bread meal: if you ate at least a kezayit of bread within k'dei achilat pras, Birkat Hamazon (bentching) covers everything else you ate at that same meal — wine, meat, fruit, dessert, etc. You do not say separate after-blessings for those other foods.

Al HaMichya (עַל הַמִּחְיָה — Bracha Achat Me'ein Shalosh):
• After Mezonot — grain foods that are not bread: cake, crackers, pasta, cereal, etc. (wheat, barley, spelt, oat, rye)
• A condensed “one blessing like three” derived from Birkat Hamazon
• At the same sitting, if you also had enough wine/grape juice or enough shivat ha-minim tree fruits (below), use one Al HaMichya text with the inserted phrases (v'al hagafen v'al pri hagafen, v'al ha'etz v'al pri ha'etz) in your siddur

Al HaGafen (עַל הַגָּפֶן וְעַל פְּרִי הַגָּפֶן):
• After wine or grape juice — the full after-blessing is על הגפן ועל פרי הגפן (the vine and the fruit of the vine), not על הגפן alone
• At least a revi'it within k'dei shtiyat revi'it (not Borei Nefashot)
• Same category as the before-blessing Borei pri ha'gafen (בּוֹרֵא פְּרִי הַגָּפֶן)
• Wine or grape juice only — fresh grapes eaten as fruit take Al HaEtz (see below), not Al HaGafen

Al HaEtz (עַל הָעֵץ וְעַל פְּרִי הָעֵץ — Bracha Achat Me'ein Shalosh):
• After shivat ha-minim (Seven Species) tree fruits: dates, figs, pomegranates, olives — and fresh grapes eaten as fruit (not grape juice)
• Fresh grapes: Because grapes are one of the Seven Species, eating a kezayit of fresh grapes always requires Al HaEtz (Me'ein Shalosh), not Borei Nefashot — whereas regular tree fruits like apples or oranges take Borei Nefashot only
• The full phrase is על העץ ועל פרי העץ (the tree and the fruit of the tree) throughout the blessing — opening, thanks, and closing — not על העץ alone
• Not for apples, oranges, bananas, berries, or other non–shivat ha-minim tree fruits — those use Borei Nefashot only
• A kezayit within k'dei achilat pras

Borei Nefashot (בּוֹרֵא נְפָשׁוֹת — "Who creates souls"):
• After most other foods: meat, fish, eggs, dairy, vegetables, and fruits that are not shivat ha-minim
• After most drinks other than wine/grape juice, if you drank a revi'it within k'dei shtiyat revi'it
• The shortest after-blessing; can be memorized in minutes

How much food — k'dei achilat pras (eating):
• Generally need at least a kezayit (כְּזַיִת — about olive-sized; many use roughly 1 oz / 30 g)
• Eaten within k'dei achilat pras (כְּדֵי אֲכִילַת פְּרַס — the time to eat about half a loaf): lechatchila about 4 minutes; many poskim allow up to about 6–9 minutes bedi'eved (Shulchan Aruch / Mishnah Berurah)
• Less than a kezayit, or spread over longer than k'dei achilat pras: no after-blessing on that food

How much drink — k'dei shtiyat revi'it (drinking) — not the same as k'dei achilat pras:
Halacha often requires an action within a specific continuous timeframe. For eating a kezayit, that is k'dei achilat pras (minutes — see above). For drinking, it is much faster.

K'dei shtiyat revi'it (כְּדֵי שְׁתִיַּת רְבִיעִית) literally means the time it takes to drink a revi'it (about 3–5 fl oz / 86–150 ml depending on custom). It is a very short span — a few continuous seconds — used as a legal measurement for drinking.

For bracha acharona on a beverage:
• You need a revi'it drunk within k'dei shtiyat revi'it
• If you sip coffee slowly over 20 minutes, you did not drink the required volume within the required time — no after-blessing on that drink
• How many seconds? Poskim differ:
  — Sephardic / Rambam / Rav Ovadia Yosef: one continuous drink without removing the cup from your lips — the strictest view; halacha defines this qualitatively (not as a fixed second count), but it is only a brief uninterrupted span — often estimated at just a few seconds for a full revi'it
  — Ashkenaz / Mishnah Berurah: often estimated as a continuous sequence of normal gulps within 5–9 seconds max. To guarantee your obligation for an after-blessing, drink the beverage in a single, uninterrupted sequence.
• Cold drinks (water, juice): to be obligated in Borei Nefashot or Al HaGafen, swallow a revi'it quickly and continuously in those few seconds rather than nursing it slowly
• Hot coffee, tea, or soup: drinking that fast is difficult, so common practice is no after-blessing when you only sip slowly — unless you set aside at least a revi'it and drink it at once when cool enough

Drinks only — which after-blessing?
• Wine or grape juice: Al HaGafen (עַל הַגָּפֶן וְעַל פְּרִי הַגָּפֶן) if a revi'it within k'dei shtiyat revi'it
• Water, milk, soda, etc.: Borei Nefashot if a revi'it within k'dei shtiyat revi'it
• Mezonot snack plus wine at the same sitting: one Al HaMichya including the wine phrase if both meet their thresholds

Where to find the exact text:
Open your siddur or bentcher to the bracha acharona section for the exact texts.
- After meat → before dairy
- After meat: wait {hours} hours before dairy
- After the fast day is over, after nightfall when the fast ends: follow accepted Chabad psak — Ashkenazi custom often continues meat, wine, music, laundry, and bathing for pleasure until chatzos on 10 Av (not at nightfall of 9 Av itself). Ask your rav if unsure.

Nine Days Havdalah: use wine or grape juice; Ashkenazi-style custom often gives the cup to a child (ages 6–9) when possible.
- After the fast day is over, after nightfall when the fast ends: many Sephardim resume most restrictions at that point, while others continue meat, wine, laundry, and bathing for pleasure until chatzos on 10 Av — confirm your community's psak before resuming.
- After the fast day is over, after nightfall when the fast ends: some Nine Days restrictions begin to lift — but Ashkenazi custom continues meat, wine, music, laundry, and bathing for pleasure until chatzos (halachic midday) on 10 Av (not at nightfall of 9 Av itself). When 9 Av was Shabbat and the fast was Sunday, or when 10 Av is Friday, see the special-calendar notes above. Ask your rav before resuming.

Nine Days Havdalah: On Motzei Shabbat during the Nine Days, use wine or grape juice for Havdalah. Ashkenazi custom (Rema O.C. 551:10): ideally a child (ages 6–9) drinks the cup; if none is present, the one reciting Havdalah drinks it — the mitzvah of Havdalah overrides the custom of restraint.
- After using the bathroom, we wash our hands and say the Asher Yatzar blessing — every time you finish.

When to say it:
• Normally: say Asher Yatzar each time you use the bathroom, once you are done and have washed your hands.
• If you are ill — for example diarrhea or another condition where you may need the bathroom again right away — wait until you are reasonably sure you have finished for that round before saying it. Otherwise you might say the blessing and then need the bathroom again immediately, which is impractical and not the point of the bracha.

What it is:
Asher Yatzar (אֲשֶׁר יָצַר — "Who fashioned") is a blessing thanking G-d for the miraculous design of the human body. It is one of the most medically insightful prayers ever composed.

The blessing says:
G-d created the human body with wisdom — with openings and closings (tubes, valves, sphincters). If any one of them were to open when it should be closed, or close when it should be open, it would be impossible to survive.

Why this is profound:
Modern medicine has confirmed exactly this. A blocked ureter, a stuck heart valve, or a blocked bowel — any of these is a life-threatening emergency. Every time your body functions normally, it is a miracle. Asher Yatzar trains us to recognize it as one.

How to do it:
1. After using the bathroom, wash your hands (without a blessing, unless this is your morning washing)
2. Say Asher Yatzar — it takes about 20 seconds

Find the text in any Jewish prayer book (siddur) or bentcher.
- Afternoon Prayer
- Afternoon prayer (Mincha) {when}
- Aggadah is the non-legal storytelling and teaching in the Talmud and midrash — ethics, theology, and narrative. It complements halacha (law). Shabbat table stories and many midrashim are aggadah.
- Aggressively brushing or combing raw, tangled material to separate and align fibers. For hair, this means a hard-bristled brush that forcefully separates and tears strands falls under this or Gozez. On Shabbat, use a soft brush or wide-toothed comb gently on dry hair.
- Al HaNissim
- Al HaNissim ("for the miracles") is added to the Amidah and Birkat Hamazon on Chanukah and Purim. It summarizes the salvation — the Maccabees or Esther/Mordecai — and thanks G-d. On Purim Meshulash (Jerusalem when 15 Adar is Shabbat), Al HaNissim is recited only on Shabbat — not on Friday or Sunday when other Purim mitzvot occur.
- Al HaNissim — Al HaNissim ("for the miracles") is added to the Amidah and Birkat Hamazon on Chanukah and Purim. It summarizes the salvation — the Maccabees or Esther/Mordecai — and thanks G-d. On Purim Meshulash (Jerusalem when 15 Adar is Shabbat), Al HaNissim is recited only on Shabbat — not on Friday or Sunday when other Purim mitzvot occur.
- Al Netilat Yadayim
- Al Netilat Yadayim — blessing on ritual handwashing
- Al hamichya (Al HaMichya) is the after-blessing on mezonot, grain foods that are not bread — cake, crackers, pasta, cereal, etc. — when you ate a kezayit (approx. golf ball size of food) within k'dei achilat pras (4–9 mins). If you had a bread meal, Birkat Hamazon covers all foods. You may also need to say Birkat Hamazon if you ate a lot of mezonot — depending on the type of food. Ask your rav.
- Al netilat lulav
- Al netilat lulav is the blessing before taking the Four Species on Sukkot — "Who sanctified us with His mitzvot and commanded us to take the lulav." Men say it every day of the festival when taking the lulav (except Shabbat); women follow community custom — Ashkenazi women say the bracha and wave; Sephardic women generally wave without the bracha. Say Shehecheyanu on the first day you take the lulav; if you missed the first day, say it on the first day you take (Mishnah Berurah 651:12). Then wave the lulav in the directions your siddur prescribes.
- Al netilat lulav — Al netilat lulav is the blessing before taking the Four Species on Sukkot — "Who sanctified us with His mitzvot and commanded us to take the lulav." Men say it every day of the festival when taking the lulav (except Shabbat); women follow community custom — Ashkenazi women say the bracha and wave; Sephardic women generally wave without the bracha. Say Shehecheyanu on the first day you take the lulav; if you missed the first day, say it on the first day you take (Mishnah Berurah 651:12). Then wave the lulav in the directions your siddur prescribes.
- Aleinu
- Aleinu is a closing prayer declaring G-d's sovereignty and our hope for universal recognition of His kingship. It ends daily Shacharit and Maariv and appears near the end of High Holiday services. The bow at "we bend the knee" is a moment of submission and awe.
- Aleinu — Aleinu is a closing prayer declaring G-d's sovereignty and our hope for universal recognition of His kingship. It ends daily Shacharit and Maariv and appears near the end of High Holiday services. The bow at "we bend the knee" is a moment of submission and awe.
- Aliyah means "going up" — being called to bless the Torah reading in shul, or immigrating to Israel. In shul, the oleh says blessings before and after the portion; the baal koreh chants. Honoring aliyot goes to kohanim, leviim, a groom, bar mitzvah, and yahrzeit per order on the gabbai's list.
- All cooking is permitted for that day's needs — including liquids (soups, coffee, etc.), not only dry food.
- All done for now!
- All holidays & fasts
- All languages (online) →
- Alot hashachar is halachic dawn — when the sky begins to lighten. Many morning laws start here, such as the beginning of public fast days. It is before sunrise (netz). It is not the normal time for the bracha on tallit and tefillin — see misheyakir. In great need, Igros Moshe (O.C. 4:6) allows putting them on after alot hashachar without a bracha and reciting the brachos after misheyakir — ask your rav.
- Always consult a qualified Orthodox rabbi for personal halachic guidance.
- Amen
- Amen affirms a bracha someone else recited, "so may it be." The Talmud praises one who answers Amen even more than the one who made the blessing. It should not be rushed or mumbled; it is a small but constant mitzvah opportunity in synagogue and at home. The Talmud (Shabbat 119b) offers a homiletic reading: its letters recall "G-d, faithful King" (Keil Melech Ne'eman) — a drash, not the word's etymology.
- Amen — Amen affirms a bracha someone else recited, "so may it be." The Talmud praises one who answers Amen even more than the one who made the blessing. It should not be rushed or mumbled; it is a small but constant mitzvah opportunity in synagogue and at home. The Talmud (Shabbat 119b) offers a homiletic reading: its letters recall "G-d, faithful King" (Keil Melech Ne'eman) — a drash, not the word's etymology.
- Amidah
- Amidah / Shemoneh Esrei (Morning prayer)
- Amidah — The Amidah ("standing" prayer) is the core of every service — also called Shmoneh Esrei (eighteen, now nineteen blessings as 1 was added). It is recited silently while standing, facing Jerusalem or the Temple Mount (if inside Jerusalem), with feet together.
- An eruv (especially eruv chatzerot or city eruv) is a halachic enclosure letting Jews carry within a defined area on Shabbat. It requires kosher boundaries and community upkeep. When the eruv is down, carrying keys and pushing strollers outdoors may be forbidden — shuls often text status Friday. It does not permit driving or phones.
- Another Yom Tov day of Pesach begins after sunset today — no melacha from then.

• Festive meals in kosher-for-Passover utensils only.
• Light Yom Tov candles before sunset.
