- Currently using: {nusach}
- Cutting, tearing, or trimming any object to a specific, measured dimension for a productive purpose. Tearing toilet paper on perforated lines is a direct violation (cutting at a measured boundary). Snapping apart multi-pack yogurt containers along a perforated seam also falls here.
- DEBUG — Calendar simulator
- Daily Mitzvot Guide
- Daily Prayer
- Daily ongoing mitzvot
- Daily prayer (at least once)
- DailyHalacha.com
- Daven (davening) means to pray. You 'daven' the daily services (Shacharit, Mincha, Maariv). The word is Yiddish-derived Ashkenazi slang but widely understood. People often say "daven Shacharit" for a service and "daven" for praying in general.
- Davening
- Davening — praying; any prayer, often refers to the formal daily prayer services
- Day
- Dayeinu
- Dayeinu ("it would have been enough") is the Seder song listing gifts from the Exodus — each step alone would have justified gratitude. Singing it trains children and adults to notice cumulative kindness from G-d. It appears after the Maggid narrative and before Hallel.
- Dayeinu — Dayeinu ("it would have been enough") is the Seder song listing gifts from the Exodus — each step alone would have justified gratitude. Singing it trains children and adults to notice cumulative kindness from G-d. It appears after the Maggid narrative and before Hallel.
- Days of Awe
- Days of Awe — The Days of Awe (Aseret Yemei Teshuvah) span Rosh Hashana through Yom Kippur — a time of judgment, prayer, and teshuvah. Customs intensify: Selichot, charity, and asking forgiveness. Even Jews who are less observant year-round often attend services. The tone is solemn but hopeful — repentance can change a harsh decree.
- December
- Deep dive: Study one line of Torah today and REALLY get it! 🧠 The Sages teach that Torah contains both revealed (Nigleh) and hidden (Nistar) dimensions, and even a single line can hold infinite wisdom! Don't just skim; choose one pasuk (verse) or a short halacha and immerse yourself. Read the commentaries (like Rashi, Ramban, or a contemporary commentary) and truly ponder its deeper meanings. How does it apply to your life? What new insight can you glean? This focused, thoughtful approach to learning transforms a simple reading into a profound spiritual experience, connecting you directly to Hashem's infinite wisdom. Source: Pirkei Avot 2:16; Zohar, Parshat Yitro; Talmud, Eruvin 13a.
- Default wait times are set automatically based on your nusach if not customized here.
- Deliberately unraveling or separating woven or braided material with the goal of re-using or re-weaving the strands. Pulling a loose thread from a sweater that unravels a row of stitches is a practical example. Shabbat forbids both constructing and deconstructing the material world.
- Deliberately untying a knot. Any knot that is forbidden to tie is forbidden to untie. If a tight professional knot was intended to be permanent, it cannot be untied on Shabbat. An accidental knot — one never intended to be tied — may generally be untied leniently.
- Detaching any natural growth from the body of a living creature. This includes cutting hair, trimming fingernails, shaving, plucking, or pulling loose feathers from a pillow. Even biting fingernails or pulling loose skin is restricted. Preparing these beforehand is itself a mitzvah honoring Shabbat.
- Determines which mitzvot appear in your daily checklist
- Diaspora
- Diaspora — The Diaspora (Galut) means Jewish life outside the Land of Israel. For centuries communities developed local minhagim while staying loyal to Torah. Festival calendars often add an extra day of Yom Tov outside Israel. Aliyah to Israel is a mitzvah for many; Diaspora communities remain fully Jewish and vibrant.
- Different Jewish communities have slightly different prayer traditions (nusach). Choosing yours helps the app show the correct prayer text and customs.
- Disclaimer
- Discover #2 of the Constant Mitzvot: Don't believe in any other powers! 🚫 This isn't just about avoiding idol worship; it's about deeply internalizing that Hashem is the *only* true power in the universe. Everything – from the smallest atom to the grandest galaxy – is completely under His control. Nothing can act without His permission! Take a moment to recognize that every event, every challenge, and every blessing you experience is directly from Him, with no intermediary forces or independent powers. This profound belief brings immense trust and peace of mind. Source: Exodus 20:3; Rambam, Sefer HaMitzvot, Negative Commandment 2; Mishneh Torah, Avodat Kochavim 2:1.
- Discover G-d's unique unity! ✡ The Rambam reveals something mind-blowing in Halacha 7: G-d's oneness isn't like anything we know! A person is 'one' but has many parts (arms, legs, etc.). A group can be 'one' but made of many individuals. But G-d's oneness? Absolutely unique - no parts, no divisions, pure unity! Here's the challenge: Take a moment to contemplate this deep idea. Try to imagine a unity so complete that there's NOTHING outside of it!
- Discover a powerful prayer hack: persistent davening! 🗣️ Ever feel like your prayers aren't getting through? The Sages teach us the importance of persistent prayer. While there's no magic number like '20 times in a row,' the core idea is to truly pour out your heart and not give up. Think of it like a child asking a parent repeatedly for something they really need! Before you even ask for your desires, the kabbalists teach a powerful sequence: (1) Praise Hashem for His greatness, (2) Thank Him for all the blessings you already have. This helps open the 'gates of prayer' and puts you in a mindset of gratitude and trust. Keep davening with faith, and never despair! Source: Based on various rabbinic teachings on tefillah (prayer).
- Discover the Tikkun HaKlali — Rabbi Nachman's powerful ten psalms! 📖 Rabbi Nachman of Breslov revealed a specific spiritual remedy known as the Tikkun HaKlali (General Rectification), consisting of ten chapters of Tehillim (Psalms 16, 32, 41, 42, 59, 77, 90, 105, 137, 150). He taught that this special collection has immense power to rectify the soul, particularly in cases of spiritual impurity or distress. What's truly remarkable? Rabbi Nachman stated that the revelation of this unique remedy was 'a very wonderful and awesome rectification' that had remained hidden since the time of Creation! You can recite these psalms anytime you need spiritual cleansing, strength, or a profound connection to Hashem. Source: Likutei Moharan I, 205; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.
- Discover the deeper meaning of the beard in Judaism! 🧔 Did you know? Kabbalists say that the 13 attributes of Divine mercy can emanate through the hairs of the beard! Now that's what I call a valuable hairloom.
- Discover the letter Dalet (ד)! 🚪 The shape of this letter teaches us about humility - it's bent over, like someone who is humble. Its name means 'door' in Hebrew, reminding us that humility is the doorway to all spiritual growth. Did you know? The Dalet's numerical value is 4, connecting to the 4 directions - showing how a humble person is open to learning from everyone! Take a moment to practice humility today by learning something new from someone else.
- Discover the letter Nun (נ)! 🌊 The Nun teaches us about loyalty and trust (Ne'emanut). Did you know? Its bent shape (נ) represents a faithful servant, while its final form (ן) represents unwavering dedication. The Talmud reveals that Nun was left out of Ashrei (Psalm 145) because it represents 'falling' (Nofilah), but the next verse speaks of G-d's support - teaching that even in our falls, G-d catches us!
- Discover the letter Tav (ת)! 🎯 The last letter of the Alef-Bet represents truth (Emet) and perfection! Did you know? The word Emet (truth) is made up of the first letter (א), middle letter (מ), and last letter (ת) of the Alef-Bet, teaching that truth must be consistent from beginning to end! Today's mission: Make sure your words and actions align with truth.
- Discover the letter Tet (ט)! ✡ This amazing letter represents goodness - it's the first letter that appears in the Torah in the word 'Tov' (good)! Did you know? Its shape is curved inward, teaching us that true goodness is often hidden and modest. The numerical value is 9, representing the 9 months of pregnancy - a time when hidden goodness grows! Take a moment today to notice the hidden blessings in your life.
- Discover the letter Vav (ו)! ✡ This amazing letter is shaped like a hook, connecting heaven and earth! Did you know? The letter Vav means 'and', teaching us that everything in creation is connected! Its numerical value is 6, representing the six days of creation. Here's something cool: When you add a Vav to the beginning of a Hebrew word, it transforms future tense to past - showing how G-d transcends time! Take a moment to notice the connections in your life.
- Discover the letter Zayin (ז)! ✡ This powerful letter looks like a crown with a sword pointing down! Did you know? Zayin means 'weapon' in Hebrew, teaching us that Torah is our spiritual weapon against negativity! Its numerical value is 7, connecting to Shabbat, the 7th day. Here's something amazing: The Zayin's shape teaches us to crown our physical actions with spiritual purpose! Take a moment today to elevate a physical activity with holy intention.
- Discover the power of Divine protection! 🛡️ Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) how G-d turned Balaam's curses into blessings. Here's something amazing: A powerful sorcerer was hired to curse the Jewish people, but G-d made him speak beautiful blessings instead! Including the famous 'How goodly are your tents, Jacob' that we say every morning! The lesson? When others try to bring you down, G-d can transform their negativity into the biggest blessings!
- Discover the power of Peh (פ)! 👄 The Peh means 'mouth' and teaches us about the power of speech. Its shape includes a Bet (ב) on its inside (the empty space within it when written in a Torah scrollforms a bet), reminding us that what's inside us affects what we say.
- Discover the power of Psalm 7! 🛡️ Did you know? This psalm is David's response to false accusations! Here's something incredible: It teaches us how to maintain our integrity even when facing unfair criticism. Today's mission: Read this psalm and reflect on maintaining your moral compass in challenging times.
- Discover the power of Resh (ר)! 👑 This letter teaches us about leadership - it stands for 'Rosh' (head). Did you know? Its shape shows someone bent in humility, teaching that true leadership comes through serving others. The gap in its top shows that even a leader must remain open to learning from others. Today's mission: Practice being both a good leader and a good follower.
- Discover the power of the Mezuzah! 🚪 Did you know? It's like a Divine security system for your home! Sages say that when a destructive force wants to hurt someone, if it sees a kosher mezuzah on that person's doorpost, it will get scared by G-d's name which is written on it and run away.
- Discover the righteous Tzadik (צ)! ⚖️ This special letter represents the tzaddik (righteous person) whose actions are perfectly balanced. Did you know? Its shape shows a Nun (נ) with a Yud (י) on top - teaching that a tzaddik must combine humility (bent Nun) with connection to G-d (Yud)! The final form (ץ) extends below the line, showing how a tzaddik's influence reaches even the lowest places. Today's challenge: Balance kindness with boundaries in your interactions
- Discover the secret of the priestly sash! 🎗️ Did you know? In the Beit Hamikdash, a Kohen's sash was an incredible 32 amot long (about 48 feet)! Here's something amazing: The priests would wrap it around and around, creating layer upon layer. This teaches us that sometimes even the holiest people need multiple layers of protection to guard their spiritual essence. Today's challenge: Learn about the Kohanim's clothing and think about what they could symbolize.
- Dive into Kabbalah's depths! 🌊 The great rabbis emphasized that learning Kabbalah properly can transform your Jewish practice! Did you know? The Arizal said that in these later generations, it's not just permitted but a mitzvah to reveal these teachings! Here's something powerful: Kabbalah helps us understand the deeper reasons behind the mitzvot. Today's mission: Learn one mystical insight about a mitzvah you do regularly.
- Dive into Psalm 3 of Tehillim! 📖 Did you know? This powerful psalm was composed by King David when fleeing from his own son Absalom! Here's something amazing: Even in his darkest moment, David found strength in his faith. The psalm teaches us that G-d is our shield and protector, no matter how many opponents we face! Today's mission: Read this psalm and find your own strength in times of challenge.
- Dive into the deep end and learn the kosher fish signs that determine if your seafood platter is a go! 🐟 For a fish to be kosher, it needs two specific features: fins and scales. While the fins help it navigate, the Talmud teaches that if a fish has valid scales, it is guaranteed to have fins too! These aren't just any scales, though; they must be visible to the naked eye and easily removable without tearing the skin. 🛡️ Symbolically, the Sages teach that scales represent a coat of armor for spiritual protection against outside influences, while fins represent the ambition and direction needed to swim against the current of life. Interestingly, some fish are masters of disguise! The swordfish is a major point of debate because it has scales as a juvenile but loses them as it grows up. ⚔️ Then you have the sturgeon, whose bony ganoid scales are so stuck on that most rabbis rule it non-kosher, which is why your high-end kosher caviar is usually from a different fish like paddlefish or salmon! 🥯 Did you know? Even though most blood of animals and birds is forbidden to eat (that's why we salt our meat, to remove the blood), fish blood is actually permitted to eat (you're just supposed to leave some fish scales in there so people don't think you're drinking forbidden blood)! Bottoms up! Learn more!
- Dive into wisdom! Learn one law from the Mishneh Torah 📜. Written by the great Maimonides (Rambam), it's like a broad roadmap of all Jewish law. Each law you learn is a separate mitzvah - talk about a spiritual power-up! Choose any topic that interests you and explore the beauty of Torah wisdom.
- Do the mitzvah of Torah study! Here's a fun way to start - read this tiny but mighty verse: וּבְנֵי־דָ֖ן חֻשִֽׁים (U'vnei Dan Chusheem - Translation: And the sons of Dan: Chusheem). 📜 Fun fact: This is actually the shortest verse in the entire Torah! Even reading just one verse connects you to infinite wisdom. How cool is that? Also, Dan had only one son, Chusheem (and he happened to be deaf), but by the time the tribes were counted later on in the Torah, he had the second largest tribe! When you're blessed by G-d, anything is possible! ✡
- Do the mitzvah of experiencing both love and awe of G-d! ❤️ Watch a sunset, climb a mountain, feel the rain on your face. These wonders will blow your mind and make you realize how incredibly small and insignificant you are compared to the Creator. It's like loving a majestic lion – you're captivated by its beauty and power, but you also know you wouldn't want to mess with it. This awe-inspiring experience will fill you with love and a healthy dose of fear, reminding you of God's incredible might.
- Do the mitzvah of praying for others! Take a moment to think of someone who needs healing (refuah shleimah) 🙏. Did you know? The Hebrew word for prayer (tefillah) comes from the root word l'hitpallel- meaning 'to judge oneself.' When we pray for others, we become more compassionate people ourselves!
- Do the powerful mitzvah of praying for others! Think of someone who's looking for their soulmate (shidduch) and say a heartfelt prayer for them 💑. Your sincere prayer could be the key that opens the gates of blessing for them!
- Done
- Dosh — Threshing
- Double your love! ❤️ The Rambam explains two beautiful mitzvot: loving fellow Jews and showing special love to converts. Why the extra love for converts? They chose to join our people and follow Torah - that's amazing! Here's something practical: Make an extra effort today to show love to another Jew through action - whether they were born Jewish or chose to join our people!
- Double your mitzvah joy! Plan/prepare/order a special Shabbat treat 🍰. Here's the amazing part - just preparing for Shabbat is one mitzvah, and enjoying it on Shabbat is another! Whether it's your favorite dessert or a new delicacy you've been wanting to try, add to the holiness of Shabbat with some extra delight!
- Dry, fully cooked food only — reheat with a shinui (unusual method), e.g. upside-down on blech or inside another vessel. Food on blech from before Shabbat may stay.
- During Sefirat HaOmer we keep customs of mourning (aveilut) because Rabbi Akiva's 24,000 students died in a plague during this period between Pesach and Shavuot (Talmud, Yevamot 62b). Their deaths ceased on Lag BaOmer — which is why many communities ease some restrictions then, while others continue until Shavuot or the morning of the 33rd day of the Omer.

Why we mourn: The Omer is the path from physical freedom (Pesach) to spiritual receiving of Torah (Shavuot). The plague cut short Torah transmission — so we temper joy with restraint until we reach Matan Torah.

Common customs (timing varies — ask your rav):
• No live music (recordings and a cappella rules vary by posek)
• No weddings
• No haircuts for part or all of the Omer

Follow your community's start and end dates for these practices.
- Eat a proper meal after Maariv and Havdalah.
- Eating & Blessings
- Eating matzah on Pesach after the Seder night(s) is a mitzvah many observe, though not with the same strict obligation as the first Seder night(s).

Levels:
• First Seder night: Torah obligation (k'zayit) for men and women.
• Rest of Pesach including Chol HaMoed: Rabbinic command (if you have a second seder) and optional mitzvah to eat matzah every day of Pesach. Most will eat matzah at all Yom Tov and Shabbat meals.

How:
• Use shmurah matzah for the seder if available.
• A kezayit per meal is sufficient.
• No chametz or kitniyot (per your custom) the entire Pesach.
- Edot HaMizrach
- Edot HaMizrach (communities of the East) refers to Middle Eastern and North African Jewish kehillot — Iraqi, Syrian, Moroccan, Persian, Yemenite, and related traditions — usually praying Nusach Edot HaMizrach. Halacha generally follows Shulchan Aruch, but customs vary by kehilla. Not the same as Sephardi (Iberian diaspora / Nusach Sefard), though both differ from Ashkenaz.
- Edot HaMizrach communities follow different Omer traditions (Peninei Halakha 05-03-03):
• Many follow Shulchan Arukh O.C. 493:1-2 — mourning until the morning of the 34th day of the Omer.
• Many who follow the Ari act strictly and refrain from haircuts until the day before Shavuot (Kaf HaChaim 493:13, cited in Peninei Halakha 05-03-03).
• Some North African kehillot end mourning on Lag BaOmer.

Music, weddings, and haircuts follow your kehilla's psak — ask your rav.
- Edot HaMizrach minhag (many kehillot) — when to say Al Netilat Yadayim:
• Following Shulchan Aruch and the Rashba: morning washing primarily removes ruach ra'ah from sleep.
• Say the blessing right after the first morning washing when you can — ideally immediately, even if you will use the bathroom afterward.
• If you have an urgent, pressing need to use the bathroom, go first, then wash and recite the blessing.

Baladi Yemenite and some kehillot wash after the bathroom before reciting the blessing — follow your community's psak (Shulchan Aruch O.C. 4 and your rav).
- Edot HaMizrach siddurim open Mincha with Korbanot — especially Parshat HaTamid — before Ashrei and the Amidah, matching the Shulchan Arukh order (O.C. 1:5, 232:1). Some kehillot include additional passages; follow your siddur.
- Edot HaMizrach — Edot HaMizrach (communities of the East) refers to Middle Eastern and North African Jewish kehillot — Iraqi, Syrian, Moroccan, Persian, Yemenite, and related traditions — usually praying Nusach Edot HaMizrach. Halacha generally follows Shulchan Aruch, but customs vary by kehilla. Not the same as Sephardi (Iberian diaspora / Nusach Sefard), though both differ from Ashkenaz.
- Educational checklist — confirm practices with your rabbi.
- Electricity / driving / writing
- Elevate a soul! Say a prayer for someone who has passed away 🕯️. When we pray for the elevation of their soul, we create merit for them in the Olam HaBa (World to Come). Here's something amazing: our prayers can help lift their soul higher and higher in the spiritual realms! Plus, they can become advocates for us and their loved ones still here.
- Elevate your day: Express gratitude out loud to Hashem! 🙏 Rabbi Nachman of Breslov and countless sages teach that actively thanking Hashem for *everything* – even challenges – is a profound spiritual practice. It transforms harsh judgments into mercy and opens spiritual gates to blessings! Right now, try saying 'Thank You, Hashem!' for three *specific* things in your life. Be detailed: instead of a general 'thanks for food,' try 'Thank You, Hashem, for the delicious and nourishing breakfast I had this morning that gave me energy, and for all the amazing flavors infused in it!' Speaking your gratitude aloud creates a powerful spiritual channel and shifts your entire perspective. Source: Likutei Moharan I, 79; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.
- Elevate your physical self! 🚿 Take a refreshing shower or bath with the intention of honoring G-d by serving him with a clean body. Did you know? The great sage Hillel taught that keeping our body clean is actually a mitzvah! When you care for your body with this holy intention, even something as simple as washing becomes a spiritual act! Source: (Vayikra Rabbah 34:3)
- Elokei Neshama
- Elokei Neshama thanks G-d in the morning for restoring the soul, which was pure and will be returned at death. It follows Birchot HaShachar in many siddurim. The prayer teaches that each day is a new loan of life — use it for mitzvot, not only errands.
- Elokei Neshama — Elokei Neshama thanks G-d in the morning for restoring the soul, which was pure and will be returned at death. It follows Birchot HaShachar in many siddurim. The prayer teaches that each day is a new loan of life — use it for mitzvot, not only errands.
- Elul
- Elul is the final month before the new year, serving as the dedicated season of spiritual preparation, soul-searching, and repentance ahead of the High Holidays.
- Elul — Elul is the final month before the new year, serving as the dedicated season of spiritual preparation, soul-searching, and repentance ahead of the High Holidays.
- Embrace the gift of Torah study! 📚 The Rambam reveals something amazing: Learning Torah isn't just about gaining knowledge - it's about connecting to G-d's wisdom! Did you know? Every word of Torah you learn creates an angel that will accompany you forever! Here's your mission: Set aside even 5 minutes today to learn something new. Whether it's a verse, a law, or a story - every moment of learning is priceless!
- Embrace the power of Teshuvah (repentance)! 🌱 Did you know? The Rambam teaches that even one thought of repentance can transform a person instantly! Here's something incredible: True repentance has the power to convert past mistakes into merits. Today's mission: Take a moment to reflect on one thing you'd like to improve, and make a positive change.
- Embrace the power of the Shema! 🌅 Did you know? This central prayer is recited twice daily to declare G-d's unity and our love for Him! Here's something amazing: The Shema contains fundamental principles of Jewish faith packed into just a few verses. And the sages teach it has the power to protect over entire bodies!Today's mission: Say the Shema with intention, focusing on the words.
- Emunah
- Emunah is faith that G-d exists, is one, and guides history and your personal life with purpose. It is not blind denial of difficulty — many psalms and stories show righteous people struggling — but a commitment that there is meaning even when we cannot see it. Strengthening emunah often comes through Torah study, mitzvot, and reflecting on past kindnesses you received.
- Emunah — Emunah is faith that G-d exists, is one, and guides history and your personal life with purpose. It is not blind denial of difficulty — many psalms and stories show righteous people struggling — but a commitment that there is meaning even when we cannot see it. Strengthening emunah often comes through Torah study, mitzvot, and reflecting on past kindnesses you received.
- Enable Translation
- English
- Enjoy the official Mitz Mode song!
- Eretz Yisrael
- Eretz Yisrael — the Land of Israel
- Erev
- Erev Pesach
- Erev Pesach (14 Nissan) is the day of final Pesach preparation before the Seder.

Key preparations:
• Finish eating chametz by the end of the morning (check your local zmanim).
• Bedikat chametz — search for chametz by candlelight after nightfall the night before (or according to your community's schedule).
• Biur chametz — destroy remaining chametz the next morning.
• Sell chametz through your rabbi if needed.
• Prepare the Seder plate, matzah, wine, and Haggadot for the first night(s).

The Seder is held after nightfall on 15 Nissan (and the second night in the Diaspora). No chametz may be eaten after the morning deadline on Erev Pesach.
- Erev Shabbat
- Erev Tisha B'Av (8 Av afternoon): stop learning Torah except sad topics; eat the final meal (seudah hamafseket) before the fast. Tallit and tefillin are worn at Shacharit on Erev Tisha B'Av — the restriction applies on Tisha B'Av day itself. At the seudah hamafseket, do not bentch with a mezuman or at a minyan meal — only the simple final meal before the fast.

Tisha B'Av (9 Av) — the fast and the day:
• Full 25-hour fast from sunset to nightfall; kinot at Shacharit without tallit and tefillin (Ashkenazi/Chabad omit morning tefillin; Sephardi custom varies).
• ${TishaBeavTefillinRules.fastDayTefillinNote(nusach)}
• Until chatzos on 9 Av: maintain a mournful mindset; sit on the floor or a low stool (no seat higher than about 12 inches / 30 cm).
• After chatzos on 9 Av: the five afflictions still apply until nightfall when the fast ends — but do not greet others during the fast day (until nightfall when the fast ends).
• Hand washing: only to remove ritual impurity (tum'a) — wash only to the knuckle where the fingers join the hand; or to remove actual dirt where it is on the hand.
• Do not brush teeth with water; a dry toothbrush is permitted. Flossing is permitted.
• ${TishaBeavTefillinRules.FAST_DAY_TRAVEL_NOTE}

When 9 Av begins on Motzei Shabbat (Saturday night fast):
• Recite Baruch ha'mavdil when Shabbat ends; say the candle blessing after Maariv.
• Do not say the rest of Havdalah that night. After the fast day is over, after nightfall Sunday night, recite Havdalah over wine with the hamavdil paragraph only — no spices that week.

Shabbat Chazon (the Shabbat before 9 Av): Shabbat is observed normally — meat and wine are permitted.

When 9 Av falls on Shabbat and the fast is observed Sunday (10 Av):
• Because you are already fasting on 10 Av, the usual daytime mourning restrictions for the 10th are heavily altered.
• Ashkenazim: laundry, bathing, and haircuts are permitted immediately Motzei fast Sunday night — you do not wait until Monday chatzos. Meat and wine remain prohibited Sunday night; resume Monday morning. Havdalah is delayed from Saturday night until Sunday night after the fast over wine. Music: Ashkenazi custom often continues until Monday morning, though some lenient views permit it Sunday night.
• Sephardim: when the fast is observed Sunday, laundry, haircuts, and bathing are permitted immediately Motzei fast Sunday night; only meat and wine remain restricted until Monday morning. When 10 Av is Friday, many resume bathing and laundry immediately Thursday night after the fast; follow your rav for meat restrictions.

When the fast falls on Thursday and 10 Av is Friday (Shabbat prep — kvod Shabbat):
• Haircuts, shaving, laundry, and bathing are permitted Friday morning right away so you can prepare for Shabbat — you do not wait until Friday chatzos for these.
• Ashkenazim still refrain from meat and wine until chatzos (midday) Friday, unless tasting food being cooked specifically for Shabbat.
• Sephardim generally permit bathing and laundry immediately Thursday night after the fast, but keep the meat restriction until Friday sunset.
• After the fast ends Thursday night, some melacha related to personal pleasure may resume in specific cases — ask your rav before resuming music, laundry, or bathing for pleasure.
- Erev Tisha B'Av prepares us for mourning the destruction of the Temple.

When the fast begins:
• Tisha B'Av starts at sunset tonight${'$'}sunsetLine and continues until nightfall tomorrow night — not dawn to dusk like minor fasts.

Restrictions from chatzos (halachic midday) today:
• From chatzos onward: do not eat meat or drink wine (some extend other restrictions — follow your minhag).
• Reduce pleasurable activities; avoid laundry, haircuts, and swimming per mourning customs.

Seudah hamafseket — the final meal before the fast:
• Eat a simple meal alone, sitting on the floor or a low stool, with only one cooked dish (e.g. a hard-boiled egg dipped in ashes, or bread with cold water).
• Do not eat two cooked dishes together; do not recline; do not greet others with "hello."
• Finish before sunset; afterward, only water is permitted until the fast begins.
• Many recite Birkat HaMazon and then change into non-leather shoes before sunset.

After sunset tonight until nightfall tomorrow:
• Five afflictions apply (like Yom Kippur): no eating/drinking, no leather shoes, no bathing for pleasure, no anointing, no marital relations.
• Additionally: no Torah study except sad topics (Eichah, Job, mourning laws); many sit on the floor until chatzos tomorrow; kinot are recited.${'$'}shabbatNote

Ask your rav for details if you are ill, pregnant, or nursing.

- Eruv chatzerot
- Eruv chatzerot — symbolic merging of courtyards allowing carrying on Shabbat
- Eruv tavshilin
- Eruv tavshilin is a symbolic meal set aside on Erev Yom Tov when Shabbat follows immediately afterward. You take a baked food (challah or matzah) and a cooked food (often meat, fish, or an unpeeled hard-boiled egg), recite the blessing and declaration from your siddur or machzor in a language you understand, and thereby may continue food preparation on Yom Tov for Shabbat — which would otherwise be forbidden because you may not cook on one holy day for the next. Store the designated foods in a safe, labeled spot — only the cooked dish is essential; if the baked item is accidentally eaten or discarded, the eruv remains valid. If the cooked dish is destroyed before you finish Friday cooking for Shabbat, the eruv is void. It also reminds the home to prepare for Shabbat, not only for the festival.
- Eruv tavshilin — Eruv tavshilin is a symbolic meal set aside on Erev Yom Tov when Shabbat follows immediately afterward. You take a baked food (challah or matzah) and a cooked food (often meat, fish, or an unpeeled hard-boiled egg), recite the blessing and declaration from your siddur or machzor in a language you understand, and thereby may continue food preparation on Yom Tov for Shabbat — which would otherwise be forbidden because you may not cook on one holy day for the next. Store the designated foods in a safe, labeled spot — only the cooked dish is essential; if the baked item is accidentally eaten or discarded, the eruv remains valid. If the cooked dish is destroyed before you finish Friday cooking for Shabbat, the eruv is void. It also reminds the home to prepare for Shabbat, not only for the festival.

- Eruv techumin — extends the Shabbat walking boundary in special cases

- Ever dreamed of being a king? 👑 Discover the surprising halachot (laws) of a Jewish King! Unlike other monarchs, a Jewish King had strict limits to keep him humble and focused on Hashem. For example, he couldn't acquire too many wives (to avoid his heart turning away from God - a maximum of 18, according to the Talmud), or too many horses (so he'd rely on Divine protection, not military might), or excessive silver and gold (to prevent arrogance). His primary role wasn't just ruling, but being a spiritual shepherd, a scholar immersed in Torah, and a righteous judge. These laws teach us that true leadership in Judaism is about humility, integrity, and putting the welfare of the people (and God's will) first. Even if you're not royalty, you can try to embody these kingly qualities in your own life!
- Ever wonder about some wild Torah laws? 🔮 Check this out: The Torah talks about something called 'Ov' - where people would burn incense, wave myrtle branches, and try to hear voices from... skulls! (Yes, you read that right!) The Rambam explains that someone doing this would hear voices seemingly coming from under the ground or even their armpits! While this sounds like something from a spooky movie, it shows how the Torah protects us from harmful spiritual practices. Today's mission: Appreciate how Torah guides us to connect with G-d in proper ways.
- Every mitzvah you do today matters. Keep going.
- Exit
- Exit app?
- Expand
- Expand section
- Experience #4 of the Constant Mitzvot: Love Hashem! ❤️ This isn't just a feeling; it's an active mitzvah to constantly cultivate a deep love for God. How do you do that? By reflecting on all the incredible kindnesses Hashem bestows upon you every single moment – from keeping your heart beating to orchestrating every detail of your life for your ultimate good. The more you recognize His constant presence and boundless benevolence, the more your love and appreciation for Him will naturally grow. Take a moment now to list five specific amazing/miraculous things Hashem has done for you – and thank Him with love! Source: Deuteronomy 6:5; Rambam, Sefer HaMitzvot, Positive Commandment 3; Mishneh Torah, Yesodei HaTorah 2:2.
- Experience the beauty of creation! Find a scenic spot or look at a picture of magnificent nature 🏔️. From majestic mountains to tiny flowers, each part of G-d's world is perfectly designed. Take a moment to really notice the incredible artistry all around you, and thank G-d for creating all of it! 🌎
- Experience the joy of freedom! 🌅 Take a moment to remember our exodus from Egypt (one of the 10 daily remembrances): We were slaves doing back-breaking labor, but G-d turned everything around with amazing miracles! Even cooler? The Egyptians who refused to pay us for our work ended up giving us their wealth when we left! This teaches us that G-d can completely transform any situation - from darkness to light, from slavery to freedom. When things look tough, remember: salvation can come in the blink of an eye!
- Experience the mitzvah of tzitzit! For men: Get some tzitzit to wear, or a fresh new set for the holidays! For women: Consider get some new tzitzit as a gift for the special men/boys in your life 🎁. Did you know? The numerical value of tzitzit equals 600, and when you add the 8 strings and 5 knots, it equals 613 - the total number of mitzvot!
- Experience the power of Mezuzah! 🏠 The Rambam explains that every time you pass a Mezuzah, it reminds you of G-d's presence and awakens you from spiritual sleep! The word 'mezuzah' itself has a numerical value of 65 in gematria, which is also the value of the word 'hekhal,' meaning 'chamber' or 'palace.' This connection suggests that the mezuzah transforms a house into a miniature palace, a dwelling place for the Divine Presence. Additionally, the text of the mezuzah contains 713 letters, which is the numerical value of the word 'teshuvah,' meaning 'repentance.'
- Experience the power of Psalm 5! 🌅 Did you know? This morning prayer of King David teaches us how to start our day with faith! Here's something beautiful: The psalm shows us that G-d listens to our voice in the morning, teaching us to begin each day with prayer and hope. Mission: Start your morning by reading this uplifting psalm.
- Experience the power of community prayer! 👥 Did you know? Praying with a minyan (group of 10) amplifies the power of our prayers! Here's something amazing: The Talmud teaches that G-d never rejects the prayers of a community. Today's mission: Join a minyan for prayer if possible.
- Explanation
- Explore #3 of the Constant Mitzvot: Know that Hashem is One! ☝️ This isn't just about a numerical unity; it's about understanding that God is absolutely unique, indivisible, and utterly beyond our comprehension – the ultimate singularity! He is the source of all existence, which is completely unified in His essence and power. When you say the first line of Shema Yisrael, focus deeply on this profound truth. Many have the custom to close their eyes during this line to block out distractions and concentrate entirely on Hashem's absolute unity. You can fulfill this mitzvah any time by contemplating God's oneness and His singular control over the universe. Source: Deuteronomy 6:4; Rambam, Sefer HaMitzvot, Positive Commandment 2; Mishneh Torah, Yesodei HaTorah 1:7.
- Explore the fascinating topic of magic tricks in Torah law! 🎩 Here's something surprising: While the Torah prohibits actual sorcery, performing illusions for entertainment is usually fine, as long as people understand it's not real! Did you know? The key difference is that magic tricks nowadays are clearly understood to be skillful illusions, not supernatural powers.
- Explore the foundations of faith! ✡ The Rambam teaches something amazing: The very first mitzvah isn't just to believe in G-d, but to KNOW there is a G-d. How do we do this? Look at creation with wonder - from the precise orbits of planets to the complexity of a single cell! Just like you know the house you're in had a builder without seeing them, the intricate design of the universe proves there's a Creator! Take a moment to notice the incredible wisdom in G-d's creation around you.
- Explore the majesty of Psalm 8! 🌠 Did you know? This psalm celebrates the wonder of G-d's creation and humanity's special role in it! Here's something amazing: When David gazed at the stars, he was inspired to write about human dignity and our Divine purpose. Today's mission: Step outside, look at the night sky, and recite this uplifting psalm.
- Explore the mighty Aleph (א)! ✡ The first letter of the Alef-Bet represents G-d's oneness and unity! Did you know? Its shape is made of a Vav (ו) with two Yuds (י), representing heaven above and earth below, connected by G-d's presence! The numerical value is 1, teaching us about G-d's absolute unity. Today's mission: Notice how G-d connects heaven and earth in your daily life.
- Explore the mighty Shin (ש)! 🔥 This powerful letter has three upward points, representing the three Patriarchs! Did you know? It appears on every mezuzah case and tefillin, representing one of G-d's names (Sha-dai). Its shape resembles a flame, teaching us to approach Torah with fiery enthusiasm. Today's mission: Learn Torah with passion and joy.
- Explore the mystical letter Gimmel (ג)! ✡ This amazing letter teaches us about kindness: Its shape looks like someone running - specifically, running to do good deeds! The long 'leg' of the letter reaches down, symbolizing how we should reach out to those in need. Did you know? Gimmel stands for 'Gomel' (bestowing good) - just as G-d constantly bestows goodness on us, we should bestow goodness on others! Take a moment to think: Who can you 'run' to help today? ✡
- Explore the mystical letter Heh (ה)! ✡ This powerful letter represents G-d's presence in our world! Its shape teaches us something amazing: It has an opening at the bottom, showing that even if we fall, we can always return to G-d. Did you know? The letter ה appears twice in G-d's four-letter name, teaching us that G-d is present in both the spiritual and physical worlds! Take a moment to notice G-d's presence in your life today.
- Explore the power of Mem (מ)! 💧 The letter Mem represents water (Mayim in Hebrew), the source of all life! Did you know? Like water, Torah wisdom flows from a higher place to a lower place - teaching us that true wisdom comes to those who are humble. The open Mem (מ) and closed final Mem (ם) show us that some parts of Torah are revealed while others remain hidden.
- Explore the power of Yud (י)! ✡ This tiniest letter holds incredible wisdom! Its small size teaches humility, yet it's the first letter of G-d's holy name! Did you know? The Yud represents a single point, teaching that even the smallest good deed can have infinite impact! Its numerical value is 10, connecting to the Ten Commandments.

- Find strength in Psalm 2! 👑 This powerful chapter speaks about G-d's ultimate sovereignty! Did you know? It describes how nations may plot, but G-d's plan always prevails! Here's something amazing: The psalm ends with the promise that all who trust in G-d are blessed. Today's challenge: Read this psalm and feel G-d's protective presence in your life.
- Find your 'golden middle path'! 🎯 Rambam teaches balance in most character traits - not too extreme, not too passive. Pick one area you struggle with (anger, pride, spending, speech, food) and choose one way to counterbalance your excessiveness for the rest of the day. You might find you enjoy life more when you feel balanced inside!

- Forgot Yaaleh V'yavo at Maariv on Rosh Chodesh? After Retzei or after the Amidah — do not repeat (Berachot 30b; SA O.C. 422:1). Still in Retzei before God's name — insert there.
- Forgot Yaaleh V'yavo? In Retzei — insert there; after concluding Retzei — return to beginning of Retzei; after Yihiyu L'ratzon — repeat only Mincha Amidah (SA O.C. 422:1).
- Forgot Yaaleh V'yavo? In Retzei — insert there; after concluding Retzei — return to beginning of Retzei; after Yihiyu L'ratzon — repeat only Shacharit Amidah (SA O.C. 422:1).
- Forming any meaningful symbol or letter on a surface. To violate the Torah prohibition, at least two distinct letters or symbols must be written with a permanent medium on a lasting surface. The Sages extend this to temporary writing and shaping letters. Playing Scrabble with tiles that lock into a frame is questioned by some authorities.

- Fulfill the beautiful mitzvah of Mezuzot! 🚪 If you don't have kosher Mezuzot on your doorways yet, it's a Torah commandment to get them affixed! Already have them? Make sure they're checked by a reliable Sofer at appropriate intervals (ask a rabbi) to ensure they remain kosher! Humidity, temperature changes, or even tiny cracks in the parchment or ink can render them invalid. Many people have the beautiful custom to touch the Mezuzah when they pass through a doorway and then kiss their fingers, helping us remember Hashem and His unity, and sanctifying our coming and going. The sages teach that the Mezuzah brings Divine protection to the home. Source: Deuteronomy 6:9; Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 285-291.
- Full Hallel
- Full Hallel — complete Hallel psalms (113–118) on Yom Tov days, all eight days of Chanukah, and the first day(s) of Pesach
- Gam zu l'tovah
- Gam zu l'tovah — this too is for the good; trust that hardship can be for the greater good
- Gan Eden
- Gan Eden (Garden of Eden) in Jewish thought often means the soul's reward after death — closeness to G-d, not a physical vacation. It pairs with Olam HaBa. The original Garden in Bereishit is the starting point of human story; tradition teaches souls return to a heavenly Gan Eden.
- Gan Eden — Gan Eden (Garden of Eden) in Jewish thought often means the soul's reward after death — closeness to G-d, not a physical vacation. It pairs with Olam HaBa. The original Garden in Bereishit is the starting point of human story; tradition teaches souls return to a heavenly Gan Eden.
- Gather for inspiration! 👥 Learn about the special mitzvah of Hakhel! Every seven years, the entire nation - men, women, and children - would gather to hear the king read from the Torah! Here's something amazing: Even babies were brought, and their parents received special reward for bringing them! The lesson? Sometimes just being present in a holy gathering can elevate your soul! Today's mission: Join a Torah class or gathering, even if online.
- Gebrochts ("broken") is matzah that has come into contact with liquid after baking — kneidlach (matzah balls) are the classic example. Some Chasidic communities observe the stringency of not eating gebrochts on Pesach. Others eat kneidlach freely. If you are a guest, follow the host's house rules; at home, follow your rav's psak consistently.
- Gemara
- Gemara — Talmudic commentary and discussion on the Mishnah
- Gender
- Geneivat da'at ("stealing the mind") is deception — advertising falsely, hiding defects, or misleading about credentials. It applies to customers, employees, and friends. The Torah demands "honest scales" in spirit and letter. Trust lost through geneivat da'at is hard to rebuild.
- Geshem
- Geshem — Rain. Can refer to the prayer for rain inserted on Shemini Atzeret throughout the winter
- Get a Chumash and open to a random page and verse. whatever verse you land on, it's supposed to be a message from G-d - Submitted by HB
- Get creative with Mishloach Manot (Purim food packages)! 📦😄 On Purim day, you are obligated to send food portions to at least *one* other Jewish adult to enhance friendship. But what counts? Here are the halachic requirements: (1) Minimum of *two different types* of ready-to-eat food or drink (e.g., a cookie and a juice box). No raw meat! (2) The package *must* be sent on Purim day itself (not before or after). (3) Ideally, it should be sent via a *messenger*, fulfilling the nuanced commandment of 'sending' (mishloach) rather than just delivering it yourself. Learn about it now and think about who you want to delight and enhance friendship with next Purim!
- Get ready for a hunt! Bedikat Chametz (Nighttime Search for Leaven)! 🔦 The night before Passover eve (the night of the 13th of Nissan), after dark, we conduct a formal search for Chametz (leavened food) by candlelight! Traditionally, a beeswax candle is used because it burns straight and illuminates well, but a flashlight works too. The search is a family affair, often involving a feather to sweep crumbs into a wooden spoon, which are then placed in a paper bag. A blessing is recited before the search. What's with the tradition of hiding ten pieces of bread? This clever custom ensures that the blessing isn't said in vain if you've already meticulously cleaned your home! After the search, a declaration (Kol Chamira) is made in Aramaic: 'Any chametz that is in my domain that I have not seen or removed — it is nullified and ownerless like the dust of the earth.' This 'Bitul' (nullification) is crucial, as it legally annuls your ownership of any inadvertently missed chametz. The next morning (Erev Pesach), any remaining chametz is burned (Biur Chametz), and a second declaration is made, covering chametz you might have eaten or missed. Learn more! Source: Mishnah, Pesachim 1:1; Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 432-435; Talmud, Pesachim 4b.
- Get ready for a wake-up call! 🎺 Explore the awesome mitzvah of blowing the Shofar on Rosh Hashanah! This isn't just about making noise; it's a powerful spiritual alarm clock for our souls, urging us toward introspection and Teshuva (repentance). The Shofar's blasts have deep meaning: Tekiah is a long, unbroken blast, symbolizing clarity, certainty, and our unified connection to Hashem. Shevarim consists of three shorter, broken blasts, evoking a sigh or cry, representing our brokenness and heartfelt regret for our shortcomings. Teruah is a series of at least nine rapid, staccato blasts, like rapid sobbing, signifying a desperate plea for Divine mercy and a readiness for spiritual awakening. The sequence of these sounds (Tekiah-Shevarim-Teruah-Tekiah, etc.) is crucial. Imagine these ancient sounds piercing the air on the Day of Judgment, stirring your soul to return to your Creator. Source: Numbers 29:1; Talmud, Rosh Hashanah 33b; Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 590.
- Get ready to experience the Sukkah spirit! ⛺️ During Sukkot, we have the special mitzvah of dwelling in a Sukkah – a temporary booth – just like camping, but with a huge spiritual punch! This unique dwelling reminds us of the clouds of glory that protected us in the desert and the fragility of life, encouraging us to trust completely in Hashem. What does 'dwelling' mean? It primarily means eating your meals there. For sleeping, the Shulchan Aruch (Orach Chaim 639) rules that men are obligated to sleep in the Sukkah, but women are exempt (though many choose to sleep there if comfortable). Sephardi practice is generally to be more stringent about sleeping in the Sukkah even if uncomfortable, while Ashkenazim are more lenient if there's significant discomfort (e.g., cold, rain, or a bad smell). The Talmud teaches that the Shechinah (Divine Presence) literally rests in a kosher Sukkah – no wonder it's a favorite holiday for so many! So, even if you can't build a full Sukkah, reflect on its themes of impermanence and Divine protection.
- Get rid of something chametz (leavened) you're not going to eat and have in mind that you're fulfilling the mitzvah of cleaning your home of chametz for Pesach. If it is currently Pesach, eat some matzah!
- Get wrapped up in the mitzvah of Tzitzit! 🧵 Did you know? These strings aren't just the original Jewish fashion statement - they provide divine protection from evil forces and even keep you from misdeeds! Sages say that a women who helps her husband to fulfill this mitzvah will be considered as if she wore them herself!
- Give gifts to the poor during the daytime only.
- Give the next person you see a blessing for a long and healthy life.
- Give tzedakah (charity)! Did you know? The word tzedakah actually means 'justice' - because sharing our blessings isn't just nice, it's the right thing to do! 💝 Pick any charitable organization and give what you can. Here's the cool part: even a small amount given with a happy heart makes a big difference in both this world and the spiritual realms!
- Giving charity (tzedakah — צְדָקָה) is a constant mitzvah. A foundational custom is maaser kesafim (מַעֲשֵׂר כְּסָפִים) — setting aside about ten percent of one's income for charity.

How to calculate:
Maaser is generally figured from net earnings, not gross: start with what you actually receive, then deduct taxes, necessary work-related expenses, and reasonable business overhead before taking ten percent. Ask your rabbi for guidance on your specific situation (self-employment, benefits, one-time windfalls, etc.).

Where to give:
Halachic priorities place local poor individuals, Torah institutions, and the needy of Israel at the top of the list. Excellent choices often include local family relief organizations, community food pantries, and trusted charities that serve those in need in your area and in Israel.

If money is tight:
Jewish law teaches that your own livelihood and basic needs come first. If you cannot comfortably afford the full ten percent without real hardship, you are not expected to compromise your financial stability. Give a minimal, symbolic amount when you can so you still take part in this vital mitzvah — and increase when circumstances allow.

This checklist item is a daily reminder to plan your giving with intention. Confirm amounts and recipients with your rabbi.
- Got some spare time? Visit someone who's sick. The Talmud (Nedarim 39b) says this removes 1/60th of their illness!
- Gozez
- Gozez — Shearing
- Gozez — shearing or cutting hair/nails melacha
- Grace After Meals
- Grace After Meals after eating bread
- Grace After Meals — Birkat Hamazon after eating bread meals
- Great!
- Guard against false prophecy! 🚫 The Rambam teaches in Chapter 9 of Yesodei HaTorah: Even if someone performs miracles, if they try to change or negate any part of the Torah, they're a false prophet! True prophecy always strengthens Torah, never weakens it. Today's mission: Stay strong in your commitment to Torah's eternal truth.
- Guard against harmful connections! 🤝 The Rambam teaches in Chapter 10 that we can't make certain types of agreements with idol worshippers or give them special privileges in Israel. Why? To maintain clear boundaries and prevent being influenced by their practices. Here's something fascinating: Even business partnerships needed to be carefully structured. Today's mission: Be mindful of influences in your life.
- Guard your body, mind, and soul — they're gifts from Hashem! 💫 This mitzvah isn't just about physical health; it's a holistic approach to serving G-d. The Rambam (Hilchot De'ot 3:3) teaches that maintaining your health is a Torah obligation, because 'it is impossible to understand and know G-d when one is sick or hungry.' Your body is the vessel for your soul's mission — a fit body supports a healthy spiritual life. For your MIND: engage in Torah study, which sharpens your intellect and purifies your thoughts. Avoid excessive worry and grudges, as they can harm both mind and body. For your SOUL: nurture it with regular prayer, mitzvot, and acts of kindness, strengthening its connection to its Divine Source. Simcha (joy) is also a crucial spiritual obligation; sadness can be a major barrier to spiritual growth. Practical takeaway: eat well, get enough sleep, exercise, and constantly seek joy. These aren't luxuries; they're spiritual necessities! Source: Deuteronomy 4:15; Rambam, Hilchot De'ot 3:3; Sefer HaChinuch, Mitzvah 225.
- Guard your heart against hatred! ❤️ The Torah teaches us something profound: Even if someone wronged you, don't harbor hatred in your heart. Instead, speak to them about what's bothering you! Did you know? The word for hatred in Hebrew (שנאה) has the same letters as the word for sleep (שינה), teaching us that hatred makes us spiritually asleep. Here's your mission: If you're feeling negative towards someone, try to have an honest, respectful conversation with them or speak to a rabbi for guidance.
- Guard your home from forbidden things! 🎨 The Rambam teaches in Hilchot Avodat Kochavim that we must be careful about certain types of images: Protruding human figures such as statues are forbidden, and we also can't make images of celestial bodies or angels at all! While some authorities extend these prohibitions to children's dolls, many are lenient. Today's mission: Be mindful of keeping your home free from problematic images!
- Guard your speech about G-d! 🗣️ The Rambam teaches us in Hilchot Avodat Kochavim that we must be extremely careful never to speak disrespectfully about G-d or deny His existence. Even casual oaths or unnecessary blessings are forbidden. Here's something powerful: Every word we speak about G-d should be with reverence and awe. Today's mission: Practice speaking about holy matters with extra care and respect.
- Guard your tongue! 👄 The power of speech is incredible - it can build worlds or destroy them! Here's something amazing: The Hebrew word for speech (דיבור) has the same numerical value as the word for bee (דבורה). Just like a bee can make honey or sting, our words can be sweet or harmful! Today's challenge: Before speaking about others, ask yourself: Is it true? Is it kind? Is it necessary? Make your words as sweet as honey!
- Guard your unique identity! 👕 The Rambam teaches in Chapter 11 of Hilchot Avodat Kochavim that we shouldn't imitate non-Jewish customs in dress or behavior. Here's something fascinating: This isn't about superiority, but about maintaining our unique spiritual identity! Did you know? Even seemingly neutral customs might need to be avoided if they have idolatrous origins. Today's mission: Take pride in your Jewish distinctiveness.
- Guardian of the doors of Israel (acronym Shin-Dalet-Yod (one of Hashem's names) on mezuzah)
- Guidelines
- HaNeiros halalu
- HaNeiros halalu — paragraph sung after lighting Chanukah candles
- Hadlakat Nerot
- Hadlakat Nerot (Shabbat candle lighting)
- Hadlakat Nerot is lighting candles before Shabbat or Yom Tov — welcoming holiness into the home. Women traditionally light; men if no woman is present. Ashkenazi and Chabad custom: daughters in the home may light their own candles with a bracha. Sephardic custom (Shulchan Arukh; Rav Ovadia Yosef): daughters under their parents' roof do not recite a bracha on separate candles if the mother already lit — that would be a bracha levatala. Ashkenazim often light then bless with eyes covered; Sephardim may bless first. Shabbat candles must be lit before sunset (not before Plag HaMincha). Weekday Erev Yom Tov candles are also lit before sunset. When Yom Tov begins on Motzei Shabbat, Yom Tov candles are lit only after tzeit, from a pre-existing flame. On other Yom Tov nights, before the holiday begins you may strike a new match; once Yom Tov has started, light only from a pre-existing flame.
- Hadlakat Nerot — Hadlakat Nerot is lighting candles before Shabbat or Yom Tov — welcoming holiness into the home. Women traditionally light; men if no woman is present. Ashkenazi and Chabad custom: daughters in the home may light their own candles with a bracha. Sephardic custom (Shulchan Arukh; Rav Ovadia Yosef): daughters under their parents' roof do not recite a bracha on separate candles if the mother already lit — that would be a bracha levatala. Ashkenazim often light then bless with eyes covered; Sephardim may bless first. Shabbat candles must be lit before sunset (not before Plag HaMincha). Weekday Erev Yom Tov candles are also lit before sunset. When Yom Tov begins on Motzei Shabbat, Yom Tov candles are lit only after tzeit, from a pre-existing flame. On other Yom Tov nights, before the holiday begins you may strike a new match; once Yom Tov has started, light only from a pre-existing flame.
- Hafrashat challah is separating a small portion of dough when baking a large amount of bread or similar flour-based foods, then burning or discarding in many customs. It recalls the Temple-era gift to the kohen (when they were in a state of ritual purity and able to eat it).
- Hagbah
- Hagbah is lifting the open Torah scroll so the congregation can see the writing and recite "V'zot HaTorah" (and related verses). Ashkenazim (and most Chabad communities) perform hagbah after the reading, before gelilah (rolling and dressing the scroll). Sephardim (Nusach Edot HaMizrach) perform hagbah before the reading begins — the scroll is shown, then read. Follow your shul's order so you know when to stand and respond.
- Hagbah — Hagbah is lifting the open Torah scroll so the congregation can see the writing and recite "V'zot HaTorah" (and related verses). Ashkenazim (and most Chabad communities) perform hagbah after the reading, before gelilah (rolling and dressing the scroll). Sephardim (Nusach Edot HaMizrach) perform hagbah before the reading begins — the scroll is shown, then read. Follow your shul's order so you know when to stand and respond.
- Haggadah
- Haggadah — The Haggadah ("telling") is the book guiding the Seder — questions, answers, plagues, Dayeinu, and Hallel. Its core is making the next generation feel they left Egypt. Many editions add commentaries; the mitzvah is participation and understanding, not speed. Mah Nishtanah is the children's opening question.
- Hakafot
- Hakafot — dancing while circling around the synagogue with Torah scrolls on Simchat Torah
- Halacha means "the way of walking" — the practical path of Jewish life drawn from Torah, Prophets, Writings, Mishnah, Talmud, and codes like the Shulchan Aruch. It covers prayer, Shabbat, diet, family life, business ethics, and festivals. Halacha is decided by qualified poskim; when you are unsure, the mitzvah is to ask your rav rather than guess.
- Halachipedia
- Half Hallel
- Half Hallel — shortened Hallel psalms on Rosh Chodesh and after the first Yom Tov of Pesach
- Hallel
- Hallel (praise) is a selection of Psalms 113–118 recited as praise and thanksgiving to G-d on Yom Tov (festivals), Rosh Chodesh (the new month), and Chanukah.

Full Hallel is recited on Shavuot, all seven days of Sukkot (including Chol HaMoed Sukkot), Shemini Atzeret, Simchat Torah, and all eight days of Chanukah.

Pesach — Full vs. Half Hallel:
• Outside Israel (Diaspora): Full Hallel on the first two nights/days of Pesach (Yom Tov); Half Hallel from Chol HaMoed onward through the last days.
• In Israel: Full Hallel only on the first day of Pesach; Half Hallel from the second day onward.
The reason for Half Hallel from Chol HaMoed: we do not fully celebrate while the Egyptians drowned in the sea.

Half Hallel (certain paragraphs omitted) is also recited on Rosh Chodesh.
- Hallel at Shacharit
- Hallel is a set of psalms (113–118, with variations) praising G-d for salvation. Full Hallel is recited on major festivals; Half Hallel (Partial Hallel) on Rosh Chodesh and the last 6 days of Pesach. Hallel is not recited on Rosh Hashana or Yom Kippur (Arachin 10b). It is said standing, with joy.
- Hallel — Hallel is a set of psalms (113–118, with variations) praising G-d for salvation. Full Hallel is recited on major festivals; Half Hallel (Partial Hallel) on Rosh Chodesh and the last 6 days of Pesach. Hallel is not recited on Rosh Hashana or Yom Kippur (Arachin 10b). It is said standing, with joy.
- Haman
- Haman is the Persian official who plotted to destroy the Jews in Esther's time. Mordechai and Esther foiled the plan. When the Megillah reader says Haman's name, many boo, rattle graggers, or stamp — erasing his name symbolically. The mitzvah is hearing the text, not drowning out so much that words are missed.
- Haman — Haman is the Persian official who plotted to destroy the Jews in Esther's time. Mordechai and Esther foiled the plan. When the Megillah reader says Haman's name, many boo, rattle graggers, or stamp — erasing his name symbolically. The mitzvah is hearing the text, not drowning out so much that words are missed.
- Hamapil
- Hamapil (Blessing before sleep)
- Hamapil blessing (according to many opinions)
- Hamapil is the very last thing said before closing your eyes to sleep.

What it is:
Hamapil (הַמַּפִּיל — "who casts") is a blessing thanking G-d for the gift of sleep and asking for protection through the night. It is unique because it is said before an action you are about to do (sleep) rather than while doing it.

The blessing asks that:
• Our eyes be allowed to sleep
• We wake in the morning in peace
• We not be disturbed by bad dreams
• Our sleep refresh and heal us

Why sleep requires a blessing:
The Talmud teaches that sleep is "one-sixtieth of death" — a nightly partial departure of the soul from the body. Saying Hamapil acknowledges this transition and expresses total trust in G-d.

How to say it:
Say it after the bedtime Shema, immediately before closing your eyes.

After saying Hamapil (Rama, Shulchan Arukh O.C. 239:1): do not eat, drink, or speak until you fall asleep. Hamapil is meant to be the final spiritual anchor directly adjacent to losing consciousness — not followed by extended activity.

If an urgent need arises (a child crying, needing the restroom, a security matter), you may speak — this does not cancel the blessing and you do not need to repeat it (Biur Halachah O.C. 239:1).

What may you do while waiting to fall asleep?
• Repeating verses of Shema, or quietly murmuring familiar protective verses you already know to help yourself doze off — this is universally permitted and is not considered an interruption (Biur Halachah O.C. 239:1).
• Sitting up to deliberately begin new Torah study, or reading lengthy chapters of Tehillim after the final blessing of Hamapil — classical codifiers discourage this; the sages wanted Hamapil to lead straight into sleep, not into a study session.

If you find yourself wide awake for hours and simply cannot sleep, some poskim permit leniently relying on the view that learning Torah is allowed rather than lying idle — but this is a fallback for genuine inability to sleep, not an open standard permission from the outset.

What if you're not sure you'll fall asleep?
There are two main positions:

1. Ashkenazi ruling — always say it (Mishnah Berurah 239:6; Chazon Ish, OC 37:11):
Hamapil is a blessing of praise for G-d's creation of sleep in general, not a personal guarantee that you will fall asleep at once. Even if you suffer from insomnia, say it. As long as you lie down with the intent to sleep, the blessing is valid — any sleep you eventually get that night validates it retroactively.

2. Sephardic and Kabbalistic ruling — exercise caution (Ben Ish Chai, Year 1, Parashat Pekudei 12; Kaf HaChaim OC 239:18):
If you are genuinely uncertain you will sleep soon — due to illness, severe anxiety, or acute insomnia — do not say the full blessing with G-d's name (Shem u'Malchut), to avoid a blessing said in vain (bracha levatala). Instead, say the words as a meditative prayer while thinking G-d's name silently, or omit the closing signature (Baruch Atah...) entirely. The principle applied is: safek brachot l'hakel — when in doubt about a blessing, be lenient and omit it.
- Hamapil — blessing entrusting the soul to G-d before sleep, part of bedtime Shema
- Hamotzi
- Hamotzi is the blessing over bread — "Who brings forth bread from the earth" — beginning a meal that requires bentching. Wash netilat yadayim before bread meals. Cut bread for others after you bless if you are host. On Pesach, hamotzi is on matzah; on Shabbat and Yom Tov, lechem mishneh — two whole loaves.
- Hamotzi — Hamotzi is the blessing over bread — "Who brings forth bread from the earth" — beginning a meal that requires bentching. Wash netilat yadayim before bread meals. Cut bread for others after you bless if you are host. On Pesach, hamotzi is on matzah; on Shabbat and Yom Tov, lechem mishneh — two whole loaves.
- Havdalah
- Havdalah (separation) is the ceremony performed after Shabbat ends — Saturday night when three stars appear — marking the transition back to the weekdays.

The ceremony has four blessings: wine, fragrant spices (besamim), fire, and the Havdalah blessing (Hamavdil). The spices comfort the soul for the departure of the extra Shabbat soul (neshama yeteira).

Important: you may not perform any melachah (Shabbat-forbidden labor) after Shabbat ends until you have heard Havdalah or said the phrase "Baruch HaMavdil bein Kodesh l'chol" (Blessed is He Who separates the holy from the mundane).
- Havdalah in Kiddush
- Havdalah in Kiddush — Yaknehaz order when Shabbat leads into Yom Tov
- Havdalah separates holy time from weekday. On a normal Motzei Shabbat it uses wine, a multi-wick candle, and besamim (spices) — comforting the soul as the extra neshama yeteira departs. After Yom Tov there's usually no spices or candle. After Yom Kippur we do use a candle which was lit throughout Yom Kippur.
- Havdalah — Havdalah separates holy time from weekday. On a normal Motzei Shabbat it uses wine, a multi-wick candle, and besamim (spices) — comforting the soul as the extra neshama yeteira departs. After Yom Tov there's usually no spices or candle. After Yom Kippur we do use a candle which was lit throughout Yom Kippur.
- Have Mezuzot on your doorposts
- Have a great mitzvah idea? Share it with other users!
- Have a heart-to-heart with G-d! Take 5 minutes to talk to Him like you would a close friend 💝. Share your hopes, your worries, or just chat about your day. No formal prayers needed - just genuine conversation! The sages teach us that some angels in Heaven wait thousands of years for their turn to sing praises before G-d. And we have the stupendous gift of being able to speak to Him any time we please! 🙏
- Have children
- Hayom Yom
- Hayom Yom ("Today is…") is a Chabad calendar of brief teachings — a saying, story, or law for each day of the year. It is often studied as part of daily Chitas after Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya. Entries are short and practical, meant for busy schedules.
- Hayom Yom / daily Chitas (Chabad)
- Hayom Yom — Hayom Yom ("Today is…") is a Chabad calendar of brief teachings — a saying, story, or law for each day of the year. It is often studied as part of daily Chitas after Chumash, Tehillim, and Tanya. Entries are short and practical, meant for busy schedules.
- Heading:
- Hear the Megillah during the daytime, usually after Shacharit.
- Hear the Megillah tonight after Maariv or at your shul's reading time.
- Help others through prayer! Think of someone who needs parnassa (livelihood) and take a moment to pray for their success 🙏. Every job interview, business deal, or opportunity they need could be influenced by your caring prayer! When we support each other this way, we strengthen the bonds between all Jewish people.
- Help those in need! Got clothes you haven't worn in a while? Time for a mitzvah makeover! 👕 Set aside those items that could bring joy to someone else. The amazing thing? When you help clothe others, you're actually imitating G-d, who clothed Adam and Eve in the Garden of Eden! Plus, your closet gets organized - win-win!
- Holocaust Remembrance Day
- Holy Light Checklist
- Hong Kong
- Honor G-d's Name! 👑 The Rambam teaches us something powerful: Sanctifying G-d's Name isn't just about what you say - it's about how you live! When a Torah scholar is honest in business, kind to others, and speaks gently, people say 'Look how beautiful are the ways of Torah!' That's Kiddush Hashem! Here's your mission: Do something today that will make others think highly of G-d and His Torah. Remember: You're G-d's ambassador in this world! ✡
- Honor Shabbat! Pick out a special wine (or grape juice) for Kiddush 🍷
- Honor Torah teachers! 👨‍🏫 The Rambam teaches us something powerful: A teacher of Torah is like a parent who gives us eternal life! Did you know? The respect due to a Torah teacher is so great that if both your father and your teacher are carrying heavy loads, you help your teacher first (unless your father is also a Torah scholar)! Here's today's mission: Show appreciation to someone who has taught you Torah - whether through a message, a call, or a small gift.
- Honor holy books! Take a moment to check if your sefarim (holy books) are positioned respectfully 📚. Make sure they're right-side up and nothing non-holy is resting on top of them. Did you know? Treating holy books with respect shows our appreciation for the wisdom they contain and the One who gave it to us!
- Honor the departed properly! 🕯️ The Rambam teaches in Chapter 11 that we shouldn't attempt to communicate with the dead to ask them for information. In the book of Samuel, the king of Israel, Shaul, attempted to communicate with the departed prophet Samuel. The spirit of Samuel appeared to him and rebuked him for this transgression, and well, let's just say it was downhill from there for Shaul. This teaches us that 1. There is an afterlife, 2. We should not ask the spirits there for information, and 3. If you're trying to get advice, just call your mother—she's waiting for you to call anyway!
- Honor your father and mother
- Hoshana Raba
- Hoshana Raba is the 21st of Tishrei — the seventh and final day of Sukkot. While it is technically Chol HaMoed (an intermediate day), it carries special significance as the final "sealing" of the divine judgment begun on Rosh Hashana.

Synagogue service — customs vary by nusach:
• Ashkenaz: extended service with seven hakafot (circuits) with the lulav and aravot; willow branches (hoshanot) beaten on the ground after the circuits; lulav taken for the last time
• Sefard: similar hoshanot circuits in many communities; willow-beating customs vary — follow your siddur
• Chabad: hakafot with hoshanot; aravot may be beaten five times on the ground (not the lulav itself) — see your Chabad machzor
• Yemenite: distinct hoshanot liturgy and customs

Greeting someone with "a gutten kvittel" (a good inscription) is an Ashkenaz custom, referring to the final sealing of one's decree for the year.
- Hoshana Raba is the seventh day of Sukkot — the climax of Hoshanot with extra circuits around the bimah. Many communities beat aravot (willow branches) against the ground after the circuits. The Talmud (Sukkah 44a) treats this rite as a Yesod Nevi'im or Minhag Nevi'im — an institution of the Prophets, not merely a local custom. The number of times and exact form vary by kehilla — often about five strikes in Ashkenaz and Chabad custom. See also Hoshanot.
- Hoshana Raba — Hoshana Raba is the seventh day of Sukkot — the climax of Hoshanot with extra circuits around the bimah. Many communities beat aravot (willow branches) against the ground after the circuits. The Talmud (Sukkah 44a) treats this rite as a Yesod Nevi'im or Minhag Nevi'im — an institution of the Prophets, not merely a local custom. The number of times and exact form vary by kehilla — often about five strikes in Ashkenaz and Chabad custom. See also Hoshanot.
- Hoshana Rabbah (הושענא רבה) is the 21st of Tishrei — the seventh and final day of Sukkot. It is Chol HaMoed, but the day has its own identity and heightened teshuvah: many treat it as the final sealing of the divine judgment from Yom Kippur.

Morning — lulav and Hoshanot:
• Take the arba minim as on other Chol HaMoed days (not on Shabbat).
• In many kehillot there are extra hakafot (circuits) around the bimah with Hoshanot prayers — follow your siddur and shul.

Beating the aravot (Minhag Nevi'im):
• Separate from your daily lulav set, prepare a bundle of five fresh willow branches (aravot).
• At the conclusion of synagogue services (after Hoshanot / before or after Musaf per minhag), many communities beat these branches against the ground (Chagizat Aravah).
• The Talmud (Sukkah 44a) treats this rite as a Yesod Nevi'im or Minhag Nevi'im — an institution of the Prophets, not merely a local custom. The number of times and exact form vary by kehilla — often about five strikes in Ashkenaz and Chabad custom. Follow your siddur.
• The beaten branches are not used for the lulav mitzvah; many discard them respectfully after the rite.

Nusach notes:
• Ashkenaz: extended Hoshanot with seven hakafot; aravot beaten on the ground after circuits.
• Sephardi / Edot HaMizrach: Hoshanot customs vary by kehilla — follow your siddur.
• Chabad: hakafot with Hoshanot; aravot beaten five times on the ground (not the lulav itself) — see your Chabad machzor.

Confirm times with your shul — especially if Hoshana Rabbah is Erev Shabbat / Erev Shemini Atzeret.
- Hoshanot
- Hoshanot are prayers of salvation recited while holding the Four Species and circling the synagogue sanctuary platform (Bimah) during Sukkot (except Shabbat). Hoshana Raba (seventh day) has extra circuits. It is the last push of repentance after Yom Kippur — joy and urgency together.
- Hoshanot — Hoshanot are prayers of salvation recited while holding the Four Species and circling the synagogue sanctuary platform (Bimah) during Sukkot (except Shabbat). Hoshana Raba (seventh day) has extra circuits. It is the last push of repentance after Yom Kippur — joy and urgency together.
- Hotza'ah
- Hotza'ah is carrying from private to public domain (or four amot in public) on Shabbat. Without an eruv, keys in a pin-on pouch, strollers in some areas, and wheeling objects outdoors may be issues. An eruv symbolically encloses a community — check if yours is up this week.
- Hotza'ah — Carrying
- Hotza'ah — Hotza'ah is carrying from private to public domain (or four amot in public) on Shabbat. Without an eruv, keys in a pin-on pouch, strollers in some areas, and wheeling objects outdoors may be issues. An eruv symbolically encloses a community — check if yours is up this week.
