- If you forgot at Maariv on Rosh Chodesh:
• Still in Retzei before God's name at the conclusion — insert Yaaleh V'yavo there and continue (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 422:1; Peninei Halakha 05-01-10).
• Once you finished Retzei, or after the entire Amidah — do not go back and do not repeat. Beit Din sanctified the new month by day, not at night (Berachot 30b; Shulchan Arukh O.C. 422:1; Peninei Halakha 05-01-10). Continue davening.
- If you forgot:
• Still in Retzei (before concluding the blessing) — insert Yaaleh V'yavo in its place and continue (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 422:1; Peninei Halakha 05-01-10).
• After concluding Retzei — return to the beginning of Retzei, insert Yaaleh V'yavo, and complete the remaining blessings (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 422:1; Peninei Halakha 05-01-10).
• Finished the entire Amidah (after the final Yihiyu L'ratzon) — repeat only the Mincha Amidah (Shemoneh Esrei), never the full service, even if you already davened Musaf, Maariv, or anything else afterward (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 422:1; Peninei Halakha 05-01-10).
- If you forgot:
• Still in Retzei (before concluding the blessing) — insert Yaaleh V'yavo in its place and continue (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 422:1; Peninei Halakha 05-01-10).
• After concluding Retzei — return to the beginning of Retzei, insert Yaaleh V'yavo, and complete the remaining blessings (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 422:1; Peninei Halakha 05-01-10).
• Finished the entire Amidah (after the final Yihiyu L'ratzon) — repeat only the Shacharit Amidah (Shemoneh Esrei), never the full service, even if you already davened Musaf, Maariv, or anything else afterward (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 422:1; Peninei Halakha 05-01-10).
- If you have clothes or garments with wool in them, get them checked for shaatnez (a forbidden mixture of wool and linen) 🧵. If no need, learn something about shaatnez: The prohibition is linked to the story of Cain and Abel. Kayin, a farmer, brought produce (symbolized by linen) as an offering to G-d, and Hevel, a shepherd, offered animals (symbolized by wool). When G-d favored Abel's offering, it led to jealousy and conflict. Shaatnez teaches us to respect differences rather than fight them. This separation is only resolved in the Temple service, where wool and linen coexist in the priestly garments, symbolizing ultimate unity under divine purpose 👔.
- If you miss a prayer service (tefillah), there is a rabbinic mechanism to partially make it up — called tashlumin (תַּשְׁלוּמִין — "completions"). The rules are strict and the window is narrow.

Source: Talmud Bavli, Berachot 26a. Codified in Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 108:1:
"If one erred or was prevented from praying a service, they may make it up by praying twice during the service that immediately follows it."

The Core Rule — Immediate Succession Only:
The makeup can only happen at the very next scheduled prayer. Once that window closes, the opportunity is permanently gone (Mishnah Berurah 108:3 — "Avar zmano, batel korbano" — its time has expired, the obligation is cancelled).

Missed Prayer → When to do Tashlumin:
• Shacharit (morning) → at Mincha. Cannot be made up at Maariv.
• Mincha (afternoon) → at Maariv. Cannot be made up the next morning.
• Maariv (evening) → at the next morning's Shacharit. Cannot be made up at Mincha.

How to do it (order is mandatory — SA OC 108:1):
1. First: pray the Amidah of the current service (the prayer whose time it actually is now).
2. Pause briefly (enough time to walk four cubits).
3. Second: pray the Amidah again as the tashlumin for the missed prayer.

Penalty for reversing the order: If you accidentally say the makeup first intending it as the makeup, halacha automatically applies that first Amidah to your current obligation instead (Mishnah Berurah 108:2). The second Amidah you pray then immediately counts as your valid makeup. You do not need to pray a third time.

If you missed the window entirely — Tefilat Nedavah:
If Shacharit was missed, Mincha was also missed, and it is now Maariv — the formal tashlumin is gone forever. However, you may pray a second voluntary Amidah as a gift to G-d (Tefilat Nedavah — prayer of donation), subject to two conditions (SA OC 107:1):
• Make a mental stipulation: "If I am permitted to pray a voluntary prayer right now, let this be one. If not, let it not count for anything."
• Include a small personal request or textual innovation not in the standard Amidah, to distinguish it as a genuinely spontaneous gift rather than a mistaken obligation.

Additional notes:
• Tashlumin applies only when the prayer was missed unintentionally (forgetfulness or unavoidable circumstances). Deliberate skipping has no makeup.
• If you missed Shacharit on Shabbat, Rosh Chodesh, or Yom Tov, make it up at Mincha — not at Musaf. Musaf is a separate holiday Amidah, not a standard daily boundary prayer. Pray Mincha first, pause, then a second Amidah as tashlumin (SA O.C. 108:9).
• When in doubt about any situation, ask your rabbi.
- If you missed it, ask your rabbi — some fulfill later the same night with guidance; morning Shema is a separate obligation.
- If you missed tonight's reading, the Friday morning reading is still required.
- If you recite the Maariv Amidah on Rosh Chodesh, add Yaaleh V'yavo in the blessing Retzei (Avodah).

- Immediately after saying Modeh Ani, wash your hands in a specific ritual way before doing anything else.

What it is:
Netilat Yadayim (נְטִילַת יָדַיִם — literally "lifting of the hands") is a ritual washing — not just hygiene, but a spiritual act. During deep sleep, the soul partially ascends and a spiritual impurity called Ruach Ra'ah (evil spirit) settles on the fingers and nails. This washing removes it and prepares us for prayer, Torah, and the day ahead.

Different minhagim disagree whether morning washing is mainly to remove ruach ra'ah upon waking or mainly as preparation for prayer — so when you say Al Netilat Yadayim differs between Ashkenaz, Sephardi, Edot HaMizrach, and Chabad.

How to pour (all traditions):
1. Ideally have a cup, bottle, or washcup filled with water and a basin ready at your bedside before you sleep
2. Pour over your right hand, then left, alternating three times: right, left, right, left, right, left
3. Dry your hands when your minhag calls for it (see blessing timing below)

Blessing timing overview (set nusach in Settings for your full text):
• Ashkenaz: usually no blessing at bedside; blessing after second washing (bathroom + dress), unless you had no bathroom need.
• Sephardi / many Edot HaMizrach: blessing after washing, before drying (Shulchan Aruch); bathroom first only if urgent.
• Some Edot HaMizrach kehillot (e.g. Baladi Yemenite): wash after bathroom — follow your rav.
• Chabad: no blessing on first washing; blessing only after second washing at the sink.

The blessing text:
"Baruch Atah Ado-nai Eloheinu Melech ha'olam, asher kid'shanu b'mitzvotav v'tzivanu al netilat yadayim"

The 4 amot rule:
Before washing, do not walk 4 amot (about 6–8 feet) continuously without stopping — this is considered a full "journey" that allows the impurity to expand. If the sink is farther than 4 amot away:
• Strict / Kabbalistic approach (Zohar, Shulchan Aruch HaRav): walk in segments shorter than 4 amot, pause fully between each, and repeat until you reach the sink
• Mainstream approach (Mishnah Berurah): walking directly to the sink to remove the impurity quickly is also permitted, though the stop-and-go method is praiseworthy
• Lenient approach (Aruch HaShulchan): the entire modern house is considered one domain; walk normally to the sink
Follow your rabbi's guidance.

If you have an urgent need to use the bathroom: go to the restroom first — you may not recite blessings or other holy words while needing to relieve oneself (guf naki / ba'al teshaktsu). Nikuy does not bypass this rule.

If your hands are dirty but you are in a clean state of body (no urgent bathroom need) and no water is nearby:
Rub your hands firmly on a clean, dry surface — a wooden headboard, wall, or clothing. This is called Nikuy (ניקוי — cleaning by friction) and permits saying holy words when water is unavailable, but does NOT remove ruach ra'ah. You must still wash with water as soon as possible (Shulchan Aruch O.C. 4:22; Mishnah Berurah 4:61).

After naps (Modeh Ani is not recited):
When waking from a daytime nap, Modeh Ani is typically omitted — wash your hands (negel vasser) per the rules below:
• Nap under ~30 minutes: the severe Ruach Ra'ah does not apply; wash for cleanliness before prayer but no bracha is required and the 4-amot rule does not apply (Mishnah Berurah 4:34)
• Long daytime nap: mainstream opinion — wash three times alternating but no bracha; strict opinion — treat as morning washing. Ask your rabbi

Other important notes:
• Do not touch your eyes, mouth, or face before washing
• Do not touch food before washing
• After using the bathroom: wash your hands with water so they are clean before reciting Asher Yatzar — no blessing is required (Shulchan Aruch O.C. 4:18). Some Ashkenazi communities or individuals pour three times in alternation as a personal stringency, but that is not the basic halachic requirement.
• After leaving a cemetery: many wash three times in alternating sequence (right-left-right-left-right-left) without a blessing — a minhag against ruach ra'ah (details vary by community).
• Before eating bread (a kezayit or more): wash differently — pour consecutively on each entire hand up to the wrist (usually twice or three times on the right hand, then twice or three times on the left — not alternating like negel vasser), then recite Al Netilat Yadayim and Hamotzi.
• Once you have said Al Netilat Yadayim in the morning, all traditions agree you do not repeat the blessing when you wash again at shul before davening
- Immerse food vessels in mikveh
- Important Lifestyle Mitzvot
- Important Note About Electronics
- Important points about Tefillin:
- In a Jewish leap year, 14 Adar I is Purim Katan ("little Purim"). The full Purim mitzvot — Megillah, mishloach manot, matanot l'evyonim, and the festive meal — apply only on regular Purim in Adar II (14 Adar, or 15 Adar in walled cities).

Purim Katan is a minor festive day: Tachanun is omitted. Some communities add a slightly more festive meal or learning; it is not a substitute for Purim observance.
- In a leap year, 15 Adar I is Shushan Purim Katan — the "walled-city" counterpart to Purim Katan, parallel to Shushan Purim on 15 Adar.

Like Purim Katan, it is a minor day: Tachanun is omitted. The full Purim mitzvot are observed only on regular Purim in Adar II.
- Info
- Intentionally dismantling or breaking down any stable structure. Dismantling a large outdoor shade canopy or taking down a multi-panel tent can violate Soter. Unscrewing a door handle completely or forcefully separating tightly locked modular furniture falls under this restriction.
- Intentionally marking, etching, or scratching guide lines on a surface for writing or cutting. Drawing a preliminary guide line across a cake before cutting, or pressing a decorative border into dough to mark cuts, is restricted. Slicing directly through without a prior guide line is fine.
- Interlacing two sets of threads at right angles to form fabric. The Talmud states that weaving even two parallel horizontal threads into a vertical grid is sufficient to violate this prohibition. Certain types of dense embroidery, cross-stitching, and basketry share the same structural mechanics.

- Israeli Fallen Soldiers Memorial Day
- Israeli Independence Day — customs vary by community
- It is a mitzvah to have a festive meal during the day in honor of Rosh Chodesh.

- Jerusalem Day — customs vary by community

- Jewish community or congregation
- Jewish law applies in the marketplace and office just as in the synagogue. Honest weights, fair deals, and trustworthy conduct are Torah obligations — not optional "extra credit."

What this includes:
• Geneivat da'at (גְּנֵבַת דַּעַת — deception): misleading a customer or employer about price, quality, terms, or your qualifications.
• Ona'ah (אוֹנָאָה): overcharging or underpaying when the other party does not know the fair value. Classic rule (Bava Metzia 49b): when price differs from fair market value by one-sixth or more, Torah laws of ona'ah apply. At exactly one-sixth, return the excess; beyond one-sixth, the wronged party may rescind the entire sale.
• Keeping your word: honoring agreements, deadlines, and commitments; not making promises you cannot keep.
• Paying workers on time: wages owed must be paid when due (Leviticus 19:13).
• Not stealing in any form — including time theft, expense fraud, or taking what is not yours.
• Taxes and debts: fulfilling legal obligations honestly; not cheating the government or private parties.

Key idea:
"In righteousness you shall judge your fellow" (Leviticus 19:15) — business dealings are judged by the same moral standard as ritual mitzvot. A Jew's integrity (yosher — יֹשֶׁר) is a sanctification of G-d's name in the world.

How to practice:
• Be transparent in sales, billing, and negotiations.
• When in doubt, ask a rabbi or trusted advisor about a specific contract or industry practice.
• Treat employees, clients, and employers with the dignity the Torah demands.

Check this off as a daily intention to act with honesty in all financial and professional interactions.
- Jewish men cover their heads throughout the entire day as a sign that G-d is above us.

What it is:
A kippah (כִּפָּה — also called a yarmulke, from the Yiddish) is a small head covering — a skullcap worn on top of the head. The word kippah means "dome" or "vault," and the word yarmulke comes from an Aramaic phrase meaning "awe of the King."

Why we wear it:
The Talmud records that covering the head cultivates yirat Shamayim — literally "fear of Heaven," which in Jewish thought means a constant awareness that G-d is present and that our actions matter. Wearing a kippah is a visible, ongoing reminder of that awareness.

Practical notes:
• Wear it all day, every day — not just during prayer or Torah study
• There is no need to wear a kippah while bathing or swimming
• Any size, color, or style is acceptable; follow your community's custom
• Many men wear a hat or a larger covering over the kippah during prayer as an added sign of respect

For women:
Married women are obligated in hair covering — see the modesty section.
- Jewish men wear a kippah (skullcap) as a sign of respect and acknowledgment that G-d is above us. 
            
            While some authorities consider it a custom rather than a strict requirement, wearing a kippah is universally practiced by observant Jewish men and boys.
            
            It serves as a constant reminder of G-d's presence and our duty to live according to His will.
- Jews following Sephardi rites and customs
- Joining two separate pieces of flexible material into a unified entity using a connecting element (thread, glue, staples). Using liquid glue to paste pages together or applying duct tape to bind two surfaces falls under this category. In a fashion emergency, a temporary safety pin used casually is permitted.
- July
- June
- K'dei achilat pras is the halachic time limit to consume a specific volume of food so it counts as one act of eating — about 4–7 minutes per many poskim. It applies to eating a kezayit of matzah at the Seder, maror, and eating enough bread or cake within that window to require an after-blessing (Birkat Hamazon or al hamichya). If you ate a kezayit of bread within k'dei achilat pras, Birkat Hamazon covers the whole meal.
- K'fi daato means training a child according to what they can understand and do reliably — not demanding adult perfection. Chinuch grows mitzvot stepwise: brachot before full Amidah, short Shabbat attendance before long services. Pressure without da'at backfires; joy in mitzvot lasts longer.
- Kabbalah
- Kabbalah is the Jewish mystical tradition exploring how G-d relates to creation, the soul, and mitzvot. Texts include the Zohar and later works like Tanya. Authentic Kabbalah is studied with Torah literacy and a teacher; it is not fortune-telling or magic.
- Kabbalah — Kabbalah is the Jewish mystical tradition exploring how G-d relates to creation, the soul, and mitzvot. Texts include the Zohar and later works like Tanya. Authentic Kabbalah is studied with Torah literacy and a teacher; it is not fortune-telling or magic.
- Kaddish
- Kaddish sanctifies G-d's Name in Aramaic — exalting G-d publicly. Mourners recite Kaddish for eleven months for parents and thirty days for other close relatives. It requires a minyan. There are half-Kaddish, whole-Kaddish, and rabbinic Kaddish placements in the service. Saying Kaddish connects the living to the deceased's merit.
- Kaddish — Kaddish sanctifies G-d's Name in Aramaic — exalting G-d publicly. Mourners recite Kaddish for eleven months for parents and thirty days for other close relatives. It requires a minyan. There are half-Kaddish, whole-Kaddish, and rabbinic Kaddish placements in the service. Saying Kaddish connects the living to the deceased's merit.
- Kallah means bride; kallah classes teach taharat hamishpacha, mitzvot of the home, and wedding laws before marriage. The kallah and chatan prepare for chuppah and building a Jewish family.
- Kaparot is a pre-Yom Kippur custom swinging a chicken (or money substituted) over one's head while reciting verses — charity replaces the chicken in many communities today. It is custom, not Torah law. Handle with sensitivity; follow local rabbinic guidance and kashrut if using chickens.
- Karpas is a vegetable (often parsley or potato) dipped in salt water at the Seder start — awakening curiosity before the meal. The salt water recalls tears of slavery. It is not the main maror mitzvah; it opens the night with a question. Prepare enough for every guest.
- Kashering
- Kashering makes utensils fit for kosher or Pesach use. The core principle is kebolo kach polto — as a vessel absorbed forbidden taste, so it expels it. Items used in boiling water often need hag'alah (immersion in rolling boiling water); items used directly on fire may need libun (intense heat until they turn red hot). Plastic and glass have highly conflicting rulings between Ashkenazim and Sephardim — and it's less lenient on Pesach. Ask your rav. Ceramic cannot be kashered. Many families keep a separate Pesach set.
- Kashering — Kashering makes utensils fit for kosher or Pesach use. The core principle is kebolo kach polto — as a vessel absorbed forbidden taste, so it expels it. Items used in boiling water often need hag'alah (immersion in rolling boiling water); items used directly on fire may need libun (intense heat until they turn red hot). Plastic and glass have highly conflicting rulings between Ashkenazim and Sephardim — and it's less lenient on Pesach. Ask your rav. Ceramic cannot be kashered. Many families keep a separate Pesach set.
- Kashrut
- Kashrut Timer
- Kashrut is the Torah system of permitted food, shechita, separating meat and dairy, and supervising production. It sanctifies eating — turning meals into service of G-d. Learning labels (OU, OK, etc.), hechsherim, and kitchen setup takes time; ask your rav when starting.
- Kashrut is the system of Jewish dietary laws that governs what we eat, how food is prepared, and how meat and dairy are kept separate.

What kosher means:
Kosher (כָּשֵׁר) literally means "fit" or "proper." Food that meets all the Torah's dietary requirements is called kosher. Food that does not is called treif (טְרֵיף — literally "torn," referring to torn-apart animals).

The main rules:

Permitted and forbidden animals:
• Land animals must have split hooves AND chew their cud: beef, lamb, and goat are kosher; pork is not
• Fish must have fins AND scales: salmon, tuna, and carp are kosher; shrimp, lobster, and catfish are not
• Chicken, turkey, duck, and other domestic fowl are kosher

Shechita (kosher slaughter):
• All meat must be slaughtered by a shochet — a specially trained and G-d-fearing slaughterer — using a swift, painless cut. This is called shechita (שְׁחִיטָה)
• After slaughter, the meat is salted to remove blood (melicha), since consuming blood is forbidden

Separating meat and dairy (basar b'chalav):
• Meat and dairy may never be eaten together, cooked together, or served on the same dishes
• After eating meat, you wait a period of time before dairy. Six hours is standard in many communities, but customs vary (some wait one or three hours) — follow your rabbi and community
• Separate sets of dishes, pots, and utensils are kept for meat and dairy

Hechsher:
• A hechsher (הֶכְשֵׁר) is a certification mark on packaged foods indicating rabbinical supervision. Look for a recognized symbol on labels.

Why these laws:
The Torah gives these laws as divine commands. Beyond the reason, observing them elevates every meal into an act of service to G-d and builds discipline and consciousness in our daily lives.

Getting started:
Consult your local Orthodox rabbi before setting up a kosher kitchen. Many people begin by removing non-kosher meat and seafood, then work toward full observance.
- Kashrut timer active — tap to view
- Kashrut — Kashrut is the Torah system of permitted food, shechita, separating meat and dairy, and supervising production. It sanctifies eating — turning meals into service of G-d. Learning labels (OU, OK, etc.), hechsherim, and kitchen setup takes time; ask your rav when starting.
- Kavannah is focused intention — knowing what mitzvah you are doing and for Whom. Prayer without kavannah is still prayer, but the sages urge preparation: understand words, stand with respect, remove distractions. Candle lighting and Amidah are especially suited to personal requests once the formal text is said.
- Keep Kosher
- Keep Kosher (Kashrut)
- Keep going! Every mitzvah counts!
- Keep the Temple courtyard holy! 🌳 The Rambam teaches in Chapter 6 that we can't plant trees in the Temple courtyard, even for beauty! Why? Because idol worshippers would plant trees near their altars. Here's something fascinating: Even something that seems nice, like planting trees, becomes forbidden if it resembles idolatrous practices. Today's mission: Remember that serving G-d must be done His way, not our way. Yes, even if it seems nice!
- Keeping kosher involves following Jewish dietary laws:
- Keli
- Keli — A keli is a vessel, utensil, or tool. It generally refers to any manufactured object or receptacle that holds, processes, or contains something. A keli is used for netilat yadayim — a cup, bottle, or washing cup (usually) with two handles.
- Ketuvim
- Ketuvim (Writings) is the third section of Tanach — Psalms (Tehillim), Proverbs, Esther, Ruth, Daniel, and others. It includes poetry, history, and wisdom literature. Tehillim is the most commonly recited book for comfort and praise.
- Ketuvim — Ketuvim (Writings) is the third section of Tanach — Psalms (Tehillim), Proverbs, Esther, Ruth, Daniel, and others. It includes poetry, history, and wisdom literature. Tehillim is the most commonly recited book for comfort and praise.
- Key Differences
- Key Terms
- Kibud Av V'Eim - mitzvah to honor/respect and care for parents
- Kiddush
- Kiddush Hashem
- Kiddush Hashem means sanctifying G-d's Name — living and speaking in a way that makes observers respect Torah and G-d. Examples include returning lost objects, honest dealings, and gracious behavior in public as a visibly Jewish person. Its opposite, chilul Hashem, is among the most serious sins because it pushes people away from holiness.
- Kiddush Hashem — Kiddush Hashem means sanctifying G-d's Name — living and speaking in a way that makes observers respect Torah and G-d. Examples include returning lost objects, honest dealings, and gracious behavior in public as a visibly Jewish person. Its opposite, chilul Hashem, is among the most serious sins because it pushes people away from holiness.
- Kiddush Levana
- Kiddush Levana (Sanctification of the New Moon) — Birkat HaLevanah. Men are obligated in this time-bound positive mitzvah; women are exempt and the widespread custom is that women do not recite it at all (see Deracheha link below).

${'$'}waitLine

Deadline: The window ends at or before the full moon. Many authorities calculate this as approximately half the mean lunar month from the molad — often cited as roughly 14 days, 18 hours, and 22 minutes (~14.75 days) — though poskim disagree whether the cutoff follows that calculation or when the moon is visibly waning. Saying it on the night of the 15th may already be too late depending on the month and opinion. This app uses the Hebrew calendar day as a rough guide only — always check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location before the month ends.

When:
• After nightfall (tzeit), standing outdoors under the open sky.
• Ideally on Motzei Shabbat while still in nice clothes — a widespread custom because you are already dressed up.
• In Av, many wait until after Tisha B'Av; in Tishrei, many wait until after Yom Kippur — but say sooner on Motzei Shabbat if waiting risks cloudy nights.
• Not on Shabbat or Yom Tov itself.

How:
• The moon must be clearly visible — if clouds block it, wait for a clear night within the window.
• Use your siddur; it praises G-d for creation — not worship of the moon.

Window closes at or before the full moon (approximately — opinions vary; often ~14.75 days from the molad). Check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location.
- Kiddush Levana (קִידּוּשׁ לְבָנָה — Sanctification of the Moon) is a monthly blessing said once each Hebrew month when the new moon is visible at night. It is not worship of the moon — it praises G-d for creation's cycles and Israel's renewal. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 42a) compares one who blesses the moon properly to greeting the Shechinah. Men are obligated in this time-bound positive mitzvah; women are exempt (like most time-bound mitzvot) and the widespread longstanding custom is that women do not recite Kiddush Levana at all — prominent authorities (Shelah, Magen Avraham O.C. 426:1) advise against it for kabbalistic reasons as well. Say it outdoors under the open sky when possible, standing, after nightfall. Ashkenazim and Chabad often wait at least three days after the new moon; most Sephardim wait seven (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 426:4); Moroccan and some North African kehillot may begin after three days (Peninei Halakha 05-01-18). Motzei Shabbat is a widespread preference when people are dressed up and the moon is visible — but if waiting risks a string of cloudy nights and missing the monthly window, say it on the first clear weeknight the mitzvah becomes possible. The window ends at or before the full moon — often calculated as approximately half the mean lunar month from the molad (~14.75 days), though poskim disagree on the exact cutoff; saying it on the night of the 15th may already be too late; check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location. Also called Birkat HaLevanah.
- Kiddush Levana — Kiddush Levana (קִידּוּשׁ לְבָנָה — Sanctification of the Moon) is a monthly blessing said once each Hebrew month when the new moon is visible at night. It is not worship of the moon — it praises G-d for creation's cycles and Israel's renewal. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 42a) compares one who blesses the moon properly to greeting the Shechinah. Men are obligated in this time-bound positive mitzvah; women are exempt (like most time-bound mitzvot) and the widespread longstanding custom is that women do not recite Kiddush Levana at all — prominent authorities (Shelah, Magen Avraham O.C. 426:1) advise against it for kabbalistic reasons as well. Say it outdoors under the open sky when possible, standing, after nightfall. Ashkenazim and Chabad often wait at least three days after the new moon; most Sephardim wait seven (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 426:4); Moroccan and some North African kehillot may begin after three days (Peninei Halakha 05-01-18). Motzei Shabbat is a widespread preference when people are dressed up and the moon is visible — but if waiting risks a string of cloudy nights and missing the monthly window, say it on the first clear weeknight the mitzvah becomes possible. The window ends at or before the full moon — often calculated as approximately half the mean lunar month from the molad (~14.75 days), though poskim disagree on the exact cutoff; saying it on the night of the 15th may already be too late; check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location. Also called Birkat HaLevanah.
- Kiddush b'Makom Seudah
- Kiddush b'Makom Seudah means sanctifying Shabbat or Yom Tov, typically with blessings over wine, in the same place you eat a meal afterwards. You would not fulfill Kiddush if you recited it and left without eating there. On Shabbat night, the meal should include bread (hamotzi) per most poskim. On Yom Tov or daytime Kiddush, other foods may qualify in some cases; ask your rav. If you make Kiddush in one room, the meal should follow there and not in a different space.
- Kiddush b'Makom Seudah — Kiddush b'Makom Seudah means sanctifying Shabbat or Yom Tov, typically with blessings over wine, in the same place you eat a meal afterwards. You would not fulfill Kiddush if you recited it and left without eating there. On Shabbat night, the meal should include bread (hamotzi) per most poskim. On Yom Tov or daytime Kiddush, other foods may qualify in some cases; ask your rav. If you make Kiddush in one room, the meal should follow there and not in a different space.
- Kiddush is the Torah-level commandment to "remember the Shabbat day to sanctify it" (Exodus 20:8), established by the Sages through a declaration over a cup of wine.

Key rules:
• Kiddush must be recited in the same place where you will eat your meal (Kiddush b'Makom Seudah).
• The cup must hold at least a revi'it (רְבִיעִית — approximately 86ml / ~3 fl oz). The minimum amount to actually drink is maleh lugmov (מְלֹא לֻגְמָיו — a cheekful, approximately 60ml / ~2 fl oz). Ideally, drink the majority of the cup. Exact measurements are debated; consult your rabbi.
• Women are equally obligated in Kiddush.
• Grape juice is a fully valid substitute for wine.
• On Friday night, if wine is unavailable, challah (bread) may substitute.
• On Shabbat morning, if wine is unavailable, beer or whiskey (chamar medinah — a locally significant beverage) may be used. Wine is always preferred. Consult your rabbi for specifics.
• Unlike Friday night, bread can never substitute for Kiddush on Shabbat morning — saying Hamotzi without wine or chamar medina means you have skipped daytime Kiddush entirely.
- Kiddush sanctifies Shabbat or Yom Tov over wine (or grape juice, or possibly bread/other drinks if in need; ask a rav).
- Kiddush — Kiddush sanctifies Shabbat or Yom Tov over wine (or grape juice, or possibly bread/other drinks if in need; ask a rav).
- Kindling a flame
- Kinot
- Kinot — elegies read on Tisha B'Av mourning the Temple
- Kippah (Head covering for men)
- Kisui Rosh (Hair covering for married women)
- Kitniyot are legumes, rice, corn, and similar foods that Ashkenazim traditionally avoid on Pesach though they are not Torah chametz — an Ashkenazi customary stringency, not a Torah prohibition. They are halachically permitted for Sephardim (though some communities have local customs avoiding specific items). The custom began to prevent confusion with chametz grains. Peanut oil, quinoa, and similar items follow widely different psak — follow your rav, not social media debates.
- Kitzur Shulchan Arukh
- Kitzur Shulchan Arukh — The Kitzur Shulchan Arukh is a condensed summary of daily halacha — prayer, Shabbat, kashrut, festivals, and family life — written by Rabbi Shlomo Ganzfried. It is a practical first halacha book for beginners and a quick reference for experienced Jews.
- Kol Chamira
- Kol Chamira is an Aramaic declaration nullifying chametz still in your possession. The night version (after bedikat chametz) nullifies only chametz you have not seen and do not know about — you may still legally own chametz for breakfast. The morning version (after biur on Erev Pesach) is structurally different and nullifies ALL chametz, whether seen or unseen, destroyed or not.
- Kol Chamira — Kol Chamira is an Aramaic declaration nullifying chametz still in your possession. The night version (after bedikat chametz) nullifies only chametz you have not seen and do not know about — you may still legally own chametz for breakfast. The morning version (after biur on Erev Pesach) is structurally different and nullifies ALL chametz, whether seen or unseen, destroyed or not.
- Kol Nidrei
- Kol Nidrei opens Yom Kippur eve — annulling vows so the day can proceed with a clean slate regarding promises to G-d. The haunting melody is famous, but the legal text is serious. It does not cancel interpersonal debts or promises to others — only certain vows to G-d per specific formulas. Arrive early; the shul fills quickly.
- Kol Nidrei — Kol Nidrei opens Yom Kippur eve — annulling vows so the day can proceed with a clean slate regarding promises to G-d. The haunting melody is famous, but the legal text is serious. It does not cancel interpersonal debts or promises to others — only certain vows to G-d per specific formulas. Arrive early; the shul fills quickly.
- Korbanot
- Korbanot & preliminaries (Nusach Ari)
- Korbanot are the Temple offerings — animal sacrifices, incense, and libations — brought by the Jewish people in the Beit HaMikdash. Since the Temple's destruction, we cannot offer them; prayer recalls and substitutes for those offerings until the Temple is rebuilt.
- Korbanot before Mincha
- Korbanot before Mincha (Edot HaMizrach)
- Korbanot before Mincha (Sephardi)
- Korbanot before Pesukei Dezimra (Ashkenaz)
- Korbanot — Korbanot are the Temple offerings — animal sacrifices, incense, and libations — brought by the Jewish people in the Beit HaMikdash. Since the Temple's destruction, we cannot offer them; prayer recalls and substitutes for those offerings until the Temple is rebuilt.
- Koreah — Tearing
- Korech
- Korech is the "Hillel sandwich" at the Seder — matzah and maror together, recalling Temple-era practice. You eat a kezayit of matzah and maror combined after dipping maror in charoset. It is one of the Seder's core tastes: slavery's bitterness held with redemption's bread. Follow your Haggadah's order after the main matzah mitzvah.
- Korech — Korech is the "Hillel sandwich" at the Seder — matzah and maror together, recalling Temple-era practice. You eat a kezayit of matzah and maror combined after dipping maror in charoset. It is one of the Seder's core tastes: slavery's bitterness held with redemption's bread. Follow your Haggadah's order after the main matzah mitzvah.
- Koshair — Tying
- Kosher means fit for use per halacha — especially food, but also valid scrolls and acceptable conduct (e.g. kosher money for mitzvot). Kosher is not a health label; it is spiritual fitness. When in doubt on a product, call the hechsher company or your rav.
- Koteiv
- Koteiv is writing two meaningful letters on Shabbat. Typing, texting, and permanent markers violate this rabbinically and often biblically. Avoid Scrabble with locking tiles, signing checks, and filling forms. Children should have Shabbat-appropriate activities prepared.
- Koteiv — Koteiv is writing two meaningful letters on Shabbat. Typing, texting, and permanent markers violate this rabbinically and often biblically. Avoid Scrabble with locking tiles, signing checks, and filling forms. Children should have Shabbat-appropriate activities prepared.
- Koteiv — Writing
- Kotzer — Reaping
- Krias Shema
- Krias Shema — recitation of the Shema and its blessings
- Kriat Shema al HaMitah (Bedtime Shema)
- L'Dovid (Psalm 27)
- L'chatchila means the ideal way to perform a mitzvah from the outset — what you should plan to do. Bedieved is after the fact, when something went wrong and halacha may offer a corrective or leniency. Learning both terms helps you read halachic guides: "l'chatchila use a cup; bedieved if you forgot, some allow…"
- Lag BaOmer
- Lag BaOmer falls on the 18th day of the Hebrew month of Iyar. The name itself is a shorthand for its date: the Hebrew letters Lamed (ל) and Gimmel (ג) have a combined numerical value of 33, meaning the holiday takes place on the 33rd day of the Omer — the 49-day countdown connecting Passover to Shavuot.

While Passover and Shavuot are highly structured biblical festivals, Lag BaOmer is a fiery, mystical, grassroots celebration. It functions as a sudden, 24-hour pause button on a multi-week period of semi-mourning.

The two historic pillars of the day
Lag BaOmer is anchored by two entirely different historical events that occurred centuries apart, combining a devastating tragedy with profound spiritual revelation.

• The pause of the plague (Rabbi Akiva's students) — The weeks counting the Omer are traditionally a solemn time. The Talmud records that during the 2nd century CE, a catastrophic plague swept through the 24,000 students of the great sage Rabbi Akiva, killing them all. The sages teach that this tragedy occurred because "they did not show proper respect to one another." On the 33rd day of the count, the plague miraculously stopped. Because the dying ceased, Lag BaOmer became a celebration of survival and a reminder of the vital importance of radical interpersonal respect.

• The passing of Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai — The second pillar centers on Rabbi Akiva's greatest surviving student: Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai (known as the Rashbi), who went on to become the premier master of Jewish mysticism and the traditional author of the Zohar (the foundational text of Kabbalah). Rabbi Shimon passed away on the 33rd day of the Omer. Before his death, he explicitly commanded his students to celebrate his passing not with weeping, but as a day of joy (Yom Hillula), because his death marked the moment his soul achieved perfect unity with God and his hidden spiritual teachings were fully revealed to the world.

Why the fire? Traditions and observance
Lag BaOmer is famous for its distinct, high-energy customs, each deeply tied to its historical themes:

• Bonfires — Massive fires are lit all over Israel and Jewish communities worldwide. The fire serves a dual purpose: it symbolizes the immense "spiritual light" that Rabbi Shimon brought into the world through the Zohar, and it recalls the light of a miraculous fire that columned over his bed on the day he passed.

• Lifting the mourning restrictions — Because the plague paused, all the traditional mourning practices observed during the Omer are suspended for the day. People blast music, dance around the bonfires, and schedule weddings that were delayed for weeks. (Whether your community ends all Omer mourning at Lag BaOmer or only on Shavuot varies by minhag — follow your community.)

• The first haircut (upsherin) — It is a widespread tradition to hold off on cutting a boy's hair until he turns three years old. On Lag BaOmer, thousands of families travel to Meron (the burial site of Rabbi Shimon in northern Israel) to give their three-year-old boys their very first haircuts, a ceremony called an upsherin.

• Bows and arrows — Children traditionally play with toy bows and arrows on this day. This recalls the midrashic tradition that no rainbow appeared in the sky during Rabbi Shimon's entire lifetime; his personal spiritual merit was so immense that the world never required a physical reminder of God's covenant not to destroy the earth.

Liturgical note: Tachanun is omitted. Work is permitted; it is not Yom Tov.
- Lag BaOmer — 33rd day of the Omer; mourning eased in many communities; Chabad keeps haircut restrictions until Shavuot except for a 3 year old boy's first haircut
- Language
- Large
- Larger text
- Lashon hara is speaking negatively about another Jew even when the information is true and even in casual conversation, unless there's a real useful purpose like protecting someone from a harmful situation. The Torah compares it to murder because it destroys reputations, friendships, and community trust. The Chofetz Chaim systematized its laws: many situations permit speech for protection or constructive need, but for entertainment or no reason it's never permitted. Guarding speech is one of the fastest ways to grow in holiness.
- Last chance may be tonight — say if the moon is clear and still within Sof Zman.
- Last chance this month — $preferred
- Last chance this month — {timing}
- Latest time for morning Shema: {time}
- Learn a fascinating halachic debate about whether raisins need to be checked for bugs! 🍇 The Torah states in Leviticus 11:41, 'And every swarming thing that swarms upon the earth is a detestable thing; it shall not be eaten.' Chazal and the Rishonim clarify that this specific phrasing—'upon the earth'—suggests that an insect generated inside a fruit might be treated differently if it never actually swarms on the ground. ⚖️ The core of the 'machloket' is this: Detached Produce: If an insect develops in fruit after it has been harvested (detached), many authorities rule it is permitted as long as it never leaves the fruit. However, the moment it exits—even if it just moves to the surface and never touches the ground—it becomes forbidden, and crawling back inside does not change that! 🚫 Attached Produce: If an insect develops while the fruit is still attached to the tree or vine, the law is much stricter. Many major authorities, including the Rambam, rule that these insects are forbidden even if they never leave the fruit, because they are already considered to have 'swarmed' while the plant was part of the earth. Because raisins are dried grapes, some rely on a leniency associated with the 'Taz,' suggesting that any potential insects likely developed after the grapes were detached and never emerged. On the flip side, many are strict because insects could have developed while the grapes were still on the vine, or they might have crawled out during the drying process. 🧺 This is why you will see such a range of practice, from those who snack freely, to those who only use raisins with the highest level of 'checked' certification! Fascinating! Learn more about the important mitzvah of checking fruits and veggies!
- Learn about 'avak lashon hara' — the 'dust of negative speech' — and the specific patterns the Chofetz Chaim identifies as violations! 🌫️ Even when speech isn't explicitly negative, the Chofetz Chaim forbids several 'borderline' patterns: (1) 'He turned out okay' — damning with faint praise. 'Well, I'm surprised he actually delivered the project on time' clearly implies you expected him to fail. (2) 'Oh, him? Don't ask me about him!' — pretending to hold back while making the listener think there's something terrible to say. (3) Mentioning context that makes someone look bad indirectly: 'You went to Shimon's house? You know where he lives — the neighborhood with all the... well.' (4) Praising someone in front of their enemies — knowing that the enemy will respond by saying bad things about them. You triggered the lashon hara even though you technically said something positive. (5) Talking about someone's embarrassing past after they've repented and changed — bringing up their old behavior is avak lashon hara even if technically true. The underlying principle: even speech that isn't explicitly negative can still damage someone's reputation, destroy relationships, or trigger further gossip. The Torah's standard is not just avoiding lies — it's avoiding damage through words, even indirect damage.
- Learn about Birkat Kohanim — the priestly blessing — and the dramatic duchan ceremony that makes it so powerful! 🙌 The Torah gives the Kohanim a specific three-verse blessing to bestow on Israel (Numbers 6:24-26). In the Ashkenazic tradition, Birkat Kohanim is generally performed only on Yom Tov in regular synagogues outside Israel. In Israel, and among Sephardim everywhere, it is performed every single day at Shacharit. The ceremony: the Kohanim (descendants of Aaron the High Priest) remove their shoes, wash their hands (washed by Levites in many communities — an echo of the Temple service!), and ascend the 'duchan' (raised platform). They cover themselves with their tallits, spread their fingers in the special pattern, and sing the three verses while the congregation faces them with eyes closed. The Talmud (Sotah 39a) says: while the Kohanim bless Israel, the congregation should not look at the Kohanim's hands, because the Divine Presence rests upon them. The fingers form specific shapes — the pattern many may recognize as the 'Live Long and Prosper' Vulcan salute from Star Trek (fun fact: Leonard Nimoy adapted it from his childhood memories of watching Kohanim duchan!). Another interesting halacha: a Kohen who has committed murder cannot perform Birkat Kohanim — 'hands soaked in blood cannot bless' (until he does teshuva and repents).
- Learn about Bishul — the famous 'cooking' melacha on Shabbat! 🔥 While the original Mishnah calls this category baking because it relates to making the Mishkan's Showbread, it covers any way you use fire-generated heat to improve an item, tracing back to when workers boiled herbs to create colorful tapestry dyes. The basic law is that you cannot cook, bake, fry, or boil any uncooked food or liquid on Shabbat using a heat source that reaches 'yad soledet bo'—the temperature where your hand would instinctively jump back from the heat. However, because 'there is no cooking after cooking' (Ein Bishul Achar Bishul), you are generally permitted to re-warm dry, solid foods that were already fully cooked before Shabbat began (but you may require putting this onto something else so it doesn't 'look' like cooking). Reheating cold liquids, on the other hand, is completely forbidden because heat dramatically alters and improves them. These laws are very complex so you should probably learn more 👇!
- Learn about Borer — the famous 'selecting' melacha that changes how you eat on Shabbat! 🥗 Borer forbids separating a mixture of different types of objects, specifically by picking what you don't want out of what you do want. For an act of selection to be completely permissible on Shabbat, it must fulfill three strict conditions simultaneously: you must select the 'good' from the 'bad' (Ochel Mitoch Psolet), you must do it by hand (B'yad), and it must be for immediate use (M'yad). The first condition means you can only pick out the items you actually want to use or eat right now, leaving the unwanted items behind in the mixture; pulling the unwanted items out to leave the desired ones clean is a direct violation. The second condition, B'yad, means the selection must be done with your hands or a standard eating utensil like a fork, rather than using a dedicated sorting tool like a strainer, sieve, or peeler, which automatically transforms the act into a forbidden creative labor. The third condition, M'yad, dictates that the selection must be done for immediate or upcoming use—specifically for the meal happening right now—meaning you cannot sort a bowl of food in the morning to serve for lunch hours later. The law of Borer doesn't only apply to food, but anything that's in a mixture, like clothes, toys, etc. Learn more!
- Learn about Choresh — plowing and preparing the ground on Shabbat! 🚜 This melacha covers loosening, leveling, or improving the earth: from plowing a field and digging in a garden bed to aerate soil, to preparing a plot for planting. It traces back to the Mishkan, where the ground was plowed to cultivate herbs for tapestry dyes and wheat for the Showbread. Practical takeaway: this rule extends beyond actual gardening. Because leveling or rutting the earth is forbidden, we are careful not to drag heavy benches or strollers across soft outdoor dirt on Shabbat, as this inevitably carves a furrow. On Shabbat, we step back from reshaping the face of the earth itself. Learn more!
- Learn about Dosh — threshing and extracting resources on Shabbat! 🌾 In the ancient world, threshing meant beating stalks of wheat to separate the grain from its outer chaff—a vital step in the Mishkan to gather flour for the Showbread. Today, this melacha shows up in surprising ways, specifically when extracting liquid from solid food. Squeezing grapes or olives for their juice is a direct, Torah-level violation, and the Sages extended this to other fruits, which is why freshly squeezing oranges or grapefruits is forbidden on Shabbat. The rules are precise, but the theme is clear: on Shabbat, we don't 'process' raw nature to extract its hidden contents. We enjoy it exactly as it is. Learn more!
- Learn about Gozez — shearing wool and hair on Shabbat! 🐑 In the Mishkan, this melacha was performed when shearing sheep to obtain the wool needed for the beautiful woven tapestries and coverings. On Shabbat, Gozez prohibits detaching any natural growth from the body of a living creature or its hide, which applies directly to cutting human hair, trimming fingernails or toenails, and plucking hair, fur, or feathers. This is why haircuts, shaving, and nail clipping are strictly weekday activities (It's actually a mitzvah to do these things before Shabbat to honor it), and it is even why people avoid pulling loose feathers out of a pillow or down blanket if they are firmly attached. The law is so comprehensive that even pulling off loose, peeling skin or biting fingernails is restricted under this category. By pausing this melacha, Shabbat reminds us that even our own bodies are a part of creation that we do not 'harvest' or remodel on the day of rest, allowing us to honor ourselves and the natural world exactly as we are for one day a week. Learn more!
- Learn about Kiddush Hashem and Chillul Hashem — the positive commandment to sanctify G-d's Name and the severe prohibition against desecrating it! The Rambam defines both: KIDDUSH HASHEM: A Torah scholar or recognizably observant Jew who acts with exceptional integrity, patience, and fairness creates a Kiddush Hashem. Example: the Rambam says if a Torah scholar goes to the market, deals honestly, speaks pleasantly even when wronged, and people say 'What a beautiful person — what a beautiful Torah their teacher taught them!' — that is Kiddush Hashem. CHILLUL HASHEM: The opposite. A recognizably religious person who cheats, insults, or acts improperly causes a Chillul Hashem. The SEVERITY: Chillul Hashem is one of the most severe prohibitions, which some say can only be atoned for by death! Learn more!
- Learn about Kiddush Yadayim V'Raglayim — the mandatory sanctification of a Kohen's hands and feet before performing any sacred service in the Beit HaMikdash! 🌊 This crucial ritual was performed by placing the right hand over the right foot and the left hand over the left foot, allowing fresh water to pour over them simultaneously from a spigot on the copper Laver (Kiyor) or from any other sanctified vessel, poured either by himself or a colleague, as long as the flow was powered by direct human force (Koach Gava). Performing this act was an absolute requirement, and if a Kohen performed any service without first sanctifying his hands and feet, his service was completely invalid and disqualified. The sanctification would also be disqualified by any physical barrier like dirt or bandages on the skin (Chatzitzah), or instantly voided if he was up all night performing the service and then dawn broke the following morning. He would also need to wash again if the Kohen stepped outside the Temple courtyard, contracted ritual impurity, or even used the bathroom!. This elaborate process reminds us that divine service sometimes requires an uncompromised level of holiness. Learn more!
- Learn about Kotzer — reaping and detaching growth from its source! ✂️ This is one of the most everyday melachot: picking fruit from a tree, plucking a flower, or detaching anything from its source of growth. It traces back to the Mishkan, where wheat and dye-herbs were harvested. The rule is absolute: if it grows, you cannot sever it. This even includes picking herbs from a potted plant on your kitchen windowsill. To protect this law, the Sages even forbade making direct use of a tree on Shabbat—so climbing trees, leaning on them, or hanging hammocks is out. Ever wonder why people don't pick flowers or climb trees on Shabbat? This is why. Shabbat teaches us to enjoy creation without constantly taking from it or disrupting it. Learn more!
- Learn about Losh — kneading and forming a mass from separate ingredients! 🍞 Think dough: flour + water + mixing = one unified blob. In the Mishkan, Losh was a key agricultural step used when mixing water with the ground herb powders to make dyes, as well as kneading the flour to bake the Showbread. On Shabbat, this melacha prohibits combining a finely divided powder or small solid particles with a liquid to form a single, cohesive paste or thick mass. This restriction directly affects how people prepare everyday items like baby cereal, instant oatmeal, mustard, or even certain dense textures of egg salad. To avoid a Torah-level violation, the halacha requires using specific workarounds when a paste must be made, such as changing the normal order of ingredients (for instance, pouring the liquid into the bowl before adding the powder) and mixing the ingredients with an unusual motion (Shinui), like employing a crisscross slicing movement with a knife instead of a standard circular stir. This is why Shabbat challah is baked before the day of rest begins, since the creative act of unifying separate elements into a brand new entity belongs entirely to Friday, leaving us to enjoy the completed creation on Shabbat. Learn more!
- Learn about Me'ilah — misappropriating Temple property — and why using holy items for personal benefit is treated so seriously in halacha! ⚠️ In Temple times, when something was declared 'hekdesh' (consecrated to G-d), it became completely off-limits for personal use. Me'ilah (trespass/misappropriation) is what happens when you benefit from that property. Examples: eating from the Temple's showbread yourself, using a consecrated animal to carry your cargo, taking oil from the Temple's store of oil. The consequences: repay the full value PLUS an additional fifth (adding a fifth so it would be 25% of 125% instead of 20% of 100%), AND bring a korban asham (guilt offering). Fascinating detail: the violation only kicks in when you actually BENEFIT — so accidentally touching Temple property doesn't trigger it, but eating from it does. Learn more!
- Learn about Meraked — sifting and straining on Shabbat! 🫖 While Meraked achieves a similar result to Borer and Zoreh by separating desired items from undesired ones, the Talmud treats it as a distinct creative labor because it was a unique step in building the Mishkan, where a sieve was used to sift flour for the Showbread or to strain crushed herbs used for tapestry dyes. Today, Meraked specifically involves using a dedicated utensil, like a sifter or filter, to separate food or liquids from unwanted mixtures. A common toladah of this melacha is Meshamer (straining liquids), which carries a direct Torah-level prohibition if the liquid is so full of sediment or coffee grounds that the average person would completely refuse to drink it without filtration. Conversely, if a drink is already perfectly satisfactory and drinkable as it is, such as pouring orange juice through a mesh filter to remove pulp, the halacha permits it because the mixture is viewed as a single, acceptable entity rather than a mixture of two separate things. To avoid issues at the table, common activities to bypass on Shabbat include making coffee with a French press, pouring crushed matzah through a sifter to make matzah meal, using a slotted spoon to separate watery salads, or straining salad dressing directly from vegetables. This is why meals are intentionally prepared beforehand, reminding us that on Shabbat we step back from processing our physical world with tools and instead accept our food exactly as it has been prepared. Learn more!
- Learn about Musaf — the additional prayer said on Shabbat, Rosh Chodesh, and Yom Tov! 🎺 The Musaf ('additional') Amidah corresponds to the extra korban that was brought in the Temple on these special days. On a regular weekday, only the Tamid offering was brought. On Shabbat there was an ADDITIONAL offering of two lambs. On Rosh Hashanah, Rosh Chodesh, and other festival days there were numerous additional korbanot. Today we commemorate these with the Musaf Amidah, said after Shacharit. Each occasion has its own unique Musaf text. Learn more!
- Learn about Oneg Shabbat! 🍽️ Did you know that taking pleasure in Shabbat (Oneg Shabbat) isn't just a nice custom, but a fundamental part of its holiness and a mitzvah in itself? This includes enjoying delicious food, a restful atmosphere, engaging in Torah study, prayer, and quality family time. The Talmud (Shabbat 118a) teaches that one who delights in Shabbat is rewarded with abundant blessings. So, go ahead and savor some treats, take that Shabbat nap, and truly relax! Every act of enjoyment, when done with the intention of honoring the day, becomes a mitzvah-filled experience. Learn one halacha about Shabbat meals or practices and level up your next Shabbat table!
- Learn about Shemittah — the seventh year when the land of Israel rests — and what this actually means for Jews living in Israel today! 🌾 Every seven years, the Torah commands that the land of Israel must rest: no plowing, planting, pruning, or commercial harvesting. Generally you're only allowed to do basic things to keep plants alive but not to improve them. Produce that grows on its own during Shemittah has 'kedushat shevi'it' (seventh-year sanctity) and can't be sold normally. The challenge: how does a country of millions survive without farming for a year? Two main halachic solutions are used today in Israel: (1) 'Heter Mechira' — a legal sale of Israeli farmland to a non-Jew for the year, so technically a non-Jew 'owns' the land during Shemittah and can farm it. The Chief Rabbinate permits this; many orthodox authorities do not. (2) 'Otzar Beit Din' — a rabbinical court takes formal ownership of the crops and distributes them, charging only for labor costs, not for the produce itself. Products with Otzar Beit Din certification need to be treated with Shemittah sanctity in your home (eaten and not wasted or destroyed). If the produce rots on its own that's fine, but you can't add it to the garbage and thus intentionally make it inedible. Many people use special "Shemittah bins" that let the food decompose naturally if they're not going to eat it. Authorities debate whether it's a positive mitzvah to enjoy holy Shemittah fruit. Many say it is! Learn more.
- Learn about Tefillin! 👑 Did you know? The head Tefillin contains the same four Torah passages as the arm Tefillin, but arranged differently! Today's mission: Learn about how Tefillin connects our minds to G-d.
- Learn about Tochen — grinding and reducing substances to small particles on Shabbat! ⚙️ In the Mishkan, this melacha was performed by crushing herbs and minerals into fine powders to create the vibrant dyes for the tapestries and components for the incense. Because the primary category of Tochen applies strictly to things that grow from the earth (Gedulei Karka), dicing vegetables like Israeli salad into exceptionally fine pieces on Shabbat can constitute a direct violation unless you deliberately chop them into significantly larger pieces than your standard weekday cut. This earth-only definition is also why you are forbidden from scraping or crushing hardened, dried mud off your shoes on Shabbat, as breaking down caked soil into dust directly mimics the agricultural act of grinding. Conversely, because there is no grinding restriction for items that do not grow from the ground, you are permitted to finely chop or grate foods like cooked meat or cheese for immediate use. For pre-ground earth products, the halacha provides the exemption that 'there is no grinding after grinding' (Ein Tochen Achar Tochen), allowing one to crush pre-baked items like matzah or crackers into crumbs. This is why people rely on pre-ground coffee, avoid using blenders, change their vegetable chopping sizes, and handle muddy boots carefully on Shabbat, transforming meal preparation and daily upkeep into a profound lesson in refraining from dismantling the structural integrity of creation. Learn more!
- Learn about Zoreh — winnowing and scattering on Shabbat! 💨 In the Mishkan, winnowing was a critical step in making the Showbread: throwing threshed grain into the air so the wind could blow the light, worthless chaff away from the heavy, valuable kernels. While the core melacha focuses on separating food from non-food using the wind (or your breath), the Jerusalem Talmud expands this concept to include scattering any substance into the wind, even if no food is involved. Because leading halachic authorities adhere to this stricter understanding, the practical applications are eye-opening: you should not shake a tablecloth outside after a meal if the wind will catch and scatter the crumbs, nor should you blow dust off a book cover, as forcing a stream of air to scatter particles mimics the classic act of winnowing. The ultimate lesson of Zoreh is that on Shabbat, we don't use the elements—like the wind or our own breath—to sort, refine, or scatter things. Learn more!
- Learn about a surprising ruling: answering Amen can be GREATER than reciting the bracha itself! 🙏 The Talmud (Berakhot 53b) records a debate: is the one who recites a blessing greater, or the one who answers Amen? Rabbi Yose ruled: 'The one who answers Amen is greater than the one who recites the blessing!' Because Amen is an affirmation — you are declaring that what was just said is completely true. When you say Amen to a blessing, you are endorsing it and sharing in its merit. The Talmud compares it to two workers: the one who leads (recites the blessing) goes first into battle, but the one who responds (answers Amen) is the stronger fighter who reinforces the victory. The Zohar teaches that 'AMEN' stands for 'El Melech Ne'eman' — G-d, faithful King. So when you answer Amen, you're not just saying 'yes' — you're proclaiming G-d as the faithful King. Try to give your next 'amen' a little oomph!
- Learn about appreciating G-d's creations! Found something that smells wonderful? Here's a chance to turn that nice scent into a spiritual moment! Say this blessing: 'Baruch Atah Adonai Eloheinu Melech Ha'olam Borei Minei Besamim' 🌺 (Blessed are You... who creates various spices). Then take in that beautiful fragrance. Did you know? This blessing helps us recognize the divine artistry in every pleasant scent! (this is a general bracha on fragrant herbs/spices/flowers/etc.)
- Learn about honoring the Kohanim! Kohanim are male descendants of Aharon the Cohen from the Torah! They were the ones who did the service in the Beit Hamikdash, offering all the nation's sacrifices before Hashem on the altar. Even today, they are still held to a higher standard of sanctity, meaning they must avoid contact with the dead and follow specific marriage laws to maintain their spiritual level. In our daily lives, we honor their elevated status by giving them the first Aliyah in the synagogue when the Torah is read, and seeking their Priestly Blessing (in communities outside Israel, this may only happen on festival days). It is also a beautiful tradition to show Torah-observant Kohanim extra respect by not asking them to perform menial labor or personal chores, acknowledging that their time is dedicated to a higher purpose. By treating them with this special dignity, we keep the ancient spark of the priesthood alive in our modern world. Your mission: next time you see a Kohen, remember to give him special honor and not ask him to do menial tasks for you ;).
- Learn about kavod sefarim — the halachic obligation to treat holy books with respect — and the surprising level of detail this goes into! 📚 The Rambam rules that writings of Torah have inherent sanctity. Practical rules from the Shulchan Aruch and poskim: (1) Don't place a holy book face-down — this is disrespectful. (2) Don't sit on a surface that has a Torah book on it. (3) Don't use a Torah book as a pillow or headrest, or for any of your own needs like blocking rain or the sunlight from your eyes. (4) Hierarchy matters: a Chumash (Five Books) has higher sanctity than Nach (Prophets/Writings); Nach has higher sanctity than a Gemara or other books. Higher sanctity goes on TOP. (5) When a sefer falls to the ground, it is customary to kiss it upon picking it up. (6) If a sefer becomes too worn to read, it cannot be thrown away — it must go to genizah (respectful storage/burial). Many communities have a genizah box for worn-out religious texts. Learn more!
- Learn about lo talin — the prohibition against withholding a worker's wages past their deadline — and how tight the time limits actually are! ⏱️ 'The wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you overnight until the morning' (Leviticus 19:13). 'Give him his wages on that day, before the sun sets' (Deuteronomy 24:15). These two verses establish both a positive commandment (pay on time) and a negative prohibition (don't delay payment)! The consequences could be dire! Learn more!
- Learn about ona'ah — the Torah prohibition against price fraud — and the fascinating 1/6 rule that determines exactly when it kicks in! 💼 The Torah says 'Do not wrong one another' (Leviticus 25:17). The Sages specify this applies to financial transactions: if you sell something at a price MORE than 1/6 above its market value, or buy it MORE than 1/6 BELOW market value — that's ona'ah, and the transaction can halachically be reversed. If something is very difficult to obtain in a certain area of the world, then the person selling it has more free rein to set prices, since they effectively set the price for that region!
- Learn about one of the main vessels of the Beit HaMikdash — the holy Menorah. Did you know that there is a fascinating debate among the Sages regarding how often the Menorah in the Beit Hamikdash was actually lit? While it is commonly understood that the lamps were lit once daily toward evening to burn through the night—a view championed by the Raavad and Rashba—the Rambam (Maimonides) argues that the Menorah was actually lit twice a day, both morning and evening, to ensure the light remained truly continuous. Adding a layer of mystery, the Talmud and the Alter Rebbe explain that even if the other lamps were extinguished, the Western Lamp (there's much discussion about which of the 7 candles this actually was) was kept burning throughout the entire day as a miraculous testimony that the Divine Presence rested among the people of Israel! Learn more!
- Learn about one of the most surprising 'superpowers' in a kosher kitchen: the Case of the Spicy Onion! In the world of kosher law, flavor usually needs high heat to travel from a pot or a knife into your food. If everything is cold, tastes don't transfer and a simple rinse-off solves any mix-up (as long as it doesn't sit for 24 hours). But there is a fascinating exception called Davar Charif — literally 'a sharp thing' — where the rules of physics seem to change. Think of foods like onions, lemons, or hot peppers as having a sort of chemical heat. These foods are so 'aggressive' that they don't need a stove to move flavor around. If you take a cold knife that is only used for meat and slice into a sharp, raw onion, that onion actually 'squeezes' the meat essence out of the steel blade. Even though no one turned on a flame, that onion is now considered a meat product. If you accidentally tossed it into a bowl of cream cheese, you'd have a real kosher conundrum on your hands! What makes Davar Charif even more 'powerful' is how it handles time. Usually, if a pot or knife hasn't been used for 24 hours, any flavor trapped inside its pores is considered 'stale' and loses its status. But a sharp food can actually 'reawaken' that sleeping flavor. A sharp onion can pull flavor out of a knife that hasn't touched a steak in years, making the onion 'meat' all over again. This is why many people keep a special 'Pareve' knife — one that is neither meat nor dairy — specifically for their davar charif fruits and veggies. Learn more!
- Learn about proper bowing on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur! 🙇‍♂️ Here's something fascinating: When we bow down during these holy days, we don't put our faces directly on stone floors. Instead, we place something between our faces and the floor - like paper, cloth, or special mats! Why? The Rambam explains that full prostration on stone was a practice associated with idol worship, except in the Temple. This teaches us to be mindful even in how we bow to G-d!
- Learn about ribbis, the prohibition against charging or paying interest in a Jewish-to-Jewish financial transaction. The Torah states: You shall not give him your money upon interest, nor give him your food for increase (Leviticus 25:37). While the Torah prohibition applies to charging interest, the Sages expanded the prohibition to include many indirect forms of benefit and paying interest. Core Principles: Charging or paying interest between two Jewish parties is strictly forbidden without a valid Heter Iska. The Torah prohibition of ribbis applies exclusively to lending money to another Jew; charging interest to a non-Jew is permitted by Halacha (Deuteronomy 23:21). The prohibition applies not only to cash, but also to non-monetary benefits, services, or gifts connected to a loan. A Heter Iska is a legal and halachic contract that restructures a loan into a business partnership, allowing for returns that are halachically permissible.
- Learn about saying the Shema! Good news - you can say it standing, walking, or even while riding your trusty donkey (or car, in modern times 😉). Just don't try saying it while lying flat on your back or face-down - that's a no-go. Side-lying is perfectly fine though! And if you're not feeling well or can't turn to your side? No worries - just lean a bit to the side and you're good to go. The key is to say it in a way that shows respect while still being practical for your situation.
- Learn about seeing the good in everything! When life throws you a curveball, try saying 'Gam zu l'tovah' (גם זו לטובה) - this too is for the good! ✡ This powerful phrase was made famous by the sage Nachum Ish Gamzu, who always found the silver lining. Even in tough times, trust that G-d has a perfect plan. It's like having a GPS for your soul - sometimes the route looks weird, but it's taking you exactly where you need to go! 🗺️
- Learn about shechitah — kosher slaughter — and the five requirements that must all be met or the meat is forbidden! 🔪 The five invalidating factors of shechitah, remembered by the Hebrew acronym ShaCHaT NaDaH: (1) Shehiyah — pausing mid-cut. The cut must be one continuous smooth motion. Stop in the middle, even briefly, and it's potentially treif. (2) Chaladah — 'burrowing.' The knife cannot go underneath the trachea into the throat; it must cut cleanly across the surface, and the knife must be completely visible. It cannot be inserted under a flap of skin or between the trachea and esophagus (3) Drasha — pressing too hard. The knife must GLIDE, not be forced. If the shochet pushes rather than draws the blade, it's invalid. (4) Hagramah — cutting in the wrong location. There's a very precise section of the throat where the cut is valid — too high or too low and it's invalid. (5) Ikur — tearing. The windpipe or esophagus cannot be ripped out before the cut is complete. And the knife itself? It must be razor-sharp with a perfectly smooth edge — no nicks. The knife is so sharp it can put a high-quality chef’s knife or surgical scalpel to shame! Those often have a tiny, saw-like jaggedness. That microscopic 'saw' is actually what helps a kitchen knife bite into a tomato skin. For a chalaf, that jaggedness is strictly forbidden. The blade must be polished to a mirror-like, frictionless finish. If there is even a miniscule burr, nick, or unevenness on the edge, the knife will tear the animal's tissue rather than slice it cleanly, instantly rendering the meat non-kosher. Kosher meat is a high 'steaks' biz! 😂
- Learn about thanking Hashem for sustaining you! 🍞 Birkat Hamazon is unique because it is one of the only blessings commanded directly in the Torah—meaning after a bread meal. All other blessings before and after food were instituted by the rabbis. It’s not just a quick "thanks for the food" but a four-part journey through Jewish history!  First is "HaZan" written by Moshe to thank Hashem for the Manna in the desert (miraculous food that fell from Heaven).  Second is "HaAretz" written by Yehoshua as a tribute to the Land of Israel. 🇮🇱 Third is "Boneh Yerushalayim" added by Kings David and Solomon to pray for the Holy City. Finally is "Hatov Vehametiv" which was added by the Sages after the miracle at Betar! 🕊️ To do it right you have to eat at least a "kazayit" of bread (about the size of a golf ball) within about 4-9 minutes. If three or more men eat together they form a "Zimun"—a formal invitation to bless as a team—which levels up the holiness! 🤝 The Halacha says you should sit down and focus on the words. Talk about an after-dinner mint! We're mintin' mitzvot, fam! 🎊🍷
- Learn about the Holy Temple's recycling system! ♻️ Did you know? When priestly garments became worn out, they weren't just discarded - they were transformed into wicks for the Temple's lights! Here's something powerful: Even holy items that could no longer serve their original purpose were given new life in service of G-d. Today's mission: Study this practice and reflect on finding a new purpose in everything.
- Learn about the Korban Tamid — the daily offering that doesn't skip a beat when the Temple is standing. This constant offering was brought to the Temple twice a day, every single day, without exception. Once after dawn and once in the afternoon. It was always a lamb within its first year of life, perfectly unblemished. The morning Tamid was the absolute first act of the day; no other offering was allowed before it. The afternoon Tamid was the grand finale, the very last act before sunset. The only exception was the Passover lamb, but otherwise, the Tamid of the afternoon closed the doors on all other sacrifices for that day. Even though the Temple isn't standing today, the rhythm lives on. Our Shacharit and Mincha prayers correspond directly to these two daily lambs. The Talmud in Berakhot debates whether we pray because the Patriarchs started the trend—Abraham in the morning, Isaac in the afternoon, and Jacob at night—or if we do it to replace the sacrifices. The answer is basically both! The Patriarchs gave prayer its soul, but the Tamid gave it its schedule. Now, instead of a lamb, we offer the words of our lips to keep the chain goin'. 🐑
- Learn about the Melacha of Boneh—the deliberate act of building or assembling a structure—and discover how this manufacturing boundary directly shapes how we handle household items, furniture, and temporary shelters on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled artisans erected the massive wooden wall panels and fit the heavy foundation sockets together to create a unified, sturdy Tabernacle. The core definition of Boneh is the creation, improvement, or assembly of any structure, vessel, or functional space by combining parts together into a stable unit. On Shabbat, this means that heavy construction, installing permanent shelving, or repairing a loose door handle that fell out of its socket is strictly forbidden. While a person is unlikely to be pouring concrete on Saturday, Boneh introduces highly practical guidelines for routine household adjustments. A classic example is setting up a pop-up canopy or a large, multi-panel tent for shade; because you are assembling a covered shelter that shields a significant area from the sun or rain, it can constitute the creation of an Ohel (tent/canopy), which falls under the restrictions of building. Similarly, snapping together modular plastic furniture or attaching/screwing parts that form a rigid, tight unit are all problems. Learn more!
- Learn about the Melacha of Hotza'ah—the deliberate act of transferring or carrying objects between different domains—and discover how this creative boundary completely transforms our public movement and community spaces on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor is uniquely highlighted throughout the Torah and traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan. When Moshe gathered the nation to build the sanctuary, he opened with a strict warning to keep the Sabbath, teaching them that constructing Hashem's home does not override the day of rest. Thus, when the people halted the public transport of their gold, silver, and fabrics from their personal tents into the communal camp, that exact pause established the legal blueprint for ceasing public carrying on the holy day. The core definition of Hotza'ah is the moving of an object from a private domain (reshut hayachid) to a public domain (reshut harabim), vice versa, or carrying an object a distance of four cubits (about six feet) within a public domain. Under Torah law, a private domain is an enclosed space measuring at least four by four handbreadths with walls at least ten handbreadths high, while a true public domain is an unroofed, wide-open thoroughfare used by the masses. However, because most modern streets do not meet the strict population requirements of a biblical public domain, they are rabbinically classified as a karmelit (a semi-public area), where carrying is still restricted to protect the biblical boundaries. This creates highly practical implications for stepping outside the front door, as carrying everyday necessities like house keys, tissues, water bottles, or pushing a stroller becomes prohibited. To navigate this smoothly without an eiruv—a symbolic halakhic enclosure that unifies a neighborhood into a single shared 'private' domain—observant individuals utilize highly creative solutions. For instance, people fashion their house keys into wearable jewelry like functional tie clips, or belt buckles, since wearing a permitted ornament or garment does not constitute forbidden carrying. To ensure seamless movement on the day of rest, communities invest in establishing and maintaining an eiruv, while individuals without an eiruv ensure all unwearable items are left at home or placed at their destination before sunset on Friday. Learn more!
- Learn about the Melacha of Koreah—the deliberate act of tearing materials—and why breaking something down can be a profound violation of Shabbat rest! While tearing seems purely destructive, the Torah teaches that when a tear serves a constructive, productive purpose, it is an act of master craftsmanship. This labor traces directly back to the construction of the Mishkan, where workers would carefully rip apart sections of the heavy curtains if a mistake was made in the complex weaving patterns, or tear fabrics to reshape them into the exact proportions needed for the sanctuary walls. The core definition of Koraya is the tearing or ripping apart of any unified object—whether cloth, leather, paper, or plastic—with the ultimate intent of improving it, preparing it for a future creative act, or achieving a specific functional benefit.
