Unlike the 39 Melachot, which trace back to the physical labor of building the Mishkan, Muktzeh is a protective fence established by the Sages during the era of the Second Temple. The core definition of Muktzeh is any object that a person purposefully sets aside or removes from their mind before sunset on Friday, making it completely forbidden to move, handle, or carry throughout the holy day. The Sages enacted this boundary for several critical reasons: to prevent people from accidentally performing creative labor, to ensure the day of rest feels physically distinct from the workweek, and to protect the sanctity of the home from mundane clutter. Because Muktzeh focuses on an item's purpose, it introduces highly practical categories for things found in any modern household. The most common category is Kli SheMelakhto L'Issur (a tool whose primary use is forbidden on Shabbat), such as a hammer, a pen, or a smartphone; you are generally allowed to move these items only if you desperately need the physical space they are occupying, or if you need to use the object itself for a permitted Shabbat activity (like using a hammer to crack open a tough coconut). Another major category is Muktzeh Machmat Gufo (items that are inherently useless on Shabbat), which includes raw stones, dirt, or money; because these objects serve absolutely no functional purpose on the day of rest, they cannot be touched or moved under any circumstances. Even a broken item or a scrap of material that lost its utility right before Friday night becomes restricted, as it was not designated for a Shabbat purpose before the day began. To navigate these boundaries smoothly on Shabbat, observant families store their wallets, work tools, and electronics out of sight before Friday evening, creating a serene, work-free environment where every handled object is dedicated entirely to the peace and joy of the day. Learn more!
- Learn about the special Kiyor (washbasin) of the Temple! 🚰 Did you know? The Kohanim had to wash their hands and feet at the same time by placing their right hand on their right foot and left hand on their left foot! This unique position reminded them to be fully dedicated to service - from head to toe! Plus, the water had to be fresh each day, teaching us about starting each day's divine service with renewal.
- Learn about the spiritual "powerhouse" that was (and will be in the future) the center of the universe! 🏛️ The Beit HaMikdash in Jerusalem wasn't just a building—it was the one spot on Earth where the physical and spiritual worlds collided! ⚡The layout was a journey into deeper and deeper holiness. It started with the Ezrat Nashim, a massive courtyard where the nation gathered for epic celebrations like the Water Drawing Ceremony. 🎊 Then, you’d move up the steps to the Azarah, the inner courtyard where the Kohanim performed the sacred services and the massive Altar stood. 🔥 Inside the main Sanctuary building was the Kodesh, home to the golden Menorah that stayed lit 24/7, the Table with the twelve loaves of Showbread, and the Golden Altar for sweet-smelling incense! 🕯️🍞 But the absolute heart of the Temple was the Kodesh HaKodashim (Holy of Holies). This room was so sacred that only the High Priest could enter, and only once a year on Yom Kippur. 🕊️ Inside sat the Ark of the Covenant itself! 📜 Even though it’s not standing today, many Sages say the "Divine Presence" never left the area. Learn more about it! 🎊
- Learn about the ultimate "handle with care" policy for Hashem's names! ✍️ The Torah commands "you shall not do so to Hashem your G-d," which the sages explain as a strict prohibition against erasing or destroying His holy Name. This isn't just about being careful—it’s about treating the physical representation of the Divine with the highest level of honor! 🏛️ The halacha identifies seven specific Names that are so holy they can never be erased. If a scribe accidentally writes one of these in a Torah scroll and makes a mistake, he can't just reach for an eraser; the entire section must be cut out and placed in a "Genizah" (a respectful storage for holy papers until they can be buried). 📜 This law also means you have to be mindful of where you write these Names. We avoid writing them on anything that might be thrown away or treated disrespectfully—which is exactly why you'll often see people write "G-d" with a dash or use abbreviations like 'יי' in Hebrew books. There have even been documented cases of people writing Hashem's name on blackboards in schools, and the rabbis ruled that the entire blackboard needed to be buried! 📧 Even the "holy nicknames" we use for Hashem, like "The Merciful One," must be treated with dignity, though they don't have the same strict prohibition against erasing as the core seven. 🕊️ It’s a powerful exercise in mindfulness, reminding us that even the letters on a page can carry a spark of the Infinite!
- Learn about the ultimate "legal permit" to enjoy the world! 🍎 The Talmud says that everything on earth belongs to Hashem, so taking a bite without a blessing is like "stealing" from the King’s palace. Saying a bracha is essentially asking for permission and turning a physical snack into a spiritual moment! 🏛️ The system is incredibly precise: if it grows on a tree, you say "Ha'etz." If it comes from the ground, it's "Ha'adamah." For bread, you have the heavyweight "Ha'motzi," and for everything else—like meat, cheese, or chocolate—you say "Shehakol." 🕊️ But then there’s the VIP of the drink world: wine! Because wine is so special and used for holy moments like Kiddush, it gets its very own unique blessing: "Borei Peri HaGafen." 🍷 It’s so powerful that if you drink wine during a meal, it often covers all other drinks on the table! 🥂 There is even a specific "Order of Operations" called Mag'a Esh to help you decide which food gets the blessing first if you have a big plate. 🍇 It’s like a daily training session in mindfulness—stopping to recognize exactly where your food came from and all the amazing creations, tastes and flavors that Hashem put in the world for us to enjoy 🎊🥯.
- Learn about the ultimate 'Heavenly delivery service' that sustained a nation for forty years! ☁️ The Manna was a supernatural food that fell from the sky every morning, and the halacha surrounding it is absolutely wild. It was the original "clean eating" because it was perfectly absorbed by the body with zero waste! 🥯 Every person received exactly one "omer" (about 43 eggs' worth of volume), no matter how much they tried to hoard. If you tried to save some for the next day, it would miraculously spoil—teaching the nation the ultimate lesson in trusting Hashem for their daily bread! 🕊️ But on Fridays, the "Double Portion" fell, giving everyone enough for Shabbat without any work. This is actually where we get the custom of having two loaves of Challah at our Shabbat tables today! 🍞🍞 The midrash says it tasted like whatever you imagined—from honey cake to a savory steak—making it the most versatile menu in history. 🍯🥩 It only stopped falling once the Jewish people crossed the Jordan River and entered the Land of Israel. Did you know? To make sure we never forgot this miracle Hashem commanded Moshe to place a golden jar of Manna inside the Holy of Holies next to the Ark! 🏺 When King Yoshiyahu hid the Ark to protect it from invaders before the Temple was destroyed, he hid the jar of Manna right along with it where it remains hidden to this day. When Moshiach comes we hope we'll merit to see it again and say the bracha on manna: 'HaMotzi Lechem Min HaShamayim' (Who makes bread comes forth from the Heavens)! Talk about a breakfast of champions!
- Learn about the ultimate spiritual "high-voltage" connection! ⚡ Tefillin are way more than just leather boxes—they are a physical bond between your mind, your heart, your actions, and G-d Himself! Inside these hand-crafted parchment scrolls are four specific sections of the Torah that proclaim the unity and all-powerfulness of Hashem. 📜 When the Lubavitcher Rebbe was once asked where modern-day technology like computers could be found in the Torah, he said "Tefillin!" 💻 He explained that just as a computer is a synthesis of different machines connected by cables to work as one, Tefillin syncs your brain, your heart, and your hands so they all find harmony in a single coordinated purpose. 🌐 The halacha for wearing them is precise to ensure the "signal" is clear. First, you place the "Shel Yad" on the bicep of your weaker arm, tilted toward your heart. 🦾 Then the "Shel Rosh" is placed exactly above the hairline, centered between the eyes. Learn more about how to put on tefillin!
- Learn about the world's first mandatory RSVP event - the Korban Pesach! 🐑 This wasn’t just a mitzvah, it was a logistical miracle. Imagine hundreds of thousands of people descending on Jerusalem all with one goal: barbecuing enough meat to feed the entire nation! 🌃 But you couldn't just stroll in and buy a couple lamb chops. You had to be registered on a specific group's animal before the sun even went down. Every single person had to be part of a 'chaburah' or registered group. If you weren’t on the list you weren’t eating! 📝 On the afternoon of the 14th of Nissan pretty much every able-bodied Jew in the country showed up at the Temple at once. To handle this holy rush hour, the priests split everyone into three massive shifts. As soon as the first group packed the courtyard the heavy doors swung shut and the service began!  This is where it gets cinematic because the Kohanim (priests) stood in perfectly orchestrated lines reaching all the way to the altar. One row held only gold bowls and the next only silver. As lambs or goats were offered, the blood was passed hand to hand down the line like a relay race! Once your animal was slaughtered and its blood sprinkled on the altar, you got your animal back. Then each group went to where they were staying, and needed to roast their meat whole, entirely over an open fire and hold a Pesach seder! And forget the carving knife because you were strictly forbidden from breaking a single bone! It had to stay whole and regal. 🦴 The pressure was on to finish by the midnight deadline while the whole city erupted in the singing of Hallel. Jerusalem was so packed the rooftops were covered in people and the smell of roasted lamb filled the air! It was a massive roaring celebration of freedom! May we merit to experience it ourselves speedily in our days as soon as the Temple is rebuilt! Learn more! 🎊🍷
- Learn about tochachah — the Torah obligation to rebuke someone doing wrong — and the limits that make it extremely difficult to fulfill in practice! 💬 The Torah commands: 'You shall surely rebuke your fellow' (Leviticus 19:17). The Rambam rules this is a positive commandment. But the halacha is full of conditions: (1) Tochachah must be given PRIVATELY — never in front of others. Shaming someone while rebuking them is such a serious violation that the Talmud compares it to murder. (2) You must rebuke in a CALM, gentle voice — not in anger or humiliation. (3) Here's the stunning rule: you must rebuke them again and again — 'even 100 times' if necessary — until either they accept it or they tell you to stop. (4) If the person will respond with insults and become MORE sinful, you are NOT obligated to rebuke. The point is to help, not to trigger. (5) If the person is committing the sin out of ignorance (they don't know it's wrong), there's an opinion that you should leave them be — 'it is better they sin in ignorance than sin defiantly.' The Talmud says: Rabbi Tarfon declared that 'I wonder if there is anyone in this generation who knows how to give tochachah' — implying that doing it correctly (without shaming, at the right time, in the right way) is extraordinarily difficult. Learn more!
- Learn about treifot — internal defects that make a properly slaughtered animal forbidden to eat, even if everything else was done right! 🫁 An animal can be a kosher species, killed by a certified shochet with a flawless blade — and STILL be forbidden if it has certain physical defects. The lungs are the main focus: a trained inspector (bodek) examines the lungs after every single slaughter. A perforated lung is the most common treifah found. Many communities follow the 'glatt kosher' standard (from the Yiddish word for 'smooth') — meaning the lungs must be completely smooth with zero adhesions at all. Technically, some lung adhesions can be peeled off and examined, and if they peel cleanly the animal is still kosher (this is what 'regular kosher' means in many contexts). But the higher 'glatt' standard accepts only perfectly smooth lungs with no adhesions whatsoever. Other defects that make an animal treif: a punctured windpipe or esophagus, a broken spine, a missing or punctured kidney, a perforated stomach or intestine. The Talmud lists 18 main categories of treifot. The Rambam rules the list is CLOSED — nothing new can be added. This is why every piece of kosher meat you buy has been individually inspected by a trained professional. It's not a rubber stamp — real animals are failed and rejected every single day in every kosher slaughterhouse. An animal could be perfectly healthy to eat by USDA standards but still be treif. Think about what it means that Hashem only wants Jews to eat this level quality of meat.
- Learn about true prophets! 🔍 The Rambam teaches: Once a prophet is established as genuine (like Moses or Isaiah), it's actually forbidden to constantly test them! Here's why: It's like repeatedly testing a proven friend - it shows a lack of trust. Instead, we should just do what they say and know it's what G-d wants!
- Learn about tumat meit — ritual impurity from contact with death — and how it's actually the strongest form of impurity in all of Jewish law! ☠️ A corpse is called the 'father of fathers of impurity' (avi avot hatumah) — the absolute highest level of ritual impurity in the entire Torah system. Here's how it spreads: (1) Direct contact — touching a corpse makes you impure for 7 days. (2) Carrying — carrying a corpse even without touching also causes 7-day impurity. (3) Tent impurity (ohel) — this is the most famous rule: anyone or anything INSIDE the same enclosed space as a corpse becomes impure, even without touching it! So if someone dies in a house, everything in that house — people, utensils, furniture — becomes impure simultaneously. This is why Kohanim (priests) are forbidden from entering a house with a corpse, or even from walking over a grave. Kohanim keep this law today — which is why you'll see a Kohen take a long detour in a hospital (not walking through the building if someone may have died inside). The purification method? The Parah Adumah (Red Heifer ash water) — sprinkled on day 3 and day 7 of the 7-day impurity period. Without it, you stay impure indefinitely. Since we have no Red Heifer today, all Jews are considered to have tumat meit.
- Learn about tzedakah priorities — Jewish law has a specific order for who gets your charity money first! 🎯 The Shulchan Aruch (Yoreh Deah 251) establishes a clear priority order: (1) Your OWN basic needs come first — a person is not required to make themselves destitute to give tzedakah. You must maintain your basic living standards. (2) YOUR HOUSEHOLD — spouse, children, parents dependent on you. (3) RELATIVES who are poor — closer relatives take priority over distant ones. (4) Your CITY or community — the poor of your city before the poor of another city. (5) Israeli poor before the poor of other countries.
- Learn an interesting kashrut detail: Meat and milk are a well-known kosher restriction. But did you know? It applies only to cooking a kosher animal's meat with milk, not to non-kosher meat like pork (which is already forbidden because it's not a kosher species to begin with). 🧠 So yes - pork is still forbidden to eat, but you would halachically be allowed to cook it in milk. Learn more about kashrut!
- Learn and grow! Watch any Torah class that interests you 📚. Whether it's about the weekly parsha, Jewish history, or practical Jewish law (halacha) - every minute of Torah study lights up the world! Plus, you can learn at your own pace and choose topics you love 🎓
- Learn from history's mistakes! 🏜️ Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) how even after seeing incredible miracles in the desert, our ancestors sometimes lost faith and complained. The biggest mistake? Building a golden calf just 40 days after hearing G-d's voice! Here's the powerful lesson: Even if we make big mistakes, G-d always gives us a chance to return and do better. When you're feeling down about a spiritual slip-up, remember: it's never too late to start fresh!
- Learn from the Ramban's wisdom! 📜 The Ramban wrote a powerful letter to his son that's still inspiring people today! Did you know? He said that reading this letter daily would help ensure your prayers are answered! Here's something amazing: The letter focuses on developing humility and speaking gently to everyone. Today's challenge: Read the letter and choose one piece of advice to practice.
- Learn how to bid farewell to Shabbat with Havdalah! 🕯️🍷🌿 Havdalah (meaning 'separation') is the beautiful ceremony performed after Shabbat ends (Saturday night, once three stars are visible in the sky), marking our transition back to the ordinary weekdays. It's a multi-sensory experience with four distinct blessings, each connected to a specific element: (1) Wine: We recite 'Borei pri hagafen' over a full cup of wine, just like Kiddush, symbolizing the holiness that enters and exits with Shabbat. (2) Fragrant Spices (Besamim): We smell sweet spices while reciting 'Borei minei vesamim.' Why? The Talmud teaches that we receive an 'extra soul' (neshama yeteira) on Shabbat, and when it (with Shabbat) departs, the fragrant spices comfort our soul. (3) Fire: We say 'Borei m'orei ha'eish' over a lit candle with multiple wicks (like a special braided Havdalah candle), gazing at the flame and the shadows it casts on our fingernails. This blessing commemorates Adam's discovery of fire after the first Shabbat. (4) Havdalah Blessing: Finally, we recite 'Hamavdil bein kodesh l'chol,' praising Hashem who distinguishes between the holy and the mundane, light and darkness, Israel and the nations, Shabbat and the six days of work. A crucial halacha: you cannot perform any Melachah (labor forbidden on Shabbat) after Shabbat ends until you hear Havdalah or say the phrase 'Baruch Hamavdil bein Kodesh l'chol' (Blessed is He Who separates between holy and mundane). So say that even before you pick up the Havdalah items! Learn more!
- Learn how to light Shabbat candles! 🕯️ The mitzvah of lighting Shabbat candles ushers in the holy day, bringing peace, warmth, and a special spiritual light into your home. This beautiful act, performed by Jewish women (or men if no woman is available) before sunset on Friday, marks the transition from the mundane week to the sacred Shabbat. The Torah describes Shabbat as a sign between Hashem and Am Yisrael, a perpetual reminder of Creation and our unique covenant. The halacha (Jewish law) dictates a fascinating custom: after lighting the candles, we wave our hands and cover our eyes. This allows us to recite the blessing over the candles *before* we visually benefit from their light. Normally, a blessing precedes the mitzvah, but reciting the blessing itself ushers in Shabbat, after which lighting candles would be forbidden! So, we light, cover our eyes (effectively delaying our acceptance of Shabbat's holiness until after the blessing), say the blessing, and then uncover our eyes to experience the mitzvah fully. It's a brilliant halachic workaround! Now you know why we do it!
- Learn more ↗
- Learn some halacha - When doing the mitzvah of saying the Shema (verses from the Torah proclaiming G-d's oneness), remember this important mitzvah detail: If you're walking, stop for a moment to say that powerful first verse - give it the attention it deserves! After that, you can continue walking while saying the rest.
- Learn some halacha about the Shema - If you're at work when it's time to say Shema, and you can't say it later, here's what to do: Take a complete break from your work for the whole first paragraph. Whether you're self-employed or working for someone else, this special mitzvah takes priority! After that first section, you can multitask and say the rest while working. Even if you're on the clock, G-d comes first!
- Learn something interesting about kosher animals: As far as meat goes, in the whole entire world there aren't any animals allowed to be eaten by Jews aside from ten species listed in the Torah: Cows, Sheep, Goats, are the domestic ones. The wild ones are the gazelle, deer, antelope, ibex, chamois, bison, the giraffe (translations may be subject to dispute) and their subspecies. So why did Hashem create millions of species we aren't allowed to eat? Ponder this deep secret from the Torah! The Sifra, an ancient legal commentary, offers a powerful psychological perspective. A person should not say, 'I don't want to eat pork because it's disgusting.' Rather, one should say, 'I would like to eat it, but what can I do? My Father in Heaven has decreed against it.' Hashem created a world full of 'forbidden' things to give us the constant opportunity to choose His will over our own desires, transforming the simple act of not eating into a holy deed. Hashem wants to give us unfathomable rewards, in this world and in the World to Come. There are countless things in this world a person can do, let alone eat. If we do the few things that Hashem tells us are 'mitzvot' (His will), imagine the joy He gets!
- Learn the #1 Constant Mitzvah! Did you know there are six special mitzvot you can fulfill every single moment, no matter where you are or what you're doing? The very first is to know that Hashem (God) exists! This isn't just believing; it's about recognizing and internalizing that God is the Creator of everything and is actively involved in sustaining and guiding the entire world, right now! Take a moment, look around you, and marvel at the intricate design and vastness of the universe, and reflect on the miracle of your own existence. This constant awareness deepens your relationship with the Creator.
- Learn the Rambam's precise definition of teshuvah — and why verbal confession (vidui) is not optional! 🔄 The Rambam rules (Hilchot Teshuvah 1:1): teshuvah is a positive Torah commandment whenever one has sinned — whether a minor or major sin. The full process consists of four components: (1) Azivat HaChait — abandoning the sin. You actually STOP doing it, not just feel bad. (2) Charatah — genuine regret. Not just 'I got caught' regret, but authentic recognition that you did something wrong. (3) Kabalat l'atid — committing to change. Deciding that given the same circumstances, you would not repeat the sin. (4) Vidui — verbal confession out loud. Just feeling regret in your heart is NOT sufficient for teshuvah! The vidui must include: naming the specific sin, expressing regret, and committing to change according to the Rambam. He adds: repeating the same sin after teshuvah doesn't mean the earlier teshuvah was invalid — it just means you need to do teshuvah again. And again. As many times as necessary. The door never closes. For sins between people, there's a FIFTH requirement: you must first make restitution or seek forgiveness from the person you wronged. G-d does not forgive interpersonal sins without the person forgiving you first. Learn more!
- Learn the Rambam's stunning ruling on free will — and how he deals with the philosophical problem that seems to make it impossible! 🧭 The Rambam writes: 'Know that every person has the ability to choose to be righteous like Moses or wicked like Jeroboam... This is a great fundamental principle and a pillar of Torah. If G-d decreed that a person would be righteous or wicked, or if there was something in a person's nature that forced them one way... how could teshuvah even exist?' Then he makes his strongest statement: 'This is a secret that cannot be fully understood according to human intelligence.' G-d knows what will happen (divine foreknowledge) — yet each person is completely free. The Rambam says this apparent contradiction is like asking how G-d exists without time — it's beyond our conceptual framework!
- Learn the Rambam's surprisingly specific health guidelines! 🍽️ Written over 900 years ago, many of the Rambam's (Maimonides) health guidelines in Hilchot De'ot 4 are remarkably validated by modern medicine. He teaches: 'Maintaining a healthy body is part of serving Hashem, because it is impossible to understand or know Hashem if one is ill.' Here's his practical wisdom: Don't eat until you're stuffed! Stop eating when about 75% full. Eat slowly and in a seated position. Avoid drinking liquids excessively during meals. Don't sleep immediately after eating; wait a few hours. Prioritize sleep during the night; daytime napping is generally discouraged. Exercise is essential – the Rambam asserts that lack of physical movement is a primary cause of illness. Avoid foods that are clearly harmful.
- Learn the critical distinction that most people miss about Yom Kippur — and why G-d cannot forgive you for hurting another person! 💡 The Mishnah makes a fundamental ruling: 'Yom Kippur atones for sins between a person and G-d. Yom Kippur does NOT atone for sins between one person and another — until the wrongdoer appeases the victim.' This is absolute. No amount of fasting, praying, or Kol Nidrei will earn you forgiveness for badmouthing your friend, embarrassing your coworker, or stealing from your neighbor — unless you: (1) Go to that person; (2) Acknowledge what you did; (3) Sincerely apologize; (4) Offer to make practical restitution if applicable. If the person refuses to forgive you, you must try three times. If they still don't forgive you, you don't need to try again, and now the sin is on THEM for not being forgiving. But you must actually try. The sages explain: G-d cannot grant forgiveness for damage done to another person. Heaven is not a party to the divine atonement — only the person wronged can release that debt.
- Learn the difference between kibbud (honor) and mora (reverence) for parents — two separate Torah commandments with completely different content! 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 KIBBUD AV VA'EM (honor): the Torah says 'honor your father and mother' (Exodus 20:12). The Rambam defines what kibbud requires in action: feeding your parents, giving them drink, dressing them, escorting them when they leave, helping them come in and go out. Kibbud is about meeting their practical needs, treating them like royalty. MORA AV VA'EM (reverence/fear): the Torah says 'every person shall revere his mother and father' (Leviticus 19:3). The Rambam defines mora in terms of what you DON'T do: don't stand in their designated place, don't sit in their seat, don't contradict them or side against them in a dispute, don't call them by their first name. Mora is about not diminishing their standing. Striking difference: kibbud may be paid for by the parent's money if the parent has means — you use their money to care for them. But if they have no money, the child must provide from their own funds!
- Learn the famous 8 levels of tzedakah — from lowest to highest! Yes, even when you give to someone in need there are many levels, depending on HOW you give (happily or grudgingly), if the people involved know each other or not (it's better to be anonymous), and so on! Learn these well and maximize your next tzedaka-giving opportunity! 🪜
- Learn the laws of lashon hara (evil speech) from the Chofetz Chaim.  -Submitted by Moshe
- Learn the specific conditions under which speaking negatively about someone is PERMITTED — because lashon hara has important exceptions! 👄 The Chofetz Chaim (Rabbi Yisrael Meir Kagan, the definitive authority on lashon hara law) rules that negative speech is permitted — and sometimes obligatory — when ALL seven conditions are met: (1) The information must be TRUE — you verified it yourself. (2) It must be NECESSARY — there's a genuine toelet (constructive purpose), not just sharing information. (3) You must first try to solve the problem by speaking directly to the person who wronged someone, before reporting to a third party. (4) The information shared must be PROPORTIONATE to the actual harm — don't exaggerate. (5) Your intention must be purely for the benefit, not for personal satisfaction or revenge. (6) The damage caused by revealing it must not be GREATER than the damage prevented. (7) There must be no other way to accomplish the goal. The classic permitted case: warning someone that their potential business partner is a known fraudster. You MUST tell them — withholding the information would cause harm. So the prohibition of lashon hara is not 'never say anything negative' — it's 'never say anything negative without a real, valid purpose.' Learn more!
- Learn the step-by-step process of bringing a korban (offering) — and why reversing even one step could invalidate the whole thing! 🐑 Bringing a korban was a precisely choreographed process with four essential acts, in a specific order: (1) Shechitah — the slaughter, performed by any Israelite (not just a Kohen!). Any adult Jewish male could slaughter the animal. (2) Kabbalah — the Kohen catches the animal's blood in a sacred vessel immediately. This step MUST be done by a Kohen. (3) Holachah — the Kohen carries the blood-filled vessel to the altar. (4) Zerikah — the Kohen sprinkles the blood on the altar. If the steps were done out of order (e.g., a non-Kohen performed the kabbalah, or the blood was placed before the zerikah) — the entire korban was invalid ('pasul'). Another fascinating rule: intent matters throughout. If the Kohen performing the service THOUGHT about eating the meat after its permitted time window (known as 'pigul') — that mental disqualification could invalidate the offering even though he hadn't done anything wrong physically! The Torah treated the mind of the Kohen as part of the sacred act itself. Learn more about how sacrifices were offered in the Rambam's Hilchot Ma'aseh HaKorbanot!
- Lechem mishneh is two whole loaves at Shabbat and Yom Tov meals — remembering double portion of manna before Shabbat in the desert. Cover the loaves, bless hamotzi, break bread for others. On Pesach, two whole matzot serve the role. The loaves should be whole and worthy of hamotzi.
- Legendary tier — thank you
for lighting up the world.
Keep Going!
- Leining is chanting the Torah portion from the scroll with precise trop (cantillation). The baal koreh trains for months. Congregants follow in a Chumash. Hearing every word on Shabbat morning fulfills the communal mitzvah of public Torah reading.
- Leishev baSukkah is the blessing "to dwell in the sukkah" — said when eating bread meals in the sukkah on Sukkot. Dwelling means spending meaningful time there, not only a quick snack. Rain may exempt you per halacha. Decorate the sukkah and make children feel at home in it — the booth recalls clouds of glory in the desert.
- Let go of grudges! 🕊️ Here's an amazing mitzvah: Even if you can't take revenge, don't hold onto the hurt either! The Torah's example: When someone asks to borrow something, don't say 'Sure, I'm not like you who refused me!' - just lend with a full heart! Did you know? Holding a grudge is like drinking poison and expecting the other person to get sick. Free yourself by letting go! Today's mission: Replace a grudge with a blessing.
- Level up your character: Study Pirkei Avot! 📚 Pirkei Avot, the 'Ethics of the Fathers,' is a treasure trove of timeless wisdom for daily life and middot (character development). Each short teaching offers profound, practical guidance on how to be a truly good person, treat others with kindness and integrity, and serve Hashem with humility and intention. These aren't just ancient sayings; they are foundational Jewish values that have shaped our ethical framework for over 2,000 years! Make it a weekly habit: pick your favorite lesson from any chapter and reflect on how you can apply its wisdom to your own life today. It's a spiritual workout for your soul! Source: Mishnah, Pirkei Avot; Talmud, Bava Kama 30a (regarding the importance of ethical conduct).
- Level up your day: Learn one halacha (Jewish law) daily! 📚 The Sages teach that consistent Torah learning – even just one practical halacha a day – is a powerful way to stay connected to Hashem and His profound wisdom throughout your life. It's like a daily spiritual vitamin! Pick a topic that sparks your curiosity: Shabbat laws, the intricate blessings on food, business ethics, Kashrut, or how to treat others with respect. Whether it's from a Mishnah, Gemara, Shulchan Aruch, or a contemporary sefer, small, steady learning builds an unshakable foundation for a meaningful Jewish life. Did you know? Daily Torah study, particularly of halacha, is considered one of the acts that 'has no measure' – meaning its spiritual reward is truly infinite!
- Levi is the tribe serving in the Temple; today leviim are often called to the Torah after kohanim. Many carry the family name Levi or related traditions. Kohanim and leviim have other halachot (priestly blessing, tumah). If unsure of your status, ask family and your rav.
- Light menorah each night
- Light up the night: Learn about Chanukah candles and Pirsumei Nisa (Publicizing the Miracle)! 🕎 The entire purpose of lighting Chanukah candles isn't just for personal enjoyment, but to publicly proclaim the miracle of the oil! This guiding principle shapes every halacha: PLACEMENT: Ideally, place your Chanukiah at the doorway of your home, on the *left* side (so the Mezuzah is on the right, and the Chanukiah on the left – you're surrounded by mitzvot!). If you live above the ground floor, a window visible from the street is the preferred spot. In times of danger, when public display wasn't safe, one would light indoors on a table. TIMING: Candles are lit around nightfall (Tzet HaKochavim) and must burn for at least 30 minutes into the night.
- Lighting candles on Friday afternoon is one of the most beloved practices in all of Judaism — it officially welcomes Shabbat into the home.

What it is:
Hadlakat Nerot (הַדְלָקַת נֵרוֹת — lighting of candles) is a rabbinic mitzvah performed weekly before Shabbat. The Shabbat candles bring light and peace into the home, honoring the day's holiness.

Who lights:
Traditionally the woman of the household lights. If there is no woman in the home, a man lights. Ashkenazi and Chabad custom: young girls and single daughters in the home may light their own candles with a bracha (often one candle per child). Sephardic custom (Shulchan Arukh; Rav Ovadia Yosef): daughters living under their parents' roof do not recite a bracha on their own candles if the mother has already lit — the mother's candles establish light for the home; a duplicate bracha would be a bracha levatala.

When to light:
Exactly 18 minutes before sunset on Friday (some Ashkenazic communities light 20-22 minutes before; Sephardic communities often light closer to sunset). The app shows the candle-lighting time for your location.

How many candles:
• The minimum is two candles — one for Zachor ("remember" Shabbat, from Exodus 20:8) and one for Shamor ("guard" Shabbat, from Deuteronomy 5:12)
• Many women add one candle per child

The blessing:
There are two customs:
• Ashkenazi custom: Light the candles first, then wave hands over the flames, cover the eyes, recite the blessing, and uncover. Lighting is done first because reciting the blessing is considered accepting Shabbat — after which relighting would be forbidden. Covering the eyes allows the blessing to precede benefiting from the light.
• Sephardi custom: Recite the blessing first, then light the candles.
Follow your family's custom. When in doubt, ask your rabbi.

The blessing: "Baruch Atah Ado-nai Eloheinu Melech ha'olam, asher kid'shanu b'mitzvotav v'tzivanu l'hadlik ner shel Shabbat."

Important:
Once you have lit the candles and said the blessing, Shabbat has begun for you. Do not light additional fire or use electricity after this point.
- Likely season in the Southern Hemisphere (Elul–Tishrei) — say the blessing when you actually see fruit trees blossoming.

- Location & Prayer Times
- Location (for prayer times)
- Location Access Needed
- Location permission was not granted.
- Loosening, leveling, or improving the earth in any way — plowing, digging, aerating — is forbidden. The prohibition extends to dragging heavy objects: dragging a massive iron or wooden lawn bench across soft dirt can easily carve a deep furrow and must be avoided. Standard baby strollers and wheelchairs, however, are fully permitted to be pushed across dirt or grass, as normal wheel tracking is not considered an act of plowing.
- Losh
- Losh — Kneading
- Losh — kneading melacha
- Maariv
- Maariv (Evening Prayer — Amidah)
- Maariv Shemoneh Esrei
- Maariv is the evening prayer service, instituted by our patriarch Yaakov (Jacob) and observed every night.

What it is:
Maariv (מַעֲרִיב — also called Arvit, עַרְבִית) is the evening prayer service. After the Shema and its blessings, we say the Amidah (Shemoneh Esrei) — the same standing prayer as in Shacharit and Mincha, though the evening version has some differences.

Structure of Maariv:
1. Ashrei and a short passage (in some traditions)
2. Barchu — the call to prayer (בָּרְכוּ — "Bless G-d"), said when praying with a minyan of 10 men
3. Evening Shema with its two blessings before and two after
4. Amidah / Shemoneh Esrei
5. Aleinu
6. Mourner's Kaddish (said by mourners or those observing yahrzeit)

Timing:
Earliest Maariv is at sunset (shkiyah). Many communities ideally daven after nightfall (tzeit hakochavim). If you daven Maariv before tzeit, repeat the Shema (without its blessings) once it is fully dark.
- Maariv is the evening service after nightfall — Shema, Amidah, and on Motzei Shabbat Havdalah or festival inserts. Saturday night Maariv may include Vatodi'enu when Yom Tov begins. Weekday Maariv is when many working Jews connect with community after the day. Consistency at Maariv builds a rhythm of ending the day with G-d.
- Maariv — Maariv is the evening service after nightfall — Shema, Amidah, and on Motzei Shabbat Havdalah or festival inserts. Saturday night Maariv may include Vatodi'enu when Yom Tov begins. Weekday Maariv is when many working Jews connect with community after the day. Consistency at Maariv builds a rhythm of ending the day with G-d.
- Maariv — available from {time} (sunset).{plagNote}
- Maariv — earliest $startTime (sunset).$plagNote$shemaRepeatNote
- Maariv — earliest {time} (sunset). Ideal for many: {tzeitTime}.{plagNote}{shemaNote}
- Maaser kesafim is setting aside about ten percent of net income for tzedakah after expenses. It trains that income is entrusted, not owned absolutely. Priorities: local poor, Torah learning, Israel. If you cannot give ten percent without hardship, give what you can and ask your rav.
- Machatzit HaShekel
- Machatzit HaShekel recalls the half-shekel each Jew gave for Temple upkeep in Adar. Today many give charity before Purim — often three coins half of a local unit. It is a widespread custom, not one of the four Purim mitzvot, but it connects the community to unified redemption. Give through your shul or reputable charity.
- Machatzit HaShekel — Machatzit HaShekel recalls the half-shekel each Jew gave for Temple upkeep in Adar. Today many give charity before Purim — often three coins half of a local unit. It is a widespread custom, not one of the four Purim mitzvot, but it connects the community to unified redemption. Give through your shul or reputable charity.
- Machloket is respectful disagreement between Torah authorities — often recorded in the Talmud. Not every machloket is settled; both sides may be valid. The mitzvah is to follow your rav's psak in practice while honoring that another community may follow a different legitimate view. Machloket l'shem shamayim is for truth; argument for ego is destructive.
- Mafshit — Skinning
- Mah Nishtanah
- Mah Nishtanah ("Why is this night different?") is the Four Questions — usually asked by the youngest child at the Seder. The Haggadah answers each question with Torah, story, and mitzvot. The point is engagement: children (and adults) should feel the Exodus personally, not only hear a lecture.
- Mah Nishtanah — Mah Nishtanah ("Why is this night different?") is the Four Questions — usually asked by the youngest child at the Seder. The Haggadah answers each question with Torah, story, and mitzvot. The point is engagement: children (and adults) should feel the Exodus personally, not only hear a lecture.
- Maisach — Warping a Loom
- Major & minor holidays, Yom Tov, and fast days
- Major Holidays & Yom Tov
- Make a difference: Give Tzedakah (charity) — even a small coin! 💸 The Torah teaches that every act of giving, no matter how small, has immense power to change the world for the better! Giving tzedakah is not just about helping the needy; it's a profound spiritual act that connects us to Hashem, who is the ultimate Giver. Even putting a single coin into a tzedakah box with a sincere heart can bring tremendous blessings and elevate your spiritual energy. The Rambam (Hilchot Matanot Aniyim 10:1) states that the mitzvah of tzedakah is equal in weight to all other mitzvot combined! So, next time you see a tzedakah box, remember the immense power in that small act of giving.
- Make an appointment for a private audience with the King of Kings! 👑 The Shemoneh Esrei—also known as the Amidah—is the heart of every Jewish prayer service. Our Sages composed it with "Ruach HaKodesh" (Divine Inspiration), meaning every word is a precise key designed to unlock heavenly gates and bring down spiritual and material blessings! 🔓 Because it is a direct conversation with Hashem, the Halacha has some "royal protocol" rules to keep it focused. First, you have to stand with your feet together, mimicking the angels who have "one leg," to show total devotion (and that you ain't goin' nowhere while talking to the King!). 😇 You also face Jerusalem—no matter where you are in the world—to point your heart toward the site of the Holy Temple! 🏛️ The prayer is split into three sections: three blessings of praise, thirteen requests for things like wisdom, health, and prosperity, and three final blessings of gratitude. On Shabbat and holidays, the format of the middle blessings are changed, so you make sure you're reading the correct Amidah from your siddur! 📜 While concentration (Kavanah) is great to have throughout, the Sages say it is most critical in the very first blessing of "Avot"—if you lose focus there, you’ve missed the core of the encounter (Ashkenazim don't go back to repeat, but Sephardim do)! 🕊️ Enter a different dimension and bring down all the blessings Hashem wants to give you! Learn more! 🎊🙏.
- Make every day special! Take a moment to think about the upcoming Shabbat 🌅. Maybe plan a special dish you'll make, or imagine the peaceful atmosphere you'll create. Did you know? It's a mitzvah to remember Shabbat every day!
- Makeh B'patish
- Makeh B'patish — Final Blow
- Makeh B'patish — final finishing touch melacha
- Makeup Prayers
- Makeup prayer
- Male
- Maleh lugmov (מְלֹא לֻגְמָיו — a cheekful, approximately 60 ml / ~2 fl oz) is what you drink after the Kiddush or Havdalah blessing — most of a revi'it, but not the full cup. The cup itself must still hold at least a revi'it of wine or grape juice. Grape juice counts like wine for many poskim.
- Many poskim and communities hold that women should say Birchot HaShachar upon waking — the same sequence of morning blessings men recite at the start of Shacharit, thanking G-d for the gifts of each new day.

What they are:
Birchot HaShachar (בִּרְכוֹת הַשַּׁחַר — Blessings of the Dawn) is a sequence of ~15 short blessings. They originated as blessings said at each stage of waking — opening your eyes, standing up, getting dressed, and stepping outside.

Women's wording:
Use your siddur's women's version. Instead of "who has not made me a woman," women say "who has made me according to His will." The other blessings are the same.

When to say them:
• From chatzos (halachic midnight) onward.
• Ideally say HaNoten LaSechvi Vinah (the rooster blessing) after alot hashachar; if you said it after chatzos, you have still fulfilled your obligation.
• The other blessings may be said from chatzos if you wake up very early. Ideally say them upon waking.

Even if you do not daven full Shacharit, many say these blessings are an important way to start the day with gratitude.

Ask your rabbi or rebbetzin about your community's practice.
- Maoz Tzur
- Maoz Tzur — "Rock of Ages," Chanukah hymn sung after lighting
- March
- Maror is bitter herb at the Seder — horseradish root or romaine — reminding the bitterness of slavery. A kezayit is required at the Seder. Charoset's sweetness is dipped with maror in Korech (Hillel's sandwich). Prepare enough fresh maror; grated horseradish loses strength quickly.
- Married
- Married Jewish women cover their hair when in public as a sign of modesty (tzniut) and to preserve the special nature of marriage.
            
            The manner of covering varies by community - some use wigs, others use scarves, hats, or a combination.
            
            This practice is based on biblical and rabbinic sources and is considered a fundamental aspect of Jewish law for married women.
- Married Jewish women in Orthodox communities traditionally cover their hair in public and in the presence of men other than their husband.

What it is:
Kisui rosh (כִּיסּוּי רֹאשׁ — literally "covering of the head") is one of the fundamental practices of Jewish married life across Orthodox communities.

Halachic status:
Poskim disagree on its source. Shulchan Aruch (Even HaEzer 115:4) requires married women to cover their hair. The practice is widely accepted in Orthodox life; ask your rav for guidance.

Why:
In communities that require covering, a married woman's hair is treated as something private that should not be publicly displayed. Covering expresses that her deepest beauty is reserved for the privacy of her home and her husband. It is also an expression of tzniut (modesty) — the Jewish value of inner dignity over external display.

Methods of covering:
• Tichel (טִיכֶל) — a headscarf or wrap, commonly used across many communities
• Sheitel (שֵׁייטֶל) — a wig; widely used in Ashkenazic and Chassidic communities
• Hat or beret
• Mitpachot — decorative wraps and turbans
• Combination (e.g., small wig with a hat over it)

Standards:
Most Orthodox authorities require the entire hair to be covered in public. Details vary by community. Speak with your rabbi or rebbetzin about what applies in your situation.
- Married women's mitzvot
- Marvel at the miracle of breath! Notice how G-d designed our bodies to breathe automatically 🫁, giving us life with almost no effort. Each breath is a gift - try taking one deep, mindful breath right now and thank G-d for it!
- Marvel at the power of wisdom! Thank G-d for giving you the ability to learn and teach 📚. Every Torah idea you grasp and every insight you share with others creates ripples of positive change in the world. Your mind is like a divine gift that keeps growing stronger the more you use it! 🎓
- Mashiach
- Mashiach (Moshiach) is the righteous anointed king from David's line who will rebuild the Temple, gather the exiles, and bring universal knowledge of G-d. Belief in his coming is a foundation of Jewish faith. We pray for redemption daily.
- Mashiach — Mashiach (Moshiach) is the righteous anointed king from David's line who will rebuild the Temple, gather the exiles, and bring universal knowledge of G-d. Belief in his coming is a foundation of Jewish faith. We pray for redemption daily.
- Master #6 of the Constant Mitzvot: Don't be led astray by your eyes and heart! 👀❤️ This mitzvah (Numbers 15:39) reminds us to guard our thoughts and desires from pulling us away from Torah values and Hashem. It's an ongoing spiritual battle! When you notice yourself being drawn towards something that doesn't align with kedusha (holiness) or when your mind wanders into non-Torah philosophies, you have an immediate opportunity to fulfill this mitzvah. Staying focused on Torah, its wisdom, and its immense rewards is the ultimate key to true happiness and connection. This mitzvah requires constant vigilance and self-control. Source: Numbers 15:39; Rambam, Sefer HaMitzvot, Negative Commandment 3; Mishneh Torah, Avodat Kochavim 2:3.
- Master the art of Birkat HaMazon! 🍽️ Did you know? This isn't just a 'thanks for the food' - it's a four-course spiritual meal of blessings! The rabbis teach us it's a segula for wealth to wash and have a bread meal every day! And some say if you say the entire thing with intention you will be saved from all harm. 😊
- Master the art of loving rebuke! 💝 The Torah gives us an amazing tool: If you see someone doing something wrong, don't just stay quiet or gossip about it - help them grow! Here's the key: Speak privately, gently, and make it clear you're coming from a place of love. Did you know? The word for rebuke (תוכחה) is related to the word for proof (הוכחה), teaching us to help others see proof of a better way! Today's challenge: If you need to give feedback, do it with kindness and wisdom.
- Matan Torah
- Matan Torah — receiving the Torah at Mount Sinai, celebrated on Shavuot
- Matana al menat lehachzir (מַתָּנָה עַל מְנָת לְהַחְזִיר) is a "gift on condition it is returned." The recipient legally owns the item for the moment it is in their hands, then returns it afterward. On the first day of Sukkot, if you do not own a lulav and etrog, someone may gift you their set conditionally so you fulfill the Torah obligation of ownership for the mitzvah, then you return it. This is not used for mechirat chametz — selling chametz requires an absolute sale.
- Matanot la'evyonim (מתנות לאביונים) helps every Jew celebrate Purim with food and joy (Esther 9:22).

The mitzvah (Peninei Halakha 05-16-03):
• Give at least one gift to each of two different poor people (minimum of two recipients total) during Purim daytime.
• Each gift should enable a modest Purim meal — money is common.

How to do it:
• Give during Purim daytime only (not at night); many give after the daytime Megillah reading (follow your minhag).
• Ideally before your Purim seudah so recipients can use it for the meal.
• You may give through a trustworthy messenger, gemach, or organization that distributes on Purim day — verify funds reach the poor that day
• If you cannot find recipients, ask your rabbi or shul — many collect on Purim morning

Who qualifies: Someone who lacks resources for Purim — your rav can guide you if unsure.
- Matanot la'evyonim are gifts sent to the poor on Purim day so they can rejoice too: at least one gift to each of two different poor people (minimum of two recipients total). Money is common; food or vouchers work if they can use them that day. Unlike mishloach manot, this mitzvah focuses on need, not friendship. Many shuls collect on Purim morning; verify distribution happens on Purim itself (and on the same day you are celebrating it, i.e. on Shushan Purim).
- Matanot la'evyonim — from dawn ({time}) until sunset.
- Matanot la'evyonim — from dawn until sunset.
- Matanot la'evyonim — set location for dawn times.
- Matanot la'evyonim — set location for sunset times.
- Matir — Untying
- Matzah is unleavened bread eaten all Pesach and central at the Seder — at least a kezayit when hamotzi is said, again for the afikoman. It recalls haste leaving Egypt. Shmurah matzah is guarded from moisture; many use it at the Seder. Store carefully — broken matzah still counts if kezayit-sized pieces remain.
- Mav'ir — Kindling
- Mavir
- Mavir (מַבְעִיר) is creating a new flame — striking a match, flipping an electronic switch, or starting a fire from scratch. It is strictly forbidden on both Shabbat and Yom Tov. On Yom Tov, however, you may transfer an existing flame (from a candle lit before Yom Tov) to cook, light Yom Tov candles, or for festival needs (ochel nefesh). That distinction — new fire vs. transferring fire — is central to Yom Tov laws.
- Mavir — Mavir (מַבְעִיר) is creating a new flame — striking a match, flipping an electronic switch, or starting a fire from scratch. It is strictly forbidden on both Shabbat and Yom Tov. On Yom Tov, however, you may transfer an existing flame (from a candle lit before Yom Tov) to cook, light Yom Tov candles, or for festival needs (ochel nefesh). That distinction — new fire vs. transferring fire — is central to Yom Tov laws.
- May
- May it be Your will, Lord our God and God of our ancestors, that You lead us toward peace, guide our footsteps toward peace, and make us reach our desired destination for life, gladness, and peace.
                May You rescue us from the hand of every foe, ambush along the way, and from all manner of punishments that assemble to come to earth.
                May You send blessing in our handiwork, and grant us grace, kindness, and mercy in Your eyes and in the eyes of all who see us.
                May You hear the voice of our supplication because You are God Who hears prayer and supplication.
                Blessed are You, Lord, Who hears prayer.
- May kindle from a pre-existing flame only — not from scratch
- Mayim achronim is washing the fingertips after a bread meal and before bentching — a reminder that we eat as servants before G-d. Not every community emphasizes it today, but many siddurim and bentchers include it. It is separate from netilat yadayim before the meal.
- Mazel Tov!
- Me'abeid — Tanning
- Me'ameir — Gathering
- Meat / Dairy Wait Times
- Mechabeh
- Mechabeh — Extinguishing
- Mechabeh — extinguishing fire melacha
- Mechateich — Cutting to Size
- Mechirat chametz is selling chametz you want to keep to a non-Jew through your rabbi before Pesach so it is not yours during the holiday. Authorize the sale well before Erev Pesach — most rabbis stop accepting forms by the night before, even though ownership transfers on Erev Pesach morning. It must be an absolute sale, not a conditional gift (matana al menat lehachzir). Chametz sold should be stored away. When including year-round dishes, rabbis typically sell only the absorbed chametz flavor in the vessels — not the physical dishes themselves — to avoid requiring tevilat kelim after Pesach. Shortly after Pesach, ownership reverts per the contract your rabbi uses.
- Meet the holy Kuf (ק)! 👣 This unique letter teaches about holiness and physicality. Its shape - like someone walking - shows how we must bring holiness into our physical actions. Did you know? The word Kadosh (holy) starts with Kuf, teaching that true holiness means elevating the physical world, not escaping it! Today's mission: Make one physical activity holy by doing it mindfully for a higher purpose
- Meet the miraculous Samech (ס)! 🔄 The Samech's shape holds a secret: Unlike other letters, it's completely enclosed - teaching about G-d's encompassing protection. Did you know? The Talmud says it miraculously floated in the Tablets of the Ten Commandments! Since the tablets were engraved all the way through, as a circle its inside had no connection to the Tablet! Today's challenge: Feel G-d's protective presence surrounding you
- Meet the towering Lamed (ל)! 🗼 This unique letter reaches higher than all others, representing the aspiration to reach beyond our limits! Did you know? Lamed means 'learn' and 'teach' - showing that true learning should lead to teaching others! Its shape suggests a tower pointing heavenward, reminding us to always strive higher in our spiritual growth. Today's challenge: Learn something new and share it with someone else.
- Megillah
- Megillah means scroll; on Purim it is Esther's book read twice — night and day. Both readings are obligatory; the daytime reading is the primary fulfillment (mitzvat ha'yom), while the night reading was instituted later. Hearing every word is the mitzvah; noise at Haman's name is custom. Shehecheyanu: Ashkenazim recite before the first evening and first daytime readings (Rema O.C. 692:1); Sephardim recite only at night — the night blessing covers the day (Shulchan Arukh; Yalkut Yosef). If you miss words, ask whether you must hear that section again.
- Megillah reading
- Megillah reading — after nightfall (tzeit).
- Megillah reading — after nightfall ({time}).
- Megillah reading — hearing the Book of Esther read on Purim
- Megillah — Megillah means scroll; on Purim it is Esther's book read twice — night and day. Both readings are obligatory; the daytime reading is the primary fulfillment (mitzvat ha'yom), while the night reading was instituted later. Hearing every word is the mitzvah; noise at Haman's name is custom. Shehecheyanu: Ashkenazim recite before the first evening and first daytime readings (Rema O.C. 692:1); Sephardim recite only at night — the night blessing covers the day (Shulchan Arukh; Yalkut Yosef). If you miss words, ask whether you must hear that section again.
- Melaben
- Melaben — Laundering
- Melaben — laundering melacha
- Melacha
- Melacha is transformative labor forbidden on Shabbat — including cooking. On Yom Tov most of the 39 melachot remain forbidden (writing, building, kindling a new fire, etc.), but the Torah permits ochel nefesh — preparing food for that festival day, including cooking transferred from a pre-existing flame (Exodus 12:16). You may not cook on Yom Tov for the next day without eruv tavshilin when Shabbat follows. When unsure, ask your rav before acting.
- Melacha — Melacha is transformative labor forbidden on Shabbat — including cooking. On Yom Tov most of the 39 melachot remain forbidden (writing, building, kindling a new fire, etc.), but the Torah permits ochel nefesh — preparing food for that festival day, including cooking transferred from a pre-existing flame (Exodus 12:16). You may not cook on Yom Tov for the next day without eruv tavshilin when Shabbat follows. When unsure, ask your rav before acting.
- Melave Malka
- Melave Malka (festive meal after Shabbat)
- Melave Malka (מְלַוֶּה מַלְכָּה) means "escorting the queen" — a meal Saturday night to escort the Shabbat Queen as she departs, the way one would honor a beloved guest at the door (Talmud Shabbat 119b). It is also called Seudat David HaMelech: King David held a Motzei Shabbat feast each week after learning he would die on Shabbat (Shabbat 30a).

When:
After Havdalah, from tzeit (nightfall) until dawn Sunday. Men and women; all nusachim.

What to do:
• Spread a clean tablecloth and set the table — the sages say to set the table even if you will eat only a small amount
• Sit and eat at the table. The ideal is a meal with bread: wash hands (netilat yadayim), say hamotzi, and eat. If you are too full for bread, you can still fulfill the mitzvah with cake or another meaningful portion of food — netilat yadayim applies only when you eat bread
• When you can, prepare something for this meal rather than only finishing Shabbat leftovers
• Many light candles on or near the table

Four candles (optional — Baal Shem Tov, Siddur Tzelota D'Yisrael; Sefer Amrei Esh):
Some light four candles at Melave Malka. Chassidic tradition teaches that King David is spiritually present at this meal. A common practice (Rabbi Aaron Roth, Shulchan HaTahor): dedicate one candle each to King David, Eliyahu HaNavi, Rabbi Meir Baal HaNess, and the Baal Shem Tov — or choose any four tzaddikim. Light them at the table, name each dedication, and ask then for your needs for the week ahead in the merit of the tzaddikim.

If you are truly too full from Shabbat, you are not required to force yourself — but even a little food at a set table honors the mitzvah.
- Melave Malka — after Havdalah, from tzeit hakochavim through dawn.
- Melave Malka — after Havdalah, from {time} through dawn.
- Melave Malka — escorting the Shabbat Queen — Motzei Shabbat meal (מְלַוֶּה מַלְכָּה) through dawn Sunday
- Memachek — Smoothing
- Menapetz — Combing Fibers
- Meraked — Sifting
- Mesarteit — Scoring Lines
- Mezonot
- Mezonot — The blessing said over grain-based foods that aren't bread — like cake, pastry, pasta, or crackers — thanking G-d for grain sustenance (borei minei mezonot).
- Mezuzot on your doorposts
- Midrash is rabbinic teaching that explores Torah stories, ethics, and values — often answering questions the plain text leaves open. Midrash Rabbah and similar collections are not the same as halacha codes; they inspire and teach. Distinguish midrash from pshat (straightforward meaning) when learning Chumash.
- Mikra Megillah (hearing the Book of Esther) is a rabbinic mitzvah instituted for Purim (Esther 9:28; Megillah 19a). Men and women are equally obligated.

When to hear it:
• Once on Purim evening — after nightfall (tzeit)
• Once on Purim day — you are halachically obligated to hear the Megillah during the daytime (usually right after morning Shacharit and before sunset). The daytime reading is the primary fulfillment of the mitzvah (mitzvat ha'yom); the night reading was instituted later — both are required.

How to fulfill:
• Hear every word read from a kosher megillah scroll by someone who can discharge your obligation (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 690)
• Stand for the blessings; customs when Haman's name is read vary by shul (many make noise; sitting vs standing — follow your community)
• Listen without talking — missed words may require hearing that passage again; ask your rabbi if unsure

Blessings before reading:
• Al mikra megillah
• She'asa nissim
• Shehecheyanu (recited on the first night; Ashkenazim recite it by day as well)

Machatzit haShekel: A widespread pre-Purim custom (not one of the four Purim mitzvot in the same way); many give before Megillah — follow your community. Confirm local reading times with your shul.

Prayers & meals:
• Insert Al HaNissim into every Amidah and into Birkat Hamazon (bentching) all day long on Purim.
- Mincha
- Mincha (Afternoon Prayer)
- Mincha (Chabad / Nusach Ari): The communal service begins with Ashrei — not Korbanot (Sefer HaMinhagim, Mincha). The gabbai or chazan opens the minyan with Psalm 145; the congregation does not wait for or jointly recite Korbanot at Mincha.

Korbanot privately (optional): The Alter Rebbe (Shulchan Arukh HaRav, O.C. 234, Kuntres Acharon 2) writes that after Mincha Gedolah an individual who is meticulous may read the Tamid and Ketoret passages on his own — a personal stringency (midat chasidut), not part of the communal service. Hayom Yom (11 Nissan) treats this as private preparation before joining the minyan.

Tachanun (Chabad): At Mincha, typically the shorter Tachanun (Vayomer David el Gad). Omitted on Rosh Chodesh, Yom Tov, Chanukah, Purim, and other special occasions — Mincha itself is still said.
- Mincha (Edot HaMizrach): The service opens with Korbanot (Parshat HaTamid), then Ashrei, the Amidah, Tachanun on weekdays, and Aleinu — per your kehilla's siddur.

Tachanun (Edot HaMizrach): At Mincha, recited after the Amidah including Vidui (Ashamnu) and the Thirteen Attributes. Following the Arizal (per Kaf HaChaim and Yalkut Yosef), do not perform nefilat apayim at Mincha — recite sitting upright, including Psalm 25 (LeDavid Alecha). Omitted on Rosh Chodesh, holidays, and standard festive days.
- Mincha (Sephardi): The service opens with Korbanot (Parshat HaTamid), then Ashrei, the Amidah, Tachanun on weekdays, and Aleinu (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 232; follow your siddur).

Tachanun (Sephardi): At Mincha, recited after the Amidah where applicable. Rav Ovadia Yosef (Yechaveh Daat 6:7) and the Ben Ish Chai rule against physical nefilat apayim — recite sitting upright. Omitted on Rosh Chodesh, holidays, and standard festive days.
- Mincha - Shemoneh Esrei/Tachanun
- Mincha is the afternoon prayer — brief, powerful, and often said in the middle of the working day.

What Mincha is:
Mincha (מִנְחָה — originally the name of the afternoon grain offering in the Temple) is the afternoon prayer service. It was instituted by our patriarch Yitzchak (Isaac), who went to the field to pray in the afternoon (Genesis 24:63, per the Talmud).

What the Mincha service contains:
• Ashrei (Psalm 145) — see morning prayer notes
• Amidah / Shemoneh Esrei — the same 19-blessing standing prayer (see above)
• Tachanun — on most weekdays (see below)
• Aleinu — a closing prayer affirming G-d's sovereignty over the world

Tachanun (weekdays):
Tachanun (תַּחֲנוּן — supplication) is a penitential prayer after the Amidah on most ordinary weekdays at Mincha — usually the shorter form (often beginning with "Vayomer David el Gad"). It is omitted on Rosh Chodesh, Yom Tov, Chanukah, Purim, and other days of joy. Follow your siddur or congregation.

Timing:
Mincha begins from about 30 minutes after midday (Mincha Gedolah). The latest time to start the Amidah depends on nusach: Ashkenazi (Gra) — ideal before sunset, bedi'eved until about 15 minutes after sunset; Sephardi / Edot HaMizrach — often may begin shortly before sunset even if the Amidah continues after sunset, until about 15 minutes after sunset; Chabad — highly optimal before sunset, but custom permits Mincha during twilight until nightfall (tzeit). The app shows your current window.
- Mincha is the afternoon service, ideally before sunset. On weekdays it is short; on Shabbat and Yom Tov it includes Torah reading (often) and Musaf on festivals. Friday Mincha is special — it enters Shabbat. Missing Mincha requires tashlumin at Maariv when possible. Working people often pray Mincha at shul or a quiet office corner.
- Mincha — Mincha is the afternoon service, ideally before sunset. On weekdays it is short; on Shabbat and Yom Tov it includes Torah reading (often) and Musaf on festivals. Friday Mincha is special — it enters Shabbat. Missing Mincha requires tashlumin at Maariv when possible. Working people often pray Mincha at shul or a quiet office corner.
- Minhag
- Minhag — A minhag is a binding community or family custom rooted in Jewish life — not merely "what people happen to do." Minhag can shape prayer text (nusach), mourning practices, Pesach stringencies, and holiday joy. When a minhag is established with rabbinic approval in a community, it often carries nearly the weight of law for that community. If you are new or between communities, ask your rav which minhagim to follow.
- Minimum Pesukei D'Zimra
- Minor Holidays & Fast Days
- Minor holiday
- Minor holiday — "little Purim" in a leap year (Tachanun omitted)
- Minor holiday — 33rd day of the Omer
- Minor holiday — New Year for Trees
- Minor holiday — Second Passover
- Misheyakir (מִשֶּׁיַּכִּיר) is when there is enough natural ambient light to recognize a casual acquaintance from about four amot (~6 feet). The earliest time to recite the bracha on tallit and tefillin is misheyakir — often roughly 35–50 minutes after alot hashachar (varies by season and location). Before misheyakir, you cannot recite the blessing l'chatchila, though putting them on after dawn (alot hashachar) still fulfills the core obligation bedieved in a case of great need. Mishnah Berurah recommends waiting until misheyakir, then putting on tallit and tefillin and reciting Krias Shema and the Amidah; many shuls daven until Yishtabach without them, then wait for misheyakir. The Gemara (Berachot 14b) compares Shema without tallit and tefillin to false testimony — avoid if you can wait. If you must leave very early: Igros Moshe (O.C. 4:6) permits wearing them after alot hashachar without a bracha, davening, then moving them slightly and reciting the brachos after misheyakir. Preference: (1) tallit and tefillin after misheyakir, Amidah at sunrise; (2) after misheyakir, Amidah before sunrise; (3) only if necessary — after alot hashachar without bracha, brachos after misheyakir.
- Mishloach Manot
- Mishloach Manot — Mishloach manot are ready-to-eat food gifts sent to at least one Jewish friend on Purim day — minimally two different foods in one package.
- Mishloach manot (משלוח מנות) — sending portions of food — increases friendship and joy on Purim (Esther 9:19).

The mitzvah (Rambam, Shulchan Arukh 695:4; Aish; Peninei Halakha 05-16-04):
• Send at least two different ready-to-eat foods or drinks to one friend on Purim day — one mishloach manot package.
• Women are equally obligated; many send to a woman friend and men to a man (Rema). Sending to additional friends is praiseworthy.
• Examples: wine and cookies, fruit and pastry — clearly two types, not one combined dish.

How to do it:
• Deliver on Purim day before sunset — by you or a messenger (shul lists, kids, or neighbors are fine)
• Food should be ready to eat without cooking
• Label sender and recipient; mishloach manot should be identifiable

Tips: You need not reciprocate every package you receive the same day. Plan ahead on Erev Purim so deliveries are not rushed at the last minute.
- Mishloach manot are ready-to-eat food gifts sent to at least one Jewish friend on Purim day — minimally two different foods in one package.
- Mishnah
- Mishnah Berurah
- Mishnah Berurah is Rabbi Yisrael Meir Kagan's detailed commentary on Orach Chaim — daily halacha for prayer, Shabbat, and festivals. It explains Shulchan Aruch with practical rulings. Many Ashkenazim rely on it for home practice. It pairs with the Chofetz Chaim's other works on speech and mitzvot.
- Mishnah Berurah — Mishnah Berurah is Rabbi Yisrael Meir Kagan's detailed commentary on Orach Chaim — daily halacha for prayer, Shabbat, and festivals. It explains Shulchan Aruch with practical rulings. Many Ashkenazim rely on it for home practice. It pairs with the Chofetz Chaim's other works on speech and mitzvot.
- Mishnah — The Mishnah is the first major written compilation of the Oral Torah, edited around 200 CE. It organizes halacha by topic (Shabbat, blessings, damages, and so on) in concise Hebrew. The Gemara and Talmud Yerushalmi explain and debate the Mishnah. Beginners often start Mishnah before full Talmud.

- Mocheik — Erasing
- Modah
- Modah — feminine form of Modeh (grateful) in Modeh Ani
- Modeh Ani
- Modeh Ani (Thank G-d upon waking)
- Modeh Ani is the first words many Jews say upon waking from primary nighttime sleep: it gives thanks to the living and eternal King for returning the soul with compassion. Say it in bed before getting up, without washing first. It is typically not recited after a daytime nap — then wash hands (negel vasser). You may repeat the words anytime as personal gratitude, but that is not the official morning prayer. Men say Modeh; women often say Modah.
- Modeh Ani — Modeh Ani is the first words many Jews say upon waking from primary nighttime sleep: it gives thanks to the living and eternal King for returning the soul with compassion. Say it in bed before getting up, without washing first. It is typically not recited after a daytime nap — then wash hands (negel vasser). You may repeat the words anytime as personal gratitude, but that is not the official morning prayer. Men say Modeh; women often say Modah.

- Motzei
- Motzei (~2h after tzeit)
- Motzei Shabbat
- Motzei Shabbat is Saturday night after Shabbat ends.
- Motzei Shabbat — Motzei Shabbat is Saturday night after Shabbat ends.
- Motzei Yom Kippur meal — after nightfall (tzeit).
- Motzei Yom Kippur meal — after nightfall ({time}).
- Motzei uses tzeit + 2 hours for your simulated location (time-of-day chips apply to erev/day only).
- Moving an object from a private domain to a public domain, or vice versa, or carrying an object four cubits (~6 feet) within a public domain. Most modern streets are rabbinically classified as a semi-public area (karmelit), where carrying is still restricted. Communities establish an eruv — a symbolic enclosure — to permit carrying within a shared area. Without an eruv, a house key can only be worn outdoors if it is structurally integrated by a specialist into a functional piece of clothing or jewelry (such as a belt clasp or decorative pin). Simply hanging a loose key on a string around your neck is a direct violation of carrying.
- Muktzeh
- Muktzeh means "set aside" — objects not used on Shabbat because they are not needed for Shabbat itself (tools, money, phones). The categories are detailed: some items may not be moved at all; others may be moved for a Shabbat need or to use the place they occupy. Learning basic muktzeh prevents accidental violations when tidying or handling objects on Shabbat.
- Muktzeh refers to items that are "set aside" and may not be moved on Shabbat. Unlike the 39 melachot which come from the Mishkan's construction, Muktzeh is a Rabbinic enactment to protect the spirit of Shabbat rest.

The main principle: any object whose primary purpose is forbidden on Shabbat — like a pen, hammer, or smartphone — may generally be moved only to use the space it occupies, or if you need it for a Shabbat-permitted purpose.

Items that are completely non-functional on Shabbat (like money, rocks, or broken objects that lost utility before Shabbat) generally may not be moved at all. To prepare, store wallets, work tools, and electronics out of reach before Shabbat begins.
- Muktzeh — Muktzeh means "set aside" — objects not used on Shabbat because they are not needed for Shabbat itself (tools, money, phones). The categories are detailed: some items may not be moved at all; others may be moved for a Shabbat need or to use the place they occupy. Learning basic muktzeh prevents accidental violations when tidying or handling objects on Shabbat.
- Musaf
- Musaf ($label) — after the morning Amidah, from sunrise.
- Musaf (Additional prayer on special days)
- Musaf (only on Rosh Chodesh, Festivals, and Shabbat)
- Musaf ({label}) — after the morning Amidah, from {time}.
- Musaf is an additional Amidah prayer added to the morning service on Shabbat, Rosh Chodesh, and all Jewish holidays.

What Musaf is:
Musaf (מוּסָף — literally "additional") is an extra Amidah — the same standing prayer as the rest of the service — but with a special middle blessing describing the Temple offerings (korbanot) that were brought on that day in Temple times, and a prayer for the restoration of the Temple service.

When Musaf is said:
• Every Shabbat
• Rosh Chodesh (new month)
• All Yom Tov holidays
• Chol HaMoed (intermediate days of Pesach and Sukkot)

Why we say it:
In Temple times, special extra offerings (musaf korbanot) were brought on these holy days. Since the Temple was destroyed, we replace the physical offerings with prayer — reciting what was offered and praying for the Temple to be rebuilt.

Timing:
Said after Shacharit (the main morning service), ideally before the end of the 7th halachic hour; bedi'eved valid until sunset (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 286:1).
- Musaf is only on Shabbat, Festivals, and Rosh Chodesh — not a regular weekday.
- Musaf is the "additional" Amidah remembering the extra Temple offerings (musaf offerings) brought on Shabbat, Rosh Chodesh, and Yom Tov. Its central blessing describes what was offered and prays for the Temple's restoration. Without the Temple, prayer replaces sacrifice — Musaf is how we still connect to that service.
- Musaf window has closed (sunset).$shabbatNote
- Musaf window has closed (sunset).{shabbatNote}
- Musaf — Musaf is the "additional" Amidah remembering the extra Temple offerings (musaf offerings) brought on Shabbat, Rosh Chodesh, and Yom Tov. Its central blessing describes what was offered and prays for the Temple's restoration. Without the Temple, prayer replaces sacrifice — Musaf is how we still connect to that service.
- Mussar
- Mussar is the Jewish discipline of ethical and character development — refining anger, arrogance, laziness, and speech through study and practice. Classic works include Mesillat Yesharim and Ale Shur. It complements halacha (what to do) with work on who you are becoming.
- Mussar — Mussar is the Jewish discipline of ethical and character development — refining anger, arrogance, laziness, and speech through study and practice. Classic works include Mesillat Yesharim and Ale Shur. It complements halacha (what to do) with work on who you are becoming.
- My prayer tradition (Nusach)
- Nachum Ish Gamzu
- Nachum Ish Gamzu — sage known for finding good in every situation
- Ne'ilah
- Ne'ilah ("closing") is the final prayer service of Yom Kippur as the gates of repentance close. The ark often stays open; the congregation pleads intensely. Many have the custom to say Shema Yisrael together at the end. After Ne'ilah and nightfall (tzeit), one prays Maariv, makes Havdalah, and breaks the fast. It is the spiritual peak of the Ten Days of Teshuvah.
- Ne'ilah — Ne'ilah ("closing") is the final prayer service of Yom Kippur as the gates of repentance close. The ark often stays open; the congregation pleads intensely. Many have the custom to say Shema Yisrael together at the end. After Ne'ilah and nightfall (tzeit), one prays Maariv, makes Havdalah, and breaks the fast. It is the spiritual peak of the Ten Days of Teshuvah.
- Nefesh is the 'animal' part of the soul closest to physical life — appetite, hunger, physical desires, etc. Torah and mitzvot attempt to refine the nefesh toward holiness instead of animal instincts.
- Nerot
- Nerot — candles, often referring especially to Shabbat and Yom Tov lights
- Neshama is the soul — the divine breath that makes you alive. Modeh Ani thanks G-d for returning it after sleep. Yizkor and Kaddish relate to the soul's journey after death. Jewish thought distinguishes levels of soul (nefesh, ruach, neshama) in mysticism; practically, nurturing the soul means Torah, mitzvot, and character.
- Neshama yeteira ("extra soul") is the tradition that Shabbat brings an added measure of spiritual capacity — more patience, joy, and ability to connect to Torah. It departs at Havdalah, which is why some feel a post-Shabbat dip. Honoring Shabbat meals, rest, and learning helps this soul express itself; rushing and stress diminish it.
- Netilat Yadayim (Ritual morning hand washing)
- Netilat yadayim is ritual handwashing with a cup (keli). Morning negel vasser removes ruach ra'ah from sleep: pour water three times on each hand in alternating sequence (right, left, right, left, right, left). Before bread (a kezayit or more), pour consecutively on each entire hand up to the wrist (usually two or three pours on the right, then two or three on the left — not alternating), then recite Al Netilat Yadayim. After the bathroom, wash with water before Asher Yatzar — no bracha; alternating triple-pour is optional stringency, not basic halacha. After a cemetery, many wash three times alternately without a bracha. Do not confuse bread washing with negel vasser.
- Nevi'im
- Nevi'im (Prophets) is the middle section of Tanach — Joshua, Kings, Isaiah, Jeremiah, and the other prophetic books. They call Israel to justice and loyalty to Hashem. Reading them is Torah study.
- Nevi'im — Nevi'im (Prophets) is the middle section of Tanach — Joshua, Kings, Isaiah, Jeremiah, and the other prophetic books. They call Israel to justice and loyalty to Hashem. Reading them is Torah study.
